# External Functions Available to DataWeave

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

In addition to the built-in DataWeave functions, Mule Runtime and some modules, connectors, and Core
components provide functions that you can use in DataWeave scripts. The functions are specific to
the modules, connectors, and components that provide them.

## Mule Runtime Functions

These functions are injected by the Mule Runtime:

- `p`
- `lookup`
- `causedBy`

> [!NOTE] Starting in Mule 4.1.4, DataWeave incorporated the Mule Runtime functions into its Mule
> function module.
>
> MuleSoft recommends that you start using the `Mule` namespace when using these functions in Mule
> apps that are running on Mule Runtime 4.1.4 or later. To use them, you simply prepend the
> namespace to the function name, for example, `Mule::p('http:port')`, instead of `p('http:port')`.
>
> In addition to its continued support for the use of Mule Runtime functions in your DataWeave
> scripts and mappings, the Mule module also enables you to use Mule Runtime functions in your
> [custom DataWeave modules](dataweave-create-module).
>
> Mule apps running on Mule Runtime versions _prior_ to version 4.1.4 cannot use the `Mule`
> namespace or use Mule Runtime functions in custom modules, only in DataWeave scripts and mappings.

For DataWeave runtime properties, see the DataWeave functions [props](dw-runtime-functions-props)
and [prop](dw-runtime-functions-prop).

### Accessing Properties (p Function)

The `p` function provides access to properties, whether these are:

- Mule property placeholders
- System properties
- Environment properties

For more on configuring properties and how they are handled, see
[Configure Properties](../../mule-runtime/latest/configuring-properties).

The following example logs the value of the property `http.port`.

Example: Property Function Usage for Mule 4.1.4 and Later

```xml
<flow name="simple">
  <logger level="INFO" doc:name="Logger"
    message="#[Mule::p('http.port')]"/>
</flow>
```

Note that you can also use the function when defining a DataWeave variable or function. This example
uses the `p` function in the `myVar` definition. You can do the same thing with pre-4.1.4 runtimes
simply by omitting the `Mule::` namespace.

```xml
<flow name="simple">
  <logger level="INFO" doc:name="Logger"
    message="#[var myVar = Mule::p('http.port') --- myVar]"/>
</flow>
```

Example: Property Function Usage for Mule 4.1.3 and Earlier

```xml
<flow name="simple">
  <logger message="#[p('http.port')]"/>
</flow>
```

See also, the [p](dw-mule-functions-p) function introduced in the Mule function module in Mule 4.2.

### Execute a Flow (lookup Function)

Similar to the Flow Reference component, the `lookup` function enables you to execute another flow
within your app and to retrieve the resulting payload. It takes the flowâs name and an input payload
as parameters. For example, `lookup("anotherFlow", payload)` executes a flow named `anotherFlow`.

The function executes the specified flow using the current attributes, variables, and any error, but
it only passes in the payload without any attributes or variables. Similarly, the called flow will
only return its payload.

Note that `lookup` function does not support calling subflows.

#### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>flowName</code></p></td><td><p>A string that identifies the target flow.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>payload</code></p></td><td><p>The payload to send to the target flow, which can be any (<code>Any</code>) type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>timeoutMillis</code></p></td><td><p>Optional. Timeout (in milliseconds) for the execution of the target flow. Defaults to <code>2000</code> milliseconds (2 seconds) if the thread that is executing is CPU_LIGHT or CPU_INTENSIVE, or 1 minute when executing from other threads. If the lookup takes more time than the specified <code>timeoutMillis</code> value, an error is raised.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

This example shows XML for two flows. The `lookup` function in `flow1` executes `flow2` and passes
the object `{test:'hello '}` as its payload to `flow2`. The Set Payload component (`<set-payload/>`)
in `flow2` then concatenates the value of `{test:'hello '}` with the string `world` to output and
log `hello world`.

Example: Using the `lookup` Function in Mule 4.1.4 or Later

```XML
<flow name="flow1">
  <http:listener doc:name="Listener" config-ref="HTTP_Listener_config"
    path="/source"/>
  <ee:transform doc:name="Transform Message" >
    <ee:message >
      <ee:set-payload ><![CDATA[%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
Mule::lookup('flow2', {test:'hello '})]]></ee:set-payload>
    </ee:message>
  </ee:transform>
</flow>
<flow name="flow2" >
  <set-payload value='#[payload.test ++ "world"]' doc:name="Set Payload" />
  <logger level="INFO" doc:name="Logger" message='#[payload]'/>
</flow>
```

Example: Using the `lookup` Function in Mule 4.1.3 and Earlier

```XML
<flow name="flow1">
  <http:listener doc:name="Listener" config-ref="HTTP_Listener_config"
    path="/source"/>
  <ee:transform doc:name="Transform Message" >
    <ee:message >
      <ee:set-payload ><![CDATA[%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
lookup('flow2', {test:'hello '})]]></ee:set-payload>
    </ee:message>
  </ee:transform>
</flow>
<flow name="flow2" >
  <set-payload value='#[payload.test ++ "world"]' doc:name="Set Payload" />
  <logger level="INFO" doc:name="Logger" message='#[payload]'/>
</flow>
```

Output

```json
hello world
```

> [!WARNING] Always keep in mind that a functional language like DataWeave expects the invocation of
> the `lookup` function to _not_ have side effects. As such, the internal workings of the DataWeave
> engine might cause a `lookup` function to be invoked in parallel with other \`lookup\`s, or not
> invoked it at all.
>
> MuleSoft recommends that you invoke flows with the Flow Ref (`flow-ref`) component, using the
> `target` attribute to put the result of the flow in a `var` and then referencing that `var` from
> within the DataWeave script.

### Matching Errors by Types (causedBy Function)

The `causedBy` function matches an error by its type, like an error handler does. This is useful
when matching by a super type is required but specific sub-type logic is also needed or when
handling a `COMPOSITE_ROUTING` error that contains child errors of different types.

The error to match against can be implicit, but the type is always a required parameter.

In the following example, a global error handler is set up to handle `SECURITY` errors in a general
way, while specific actions are set up for `HTTP:UNAUTHORIZED` and `HTTP:FORBIDDEN` errors.

Example: Error Matcher Function Usage in Mule Runtime Version 4.1.4 and Later

```xml
<error-handler name="securityHandler">
  <on-error-continue type="SECURITY">
    <!-- general error handling for all SECURITY errors -->
    <choice>
      <when expression="#[error Mule::causedBy 'HTTP:UNAUTHORIZED']">
        <!-- specific error handling only for HTTP:UNAUTHORIZED errors -->
      </when>
      <when expression="#[Mule::causedBy('HTTP:FORBIDDEN')]">
        <!-- specific error handling only for HTTP:FORBIDDEN errors -->
      </when>
    </choice>
  </on-error-continue>
</error-handler>
```

Example: Error Matcher Function Usage in Mule Runtime Version 4.1.3 and Earlier

```xml
<error-handler name="securityHandler">
  <on-error-continue type="SECURITY">
    <!-- general error handling for all SECURITY errors -->
    <choice>
      <when expression="#[error causedBy 'HTTP:UNAUTHORIZED']">
        <!-- specific error handling only for HTTP:UNAUTHORIZED errors -->
      </when>
      <when expression="#[causedBy('HTTP:FORBIDDEN')]">
        <!-- specific error handling only for HTTP:FORBIDDEN errors -->
      </when>
    </choice>
  </on-error-continue>
</error-handler>
```

Notice that the error parameter is used both explicitly and implicitly.

## Connector and Component Functions

When using connectors and components in a Mule app, you can inject functions. Unlike the Runtime
functions, these functions require a namespace, which usually matches the component name.

For example, in an app using Batch you can use the following expression:
`#[Batch::isSuccessfulRecord()]`.

## See Also

[DataWeave Core Functions](dw-functions)

[DataWeave Types](dataweave-types)

[DataWeave Selectors](dataweave-selectors)
---

# Selecting Types

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Using the dot selector (`.`) over DataWeave [types](dataweave-type-system#composite-types) enables
you to declare new types from existing ones. Combined with the
[module loader](dataweave-type-system#reuse_types), DataWeave can also load and translate
declarations from a custom module file into DataWeave type directives that can be accessed in the
same way as types from any other DataWeave module.

## Declare a New Type from an Existing Type

The following example declares the `NameType` from the `User` type. Using the dot selector in
`User.name` to declare the `name` variable as `NameType` enables the `typeOf()` function to return
the primitive type, `"String"`. The DataWeave variable `name` is declared as a `NameType` value
"Seba":

DataWeave script:

```dataweave
%dw 2.0
type User = {name: String}
type NameType = User.name
var name: NameType = "Seba"
---
typeOf(name)
```

Output:

"String"

## Compose a Type from an Existing Typeâs Field

The following example uses the dot selector (`.`) to declare a new type `Address` from one key in
the complex type `User`:

DataWeave script:

```dataweave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
type User = {
 address : {country: String, city: String, street: String, number: Number},
 userName : String
}
type Address = User.address

var userAddress: Address = {country: "Argentina", city: "Rosario", street: "Calle Falsa", number: 123}
---
userAddress
```

Output:

```json
{
  "country": "Argentina",
  "city": "Rosario",
  "street": "Calle Falsa",
  "number": 123
}
```

## Select Types from Union Types

The following example shows how you can use type selection with `Union` types that consist of
`Object` types:

DataWeave script:

```dataweave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
type ID = {id: String, firstName: String, age: Number} | {id: Number, firstName: String, secondName: String}

var nameAge: ID = {firstName: "Seba", id: "123", age: 28}
var fullName: ID = {id: 38123, firstName: "Seba", secondName: "Elizalde"}

var nameString: ID.firstName = "Seba"
var idNumber: ID.id = 38123
---
[nameAge, fullName, nameString ++ " is of type " ++ typeOf(nameString), idNumber ++ " is of type " ++ typeOf(idNumber)]
```

Output:

```json
[
  {
    "firstName": "Seba",
    "id": "123",
    "age": 28
  },
  {
    "id": 38123,
    "firstName": "Seba",
    "secondName": "Elizalde"
  },
  "Seba is of type String",
  "38123 is of type Number"
]
```

The following script fails because the second type is not an `Object` type and there is no way to
reference it with the selector:

DataWeave script:

```dataweave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
type UnionType = {name: String} | Number
type Fail = UnionType.name
---
{}
```

Output:

```json
Cannot do selection on Type: Number

4| type Fail = UnionType.name
```

## Select Types from Namespaces

The following example shows type selection of a namespace:

DataWeave script:

```dataweave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
ns ns1 http://acme.com
type User = {
 ns1#name : String,
 name : Number,
}
var nameString: User.ns1#name = "Seba"
var nameNumber: User.name = 123
---
{
   "NameString" : typeOf(nameString),
   "NameNumber" : typeOf(nameNumber)
}
```

Output:

```json
{
  "NameString": "String",
  "NameNumber": "Number"
}
```

## Select Types with Type Parameters

This example shows how type selection works with type parameters, which are similar to generics in
other programming languages:

DataWeave script:

```dataweave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
type WithParameters<A, B> = {first: A, second: B, nestedObject: {message: A}}
---
{
 a: true is WithParameters<Boolean, Number>.first,
 b: 4592 is WithParameters<String, Number>.second,
 c: "sdf" is WithParameters<String, Number>.nestedObject.message,
}
```

Output:

```json
{
  "a": true,
  "b": true,
  "c": true
}
```

## Preserve Metadata of a Type

This example shows that type selection preserves metadata associated with the type:

DataWeave script:

```dataweave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
type User = {
 birthDate: Date {format: "dd-MMM-yy"},
 userName : String {schema: "value"}
}
type FormattedDate = User.birthDate
type UserName = User.userName
var formattedDate: FormattedDate = "10-SEP-15" as Date {format: "dd-MMM-yy"}
var otherFormatDate = "23-10-2022" as Date {format: "dd-MM-yyyy"}
var userName = "Messi" as String {schema: "value"}
var otherUserName = "Di MarÃ­a" as String {schema: "otherValue"}
---
{
formattedDate: formattedDate is FormattedDate,
userName: userName is UserName,
otherFormatDate: otherFormatDate is FormattedDate,
otherUserName: otherUserName is UserName
}
```

Output:

```json
{
  "formattedDate": true,
  "userName": true,
  "otherFormatDate": false,
  "otherUserName": false
}
```
---

# DataWeave Selectors

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

DataWeave selectors traverse the structures of objects and arrays and return matching values.

A selector always operates within a context, which can be a reference to a variable, an object
literal, an array literal, or the invocation of a DataWeave function.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Selector Type</th><th>Syntax</th><th>Return Type</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Single-value</p></td><td><p><code>.keyName</code></p></td><td><p>Any type of value that belongs to a matching key</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Multi-value</p></td><td><p><code>.*keyName</code></p></td><td><p>Array of values of any matching keys</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Descendants</p></td><td><p><code>..keyName</code></p></td><td><p>Array of values of any matching descendant keys</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Dynamic</p></td><td><p>See <a href="#dynamic_selector">Dynamic Selector</a>.</p></td><td><p>&nbsp;</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Key-value pair</p></td><td><p><code>.&amp;keyName</code></p></td><td><p>Object with the matching key</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Index</p></td><td><p><code>[&lt;index&gt;]</code></p></td><td><p>Value of any type at selected array index. Use negative numbers to index from the end of an array, for example, <code>-1</code> for the last element in the array. Use of numbers beyond the array size returns <code>null</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Range</p></td><td><p><code>[&lt;index&gt; to &lt;index&gt;]</code></p></td><td><p>Array with values from selected indexes</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>XML attribute</p></td><td><p><code>@</code>, <code>.@keyName</code></p></td><td><p>String value of the selected attribute</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Namespace</p></td><td><p><code>keyName.#</code></p></td><td><p>String value of the namespace for the selected key</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Key present</p></td><td><p><code>keyName?</code>, <code>keyName.@type?</code></p></td><td><p>Boolean (<code>true</code> if the selected key of an object or XML attribute is present, <code>false</code> if not)</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Assert present</p></td><td><p><code>keyName!</code></p></td><td><p>String: Exception message if the key is not present</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Filter</p></td><td><p><code>[?(boolean_expression)]</code></p></td><td><p>Array or object containing key-value pairs <em>if</em> the DataWeave expression returns <code>true</code>. Otherwise, returns the value <code>null</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Metadata</p></td><td><p><code>.^someMetadata</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Returns the value of specified metadata for a Mule payload, variable, or attribute. The selector can return the value of class (<code>.^class</code>), content length (<code>.^contentLength</code>), encoding (<code>.^encoding</code>), mime type (<code>.^mimeType</code>), media type (<code>.^mediaType</code>), raw (<code>.^raw</code>), and custom (<code>.^myCustomMetadata</code>) metadata. For details, see <a href="dataweave-cookbook-extract-data#selector_metadata">Extract Data</a>.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

> [!NOTE] The following examples use these selectors. For additional examples of selectors, see
> [Extract Data](dataweave-cookbook-extract-data).

## Rules for Matching

In DataWeave, a name that matches the key of an object (such as the key `"someName"` in the JSON
object `{ "someName" : "Data Weave" }`) can select data from that object. For a simple example, see
[Single-Value Selector](#single_value_selector).

## Single-Value Selector

This selector matches the value of the first key-value pair in which the key matches the given
selector name. The selector can be applied to an Object or to an array. On an array, the selector
applies to all Object values inside the array and ignores all values that are not Object values. If
no key matches, the value `null` is returned.

> [!NOTE] For information about the dot selector (`.`) and how to use it on DataWeave types, refer
> to [Selecting Types](dataweave-selecting-types).

### Example: Using a Single-Value Selector on an Object

This example shows how the selector works on a simple JSON object.

#### Input Payload

The input payload is an object with the key `"name"` and the value `"DataWeave"`:

```JSON
{ "name": "Data Weave" }
```

#### DataWeave Source

The DataWeave script uses `payload.name` to select the value of the object that has the key
`"name"`. Note that `payload.name` and `payload."name"` are both valid ways to perform the
selection.

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
payload.name
```

#### Output

The script outputs the String value of the input object, "Data Weave":

```JSON
"Data Weave"
```

### Example: Using a Single-Value Selector on an Array

On an array, the selector applies to every element.

#### Input Payload

The input payload is an array that contains two objects with the same key, "name":

```JSON
[
  {
    "name": "Arg"
  },
  {
    "name": "Japan"
  }
]
```

#### DataWeave Source

The DataWeave script uses `payload.name` to select the value of any objects in input payload that
have the key "name":

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
payload.name
```

#### Output

The script outputs an array that contains the values of the input objects with the key `"name"`:
`"Arg"` and `"Japan"`:

```json
["Arg", "Japan"]
```

## Namespace Selector

The DataWeave namespace selector supports the use of a name that matches a namespace or a local
part. To match a namespace, the name must match the namespace and the local part. If the name only
matches the local part, the selector matches all namespaces with that local part, regardless of the
namespace.

Note that XML namespaces and their prefixes are defined in the `xmlns` attribute of an XML element.
For example, the element `<h:table xmlns:h="http://www.w3.org/TR/html4/"/>` defines a namespace that
assigns the prefix `h` to the namespace
`[http://www.w3.org/TR/html4/](http://www.w3.org/TR/html4/)`. All elements in that namespace contain
the `h` prefix. For example, in the element `<h:table/>`, `h` is the prefix for the namespace, and
`table` is the local part.

### Example: Using a Namespace Selector

This example selects values from XML elements using the namespace and local part.

#### Input Payload

The input payload contains XML elements, `h:table` and `f:table`, that have different namespaces but
the same local names (`table`):

```xml
<root>

  <h:table xmlns:h="http://www.w3.org/TR/html4/">
    <h:tr>
      <h:td>Apples</h:td>
      <h:td>Bananas</h:td>
    </h:tr>
  </h:table>

  <f:table xmlns:f="https://www.w3schools.com/furniture">
    <f:tr>
      <f:name>African Coffee Table</f:name>
      <f:width>80</f:width>
      <f:length>120</f:length>
    </f:tr>
  </f:table>

</root>
```

#### DataWeave Source

The DataWeave script selects XML content from specified namespaces. The scriptâs header creates
namespace (`ns`) variables `html` and `furniture` to store namespaces from the input payload. To
select the children of `h:table` into element `a` and the children of `f:table` elements into
element `b`, the script uses the `html` and `furniture` namespace variables with a namespace
selector (`#`) and local part (`table`):

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/xml
ns html http://www.w3.org/TR/html4/
ns furniture https://www.w3schools.com/furniture
---
root: {
     a: payload.root.html#table,
     b: payload.root.furniture#table
 }
```

#### Output

The script outputs children of the `h:table` element under element `a` and the children of the
`f:table` under element `b`:

```xml
<?xml version='1.0' encoding='UTF-8'?>
<root>
  <a>
    <h:tr xmlns:h="http://www.w3.org/TR/html4/">
      <h:td>Apples</h:td>
      <h:td>Bananas</h:td>
    </h:tr>
  </a>
  <b>
    <f:tr xmlns:f="https://www.w3schools.com/furniture">
      <f:name>African Coffee Table</f:name>
      <f:width>80</f:width>
      <f:length>120</f:length>
    </f:tr>
  </b>
</root>
```

### Example: Using a Local Name to Select XML Values

This example selects XML values using a local name `table` from the `h:table` element.

#### Input Payload

The input payload contains XML with a `table` element that contains the namespace
`"http://www.w3.org/TR/html4/"`:

```xml
<root>
  <h:table xmlns:h="http://www.w3.org/TR/html4/">
    <h:tr>
      <h:td>Apples</h:td>
      <h:td>Bananas</h:td>
    </h:tr>
  </h:table>
</root>
```

#### DataWeave Source

The DataWeave script selects values of children in the `table` element by using a local name of the
XML element `table` instead of its namespace:

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/xml
---
root: { a: payload.root.table }
```

#### Output

The script outputs XML that contains the values of children in the `table` element:

```xml
<?xml version='1.0' encoding='UTF-8'?>
<root>
  <a>
    <h:tr xmlns:h="http://www.w3.org/TR/html4/">
      <h:td>Apples</h:td>
      <h:td>Bananas</h:td>
    </h:tr>
  </a>
</root>
```

## Attribute Selector

The attribute selector returns the first attribute value that matches the selected name expression.
If no key matches, the selection returns the value `null`.

The following example selects the value of an XML attribute.

### Input Payload

The input payload is an XML `user` element that contains the attribute `name="Weave"`:

```xml
<user name="Weave"/>
```

### DataWeave Source

The DataWeave script selects the value of the `name` attribute from the input payload
(`<user name="Weave"/>`). Notice that the `@` indicates an attribute selection.

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
payload.user.@name
```

### Output

The DataWeave script outputs the value of `name` attribute.

```json
"Weave"
```

## Multi-Value Selector

Instead of returning the value of the first matching key in an array of objects, the multi-value
selector (`*`) returns an array containing values to the matching key (for example, `*user` where
`user` is the key). The selector does not return the values of descendants, only those at the
specified level. If no key matches, the selection returns the value `null`.

### Input Payload

The input payload contains an XML array of `user` elements.

```xml
<users>
  <user>Weave</user>
  <user>BAT</user>
  <user>TF</user>
</users>
```

### DataWeave Source

The DataWeave script uses `*user` to select the values of all `user` elements from the input
payload.

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
payload.users.*user
```

### Output

The script outputs an array of `user` values.

```json
["Weave", "BAT", "TF"]
```

## Descendant Selector

The descendant selector returns a list of all children and their descendants. You can directly chain
this selector to any other selector without using a single `.`. For example, `payload..` recursively
returns an array of all the child values, the values of _their_ children, and so on. You can also
chain the selector to another element (for example, with `payload..user`) to select the values of
each `user` key and its descendants, or you can use `payload..*name` to select the values of all
`name` descendants.

### Example: Selecting Each Descendant

This example selects each descendant of the input payload.

#### Input Payload

The input payload contains a set of elements that are nested at different levels.

```xml
<users>
  <user>
    <name>Weave</name>
    <user>
      <name>BAT</name>
      <user>
        <name>BDD</name>
      </user>
    </user>
  </user>
</users>
```

#### DataWeave Source

The DataWeave script uses `..` to recursively select all elements in the input payload and return
them in a JSON array.

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
payload..
```

#### Output

```json
[
  {
    "user": {
      "name": "Weave",
      "user": {
        "name": "BAT",
        "user": {
          "name": "BDD"
        }
      }
    }
  },
  {
    "name": "Weave",
    "user": {
      "name": "BAT",
      "user": {
        "name": "BDD"
      }
    }
  },
  "Weave",
  {
    "name": "BAT",
    "user": {
      "name": "BDD"
    }
  },
  "BAT",
  {
    "name": "BDD"
  },
  "BDD"
]
```

### Example: Selecting Descending Values

This example selects the descending `name` values.

#### Input Payload

The input payload contains a set of `name` elements that are nested at different levels.

```xml
<users>
  <user>
    <name>Weave</name>
    <user>
      <name>BAT</name>
      <name>Munit</name>
      <user>
        <name>BDD</name>
      </user>
    </user>
  </user>
</users>
```

#### DataWeave Source

The DataWeave script uses `..` to select the values of all first `name` elements from the input
payload and output those values into a JSON array.

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
payload..name
```

#### Output

The script outputs a JSON array with all `name` values.

```json
["Weave", "BAT", "BDD"]
```

### Example

This example selects the descending `name` values.

#### Input Payload

The input payload contains a set of `name` elements that are nested at different levels.

```xml
<users>
  <user>
    <name>Weave</name>
    <user>
      <name>BAT</name>
      <name>Munit</name>
      <user>
        <name>BDD</name>
      </user>
    </user>
  </user>
</users>
```

#### DataWeave Source

The DataWeave script uses `..*` to select the values of all `name` elements from the input payload
and output those values into a JSON array, including the `BAT` and `Munit` values of the repeated
`name` keys that are at the same level in the XML hierarchy.

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
payload..*name
```

#### Output

The script outputs a JSON array with all `name` values.

```json
["Weave", "BAT", "Munit", "BDD"]
```

### Example: Selecting Descending Values

This example selects the descending `Name` values of the `Item` elements.

#### Input Payload

The input payload contains a set of `Name` elements that are nested at different levels.

```xml
<Example>
    <Brand>
        <Id>32345</Id>
        <logo>circle</logo>
        <Item>
            <Name>Perfume</Name>
            <Item>
                <Name>Bosque</Name>
            </Item>
        </Item>
        <Item>
            <Name>t-Shirt</Name>
        </Item>
    </Brand>
    <Brand>
        <Id>435678C</Id>
        <logo>circle</logo>
        <Item>
            <Name>t-Shirt2</Name>
            <Item>
                <Name>t-Shirt red</Name>
                <Item>
                    <Name>t-Shirt red with logo</Name>
                </Item>
            </Item>
        </Item>
    </Brand>
</Example>
```

#### DataWeave Source

The DataWeave script uses `..*` to select the values of all `Name` elements nested in the `Item`
elements from the input payload and output those values into a XML array.

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/xml
---
{
    Supermarket: {
        Item: {
            Value: payload..*Item.Name
        }
    }
}
```

#### Output

The script outputs a XML array with all `name` values.

```xml
<?xml version='1.0' encoding='UTF-8'?>
<Supermarket>
  <Item>
    <Value>Perfume</Value>
    <Value>t-Shirt</Value>
    <Value>Bosque</Value>
    <Value>t-Shirt2</Value>
    <Value>t-Shirt red</Value>
    <Value>t-Shirt red with logo</Value>
  </Item>
</Supermarket>
```

## Dynamic Selector

The syntax for dynamic selection depends on what you are selecting:

- Single Value: `payload[(nameExpression)]`
- Multi Value: `payload[*(nameExpression)]`
- Attribute: `payload[@(nameExpression)]`
- Key Value: `payload[&(nameExpression)]`
- Single Value with a Namespace: `payload.ns0#"$(nameExpression)"`

### Example: Dynamically Selecting a Single Value

This example shows how to dynamically select a single value.

#### Input Payload

The input payload is an array of objects. The first object has the key `"ref"` and the value
`"name"`. The second has the key `"name"` and the value `"Data Weave"`:

```JSON
{ "ref": "name", "name": "Data Weave" }
```

#### DataWeave Source

The DataWeave script dynamically selects the value of the `"name"` key:

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
payload[(payload.ref)]
```

Notice that it passes `payload.ref` within the parentheses of dynamic selector `[()]`. The script
works because the value of `"ref"` is `"name"`, which matches the key `"name"`.

#### Output

The script outputs the value of the object that has the `"name"` key:

```JSON
"Data Weave"
```

### Example: Dynamically Selecting a Single Value with a Namespace

This example shows how to dynamically select a single value that contains a namespace.

#### Input Payload

The input payload contains a `<root/>` element with two child elements, one of which has the
namespace `http://www.w3.org/TR/html4/`. Both child elements (`<f:table/>` and `<h:table>`) have the
local name `table`:

```xml
<root ref="table">
    <f:table xmlns:f="https://www.w3schools.com/furniture">Manzana</f:table>
    <h:table xmlns:h="http://www.w3.org/TR/html4/">Banana</h:table>
</root>
```

#### DataWeave Source

The DataWeave script dynamically selects the value of the element that has the namespace
`http://www.w3.org/TR/html4/`:

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
ns h http://www.w3.org/TR/html4/
---
payload.root.h#"$(payload.root.@ref)"
```

Notice that the expression `payload.root.@ref` uses the attribute selector (`@`) on the `ref`
attribute of the `root` element to select the value `table`, which matches local name `table` in the
element `<h:table xmlns:h="http://www.w3.org/TR/html4/">Banana</h:table>`.

#### Output

The script outputs the value of the element that has the namespace `http://www.w3.org/TR/html4/`:

```JSON
"Banana"
```

## Use of Selectors on Content Stored in Variables

All selectors work with the [predefined Mule Runtime variables](dataweave-variables-context) and
with [DataWeave variables](dataweave-variables).

To select [Mule event](../../mule-runtime/latest/about-mule-event) data, such as the payload, an
attribute, or variable data within the Mule event, use predefined Mule Runtime variables, such as
`payload`, `attributes`, and `vars`.

Extracted values are handled as literal values of one of the supported DataWeave value types.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Data to extract</th><th>Syntax</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Payload</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p><code>payload</code>, for example: <code>payload.name</code></p></div><div><p>If the <code>payload</code> is <code>{"name" : "somebody"}</code>, <code>payload.name</code> returns <code>"somebody"</code>.</p></div><div><p>For more on the Mule payload, see <a href="../../mule-runtime/latest/about-mule-message#_message_payload">Message Payload</a>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Attribute</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p><code>attributes.&lt;myAttributeName&gt;</code></p></div><div><p>For examples, see <a href="../../mule-runtime/latest/about-mule-message#_attributes">Attributes</a>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Variable</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p><code>&lt;myVariableName&gt;</code></p></div><div><p>To avoid name collisions, you must prepend <code>vars</code> to the variable name:</p></div><div><p><code>vars.&lt;myVariableName&gt;</code></p></div><div><p>For more on Mule variables, see <a href="../../mule-runtime/latest/about-mule-variables">Variables in Mule Apps</a>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Error object</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p><code>error</code></p></div><div><p>For information on errors in the flow, you can use <code>#[error.cause]</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Flow</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p><code>flow</code></p></div><div><p>For the flow name in the Logger: <code>#[flow.name]</code></p></div><div><p>Note that <code>flow.name</code> does not work in some Core components, such as Set Payload and Transform Message.</p></div><div><p>For more on flows, see <a href="../../mule-runtime/latest/about-flows">Flows and Subflows</a>.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# Streaming in DataWeave

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

DataWeave supports end-to-end streaming through a flow in a Mule application. Streaming speeds the
processing of large documents without overloading memory.

DataWeave processes streamed data as its bytes arrive instead of scanning the entire document to
index it. When in deferred mode, DataWeave can also pass streamed output data directly to a message
processor without saving it to the disk. This behavior enables DataWeave and Mule to process data
faster and consume fewer resources than the default processes for reading and writing data.

To stream successfully, it is important to understand the following:

- The basic unit of the stream is specific to the data format.  
  The unit is a record in a CSV document, an element of an array in a JSON document, or a collection
  in an XML document.
- Streaming accesses each unit of the stream sequentially.  
  Streaming doesnât support random access to a document.

## Enabling Streaming

Streaming isnât enabled by default. You can use two configuration properties to stream data in a
supported data format:

- `streaming` property, for reading source data as a stream
- `deferred` writer property, for passing an output stream directly to the next message processor in
  a flow

For DataWeave to read source data as a stream, you must set the `streaming` reader property to
`true` on the data source. Within a Mule application, you append this setting to the value of the
**MIME Type** property `outputMimeType` or `mimeType`. You can set the property in any connector
operation or Mule component that generates data, such as an HTTP Listener operation, HTTP Request
operation, On New or Updated File operation, or a Set Payload component. DataWeave reads data as
streamed data from the point in the application where you set the streaming property through all
downstream components and connector operations that contain DataWeave expressions and scripts.

```XML
<flow name="dw-streaming-example" >
  <http:listener doc:name="Listener"
      outputMimeType="application/json; streaming=true"
      config-ref="HTTP_Listener_config" path="/input"/>
</flow>
```

Notice that `streaming=true` is part of the `outputMimeType` value. Many other Mule components, such
as the File and FTP components, also support the MIME Type setting.

To pass the streamed output to the next message processor, you can use the `output` directive in a
DataWeave script, for example:

```XML
output application/json deferred=true
```

For more detail on use of the `deferred` property. see [Streaming Output](#stream_output).

## Streaming CSV

CSV is simplest format for streaming because of its structure. Each row below the CSV header is a
streamable record. The following CSV example consists of records that contain `name`, `lastName`,
and `age` values: â

```CSV
name,lastName,age
mariano,achaval,37
leandro,shokida,30
pedro,achaval,4
christian,chibana,25
sara,achaval,2
matias,achaval,8
```

â To stream this CSV example, the following script selects values from each record. It uses the
`map` function to iterate over each record in the document. â

```DataWeave
payload map (record) ->
    {
        FullName: record.lastName ++ "," ++ record.name,
        Age: record.age
    }
```

Although streaming doesnât support random access to the entire document, a DataWeave script can
access data randomly _within each record_ because each record is loaded into memory. For example,
the expression `record.lastName "," record.name,` can access a `lastName` value before it accesses a
`name` value even though the order of values is reversed in the input.

However, streaming doesnât work in the following script. The script requires random access to the
entire document to return the elements in a different order than they are given in the input. â

```DataWeave
[payload[-2], payload[-1], payload[3]]
```

â

## Streaming JSON

â The unit of a JSON stream is each element in an array.

Note that DataWeave 2.2.0 support for JSON streaming for Mule 4.2 was limited by the requirement
that the root be an array. DataWeave support in Mule 4.3 includes streaming to arrays that arenât at
the root of the input.

```JSON
{
    "name" : "Mariano",
    "lastName": "Achaval",
    "family": [
        {"name": "Sara", "age": 2},
        {"name": "Pedro", "age": 4},
        {"name": "Matias", "age": 8}
    ],
    "age": 37
}
```

â In this example, DataWeave can stream `payload.family` and perform random access within each
element of that array. However, DataWeave cannot randomly access the container object. For example,
it isnât possible to stream `{ a: payload.age , b: payload.family}` because `age` follows `family`,
and DataWeave cannot go backwards. â

## Streaming XML

â XML is more complicated than JSON because there are no arrays in the document.

To enable XML streaming, DataWeave provides the following reader property to define the location in
the document to stream:

- `collectionPath`

For example, assume the following XML input: â

```XML
<order>
    <header>
        <date>Wed Nov 15 13:45:28 EST 2006</date>
        <customer number="123123">Joe</customer>
    </header>
    <order-items>
        <order-item id="31">
            <product>111</product>
            <quantity>2</quantity>
            <price>8.90</price>
        </order-item>
        <order-item id="31">
            <product>222</product>
            <quantity>7</quantity>
            <price>5.20</price>
        </order-item>
        <order-item id="31">
            <product>111</product>
            <quantity>2</quantity>
            <price>8.90</price>
        </order-item>
        <order-item id="31">
            <product>222</product>
            <quantity>7</quantity>
            <price>5.20</price>
        </order-item>
        <order-item id="31">
            <product>222</product>
            <quantity>7</quantity>
            <price>5.20</price>
        </order-item>
    </order-items>
</order>
```

â Given this XML source data, you can set the unit of the stream to `<order-item/>` by setting
`collectionPath=order.order-items` in the `outputMimeType` value:

```XML
<flow name="dw-streaming-example" >
  <http:listener doc:name="Listener"
      outputMimeType="application/xml; collectionpath=order.order-items; streaming=true"
      config-ref="HTTP_Listener_config" path="/input"/>
</flow>
```

Note that you need to set both `streaming=true` and the `collectionPath` value. If either is
missing, DataWeave doesnât stream the content. The following DataWeave script streams the XML input
using each `<order-items/>` element as the streamable unit.

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/xml
---
{
  salesorder: {
    itemList: payload.order."order-items".*"order-item" map {
      ("i_" ++ $$) : {
        id: $.@id,
        productId: $.product,
        quantity: $.quantity,
        price: $.price
      }
    }
  }
}
```

The script produces the following XML output:

```XML
<?xml version='1.0' encoding='UTF-8'?>
<salesorder>
  <itemList>
    <i_0>
      <id>31</id>
      <quantity>2</quantity>
      <productId>111</productId>
      <price>8.90</price>
    </i_0>
  </itemList>
  <itemList>
    <i_1>
      <id>31</id>
      <quantity>7</quantity>
      <productId>222</productId>
      <price>5.20</price>
    </i_1>
  </itemList>
  <itemList>
    <i_2>
      <id>31</id>
      <quantity>2</quantity>
      <productId>111</productId>
      <price>8.90</price>
    </i_2>
  </itemList>
  <itemList>
    <i_3>
      <id>31</id>
      <quantity>7</quantity>
      <productId>222</productId>
      <price>5.20</price>
    </i_3>
  </itemList>
  <itemList>
    <i_4>
      <id>31</id>
      <quantity>7</quantity>
      <productId>222</productId>
      <price>5.20</price>
    </i_4>
  </itemList>
</salesorder>
```

## Validate a Script for Streamed Data (Experimental Feature)

To check that your code can process an input stream successfully, DataWeave provides the following
_advanced, experimental_ annotation and a related directive:

- `@StreamCapable()`  
  Use this annotation to validate whether the script can sequentially access a variable (typically
  the `payload` variable).
- `input` directive:  
  The `@StreamCapable()` annotation requires the use of an input directive in the DataWeave script
  that identifies the MIME type of the data source, for example, `input payload application/xml`.

The DataWeave validator (which is triggered by the `@StreamCapable` annotation in the script) checks
a script against the following criteria:

- The variable is referenced only once.
- No index selector is set for negative access, such as `[-1]`.
- No reference to the variable is found in a nested lambda.

If all criteria are met, the selected data is streamable.

The following example validates successfully. The script is designed to act on the
[JSON input](#example_json) from the [JSON streaming](#stream_json) section:

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0

@StreamCapable()
input payload application/json
output application/json
---
payload.family filter (member) -> member.age > 3
```

The script successfully validates and returns the following output:

```JSON
[
  {
    "name": "Pedro",
    "age": 4
  },
  {
    "name": "Matias",
    "age": 8
  }
]
```

### Validation Failures

If any of the criteria that the validator checks is false, the validation fails.

Before proceeding, note that validation can fail in some cases when streaming works. If you write a
script in a way that sequentially accesses the input variable in a given data source, streaming
works, but that script might not work in all cases. For example, JSON doesnât place a restriction on
the order of the keys in an object. If the keys in some JSON documents arrive in a different order
than the script expects, streaming will fail in those cases. The annotation processor follows the
rules of the format and cannot assume that the keys always arrive in the same order.

#### Error: Variable Is Referenced More Than Once

Validation fails if a script attempts to reference the same variable more than once.

The following script is designed to act on the [JSON input](#example_json) from the
[JSON streaming](#stream_json) section. Validation fails because the script attempts to reference
the `payload` variable more than once:

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0

@StreamCapable()
input payload application/json
output application/json
---
 {
     family: payload.family filter (member) -> member.age > 3,
     name: payload.name
 }
```

The script fails with the following error:

```error
4| input payload application/json streaming=true
         ^^^^^^^
Parameter `payload` is not stream capable.
Reasons:
 - Variable payload is referenced more than once. Locations:
 ---------------------------

8|      family: payload.family filter (member) -> member.age > 3,
                ^^^^^^^
 ---------------------------

9|      name: payload.name
              ^^^^^^^ at
4| input payload application/json streaming=true
```

#### Error: Wrong Scope Reference

Validation fails if a script attempts to reference a variable from a scope that is different from
the scope in which the variable is defined.

The following script fails because the `payload` variable is referenced from within the lambda
expression `[1,2,3] map ((item, index) â payload)`. Even if the expression is
`[1] map ((item, index) â payload`, streaming fails because `payload` is in the wrong scope.

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0

@StreamCapable()
input payload application/json
output application/json
---
[1,2,3] map ((item, index) -> payload)
```

The example fails with the following error:

```error
4| input payload application/json
         ^^^^^^^
Parameter `payload` is not stream capable.
Reasons:
 - Variable payload is referenced in a different scope from where it was defined. Locations:
 ---------------------------

9| [1,2,3] map ((item, index) -> payload)
                ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ at
4| input payload application/json
```

## Streaming Output

After processing streamed data, you can stream the output directly to another message processor. To
facilitate this behavior, use the `deferred` writer property in the output directive of the
DataWeave script, for example, `output application/json deferred=true`.

NOTE

Exceptions arenât handled when you set `deferred = true`. For example, you can see this behavior in
Studio when a flow throws an exception. If you are running an application in Studio debug mode and
an exception occurs in a Transform Message component when `deferred = true`, the console logs the
error, but the flow doesnât stop at the Transform Message component.

Building on the example in [JSON Streaming](#stream_json), the following flow uses a DataWeave
script to filter streamed input and then streams the output directly to a Write operation:

```XML
<flow name="dw-streamingexample">
  <file:listener doc:name="On New or Updated File"
    config-ref="File_Config" directory="/Users/me/testing/json" recursive="false" outputMimeType="application/json;
    streaming=true">
    <scheduling-strategy>
      <fixed-frequency timeUnit="SECONDS" />
    </scheduling-strategy>
    <file:matcher />
  </file:listener>
  <ee:transform doc:name="Transform Message">
    <ee:message>
      <ee:set-payload><![CDATA[%dw 2.0

@StreamCapable()
input payload application/json
output application/json deferred = true
---
{
   family: payload.family filter (member) -> member.age > 1
}]]></ee:set-payload>
    </ee:message>
  </ee:transform>
  <file:write doc:name="Write"
      config-ref="File_Config2"
      path="/Users/me/testing/output.json"/>
</flow>
```

The flow provides the following configuration:

1.  The listener (`<file:listener>`) uses `streaming=true` to stream the incoming JSON data.
2.  The DataWeave script in `<ee:transform/>` filters records in the streamed data and uses the
    `deferred = true` property to stream the resulting records directly to the next processor in the
    flow.
3.  The next component in the flow, `<file:write/>`, receives the filtered stream directly and
    writes the records to a file.

## See Also

- [CSV Format](dataweave-formats-csv)
- [JSON Format](dataweave-formats-json)
- [XML Format](dataweave-formats-xml)
- [Supported DataWeave Formats](dataweave-formats)
---

# DataWeave System Properties

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

DataWeave supports several system properties. To use these properties in application development and
deployments, see [System Properties](../../mule-runtime/latest/mule-app-properties-system).

Whether a property in this reference applies depends on the DataWeave language level configured for
the application, not only on the Mule runtime version where the application runs. For how language
level is determined in Mule applications (including its relationship to `minMuleVersion`), see
[Versioning Behavior in DataWeave](dataweave-versioning-behavior).

If a property is listed as available only for specific language levels, it applies only when the
application uses one of those levels. In newer DataWeave versions, some of these properties remain
supported only for backward compatibility when the application targets a compatible earlier language
level, even if it runs on a newer Mule runtime.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Property</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.base64ChunksEnabled</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>When set to <code>true</code>, <code>dw::core::Binaries::fromBase64</code> and <code>dw::core::Binaries::toBase64</code> functions donât load the entire function argument (<code>String</code> or <code>Binary</code>) into memory. DataWeave loads the function argument (<code>String</code> or <code>Binary</code>) by chunk. This feature prevents an out-of-memory error.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default:</p><div><ul><li><p><code>true</code> for language levels: 2.11, 2.12</p></li></ul></div></li></ul></div><div><p>Available for language levels: 2.11, 2.12</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.buffered_char_sequence.enabled</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Controls whether an internal <code>BufferedCharSequence</code> improvement is enabled or disabled. When this property is set to <code>true</code>, DataWeave does not load large fields (greater than 1.5M) into memory. DataWeave reads these fields by chunk. This feature reduces performance to prevent an out-of-memory error.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default: <code>true</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.buffersize</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Sets the size (in bytes) of in-memory input and output buffers that DataWeave uses to retain processed inputs and outputs. DataWeave stores payloads that exceed this size in temporary files <code>dw-buffer-index-${count}.tmp</code> and <code>dw-buffer-output-${count}.tmp</code>. See <a href="dataweave-memory-management">DataWeave Memory Management</a>.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Number</code></p></li><li><p>Default: <code>8192</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.charbuffersize</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Determines the size (in bytes) of the buffer used by the writer.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Number</code> (in bytes)</p></li><li><p>Default: <code>4096</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.coercionexception.verbose</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>By default, adds to coercion exceptions more information about data that fails to coerce. When this property is set to <code>false</code>, DataWeave does not display the additional metadata.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default: <code>true</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.cpulimit.watchdog</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Monitors and limits script execution time when set to <code>true</code>.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default: <code>true</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.csv_reader.indexed.retainStringValueInMemory</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>When set to <code>false</code>, this property prevents DataWeave indexed readers for CSV from keeping string value references in memory. While this can reduce performance, it helps prevent out-of-memory errors.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default:</p><div><ul><li><p><code>true</code> for language levels: 2.6, 2.7, 2.8, 2.9</p></li><li><p><code>false</code> for language levels: 2.10, 2.11, 2.12</p></li></ul></div></li></ul></div><div><p>Available for language levels: 2.6, 2.7, 2.8, 2.9, 2.10, 2.11, 2.12</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.csv_writer.alwaysEscapeQuotesInCSV</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>When set to <code>true</code>, DataWeave escapes quotes in CSV.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default:</p><div><ul><li><p><code>false</code> for language levels: 2.4</p></li><li><p><code>true</code> for language levels: 2.5</p></li></ul></div></li></ul></div><div><p>Available for language levels: 2.4, 2.5</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.date_minus_back_compatibility</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Restores the <a href="dataweave-cookbook-add-and-subtract-time">Add and Subtract Time</a> behaviors that changed in 2.3.0 when this property is set to <code>true</code>.</p></div><div><p>For example, consider the following DataWeave script:</p></div><div><div><pre><code data-lang="dataweave">%dw 2.0
output application/dw
---
|2019-10-01| - |2018-09-23|</code></pre></div></div><div><ul><li><p>In DataWeave 2.3.0, the expression returns <code>PT8952H</code>.</p></li><li><p>In DataWeave 2.2.0, the expression returns <code>P1Y8D</code>.</p></li><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default:</p><div><ul><li><p><code>false</code> for language levels: 2.3, 2.4, 2.5</p></li></ul></div></li></ul></div><div><p>Available for language levels: 2.3, 2.4, 2.5</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.decode_binaries_with_writer_encoding</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>When set to <code>true</code>, binary values in JSON are written using JsonWriter encoding. Otherwise, the property uses either the encoding defined in the value schema or the default one.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default: <code>false</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.defaultOperator.disableExceptionHandling</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>When set to <code>true</code>, DataWeave disables the <code>default</code> operator that catches runtime exceptions. Enabling the <code>default</code> operator doesnât return the <code>default value</code> when a runtime exception occurs. When set to <code>false</code>, the <code>default</code> operator catches the runtime exceptions and returns the <code>default value</code>.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default:</p><div><ul><li><p><code>true</code> for language levels: 2.5, 2.6, 2.7, 2.8, 2.9</p></li></ul></div></li></ul></div><div><p>Available for language levels: 2.5, 2.6, 2.7, 2.8, 2.9</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.directbuffer.disable</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Controls whether DataWeave uses off-heap memory (the default) or heap memory. DataWeave uses off-heap memory for internal buffering, which can cause issues on systems that have only a small amount of memory. See <a href="dataweave-memory-management">DataWeave Memory Management</a>.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default: <code>false</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.dump_files</code></p></td><td><div><div><p><em>Experimental:</em> Dumps the input context and the failing script into a folder when this property is set to <code>true</code>. This behavior enables you to track the failing script and the data that makes the script fail, which is particularly useful for verifying that the received input data is valid. Incorrect scripts often fail when an upstream component generates invalid data.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default: <code>false</code></p></li></ul></div><div><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.dump_files</code> is an <em>experimental feature</em> that is subject to change or removal from future versions of DataWeave. If set to <code>false</code>, the JavaModuleLoader uses the getter and setter methods to access existing properties or the property uses the field accessor as default.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default:</p><div><ul><li><p><code>false</code> for language levels: 2.8</p></li><li><p><code>true</code> for language levels: 2.9, 2.10, 2.11, 2.12</p></li></ul></div></li></ul></div><div><p>Available for language levels: 2.8, 2.9, 2.10, 2.11, 2.12</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.java.output_types_as_string</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>When set to <code>true</code>, DataWeave scripts with Java output <code>mimetype</code> return the string representation of that type. If set to <code>false</code>, the scripts return a Java class constructed from the corresponding DataWeave type class.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default:</p><div><ul><li><p><code>true</code> for language levels: 2.0, 2.1, 2.2, 2.3, 2.4, 2.5, 2.6, 2.7, 2.8, 2.9, 2.10, 2.11, 2.12</p></li></ul></div></li></ul></div><div><p>Available for language levels: 2.0, 2.1, 2.2, 2.3, 2.4, 2.5, 2.6, 2.7, 2.8, 2.9, 2.10, 2.11, 2.12</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.java.per_app_java_bean_cache</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>When set to <code>true</code>, the <code>JavaBeanHelper</code> uses a per-app cache owned by <code>WeaveExpressionLanguage</code> instead of the global singleton. This eliminates the weak-value eviction events that can orphan Caffeineâs <code>evictionLock</code> under stack exhaustion.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default:</p><div><ul><li><p><code>true</code> for language levels: 2.12</p></li></ul></div></li></ul></div><div><p>Available for language levels: 2.12</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.java.stacktrace</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the depth of the Java stack trace.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Number</code></p></li><li><p>Default: <code>6</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.javaSqlDateToDate</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>When set, <code>java.sql.Date</code> maps to the DataWeave <code>Date</code> type. If <code>false</code>, it maps to <code>DateTime</code>.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default:</p><div><ul><li><p><code>false</code> for language levels: 2.4</p></li><li><p><code>true</code> for language levels: 2.5</p></li></ul></div></li></ul></div><div><p>Available for language levels: 2.4, 2.5</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.legacySizeOfNumber</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>When set to <code>true</code>, DataWeave returns <code>1</code> when calling <code>sizeOf()</code> function for any <code>Number</code> value.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default:</p><div><ul><li><p><code>false</code> for language levels: 2.6, 2.7, 2.8, 2.9, 2.10</p></li></ul></div></li></ul></div><div><p>Available for language levels: 2.6, 2.7, 2.8, 2.9, 2.10</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.max_memory_allocation</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Sets the size (in bytes) of each slot in the off-heap memory pool. DataWeave stores payloads that exceed this size in temporary files <code>dw-buffer-input-${count}.tmp</code> and <code>dw-buffer-output-${count}.tmp</code>.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Number</code></p></li><li><p>Default: <code>1572864</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.memory_pool_size</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Sets the number of slots in the memory pool. DataWeave buffers use off-heap memory from a pool, up to a defined size (<code>com.mulesoft.dw.memory_pool_size</code> * <code>com.mulesoft.dw.max_memory_allocation</code>). DataWeave allocates the remainder using heap memory. See <a href="dataweave-memory-management">DataWeave Memory Management</a>.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Number</code></p></li><li><p>Default: <code>60</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.messageLogging.debounceDuration</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Sets the number of milliseconds to wait before logging the same message again when using <code>MessageLoggingService.logXXXXDebounce</code> methods.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Long</code></p></li><li><p>Default: <code>3000</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.multipart.defaultContentType</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Sets the default Content-Type to use on parts of the <code>multipart/*</code> format when a <code>Content-Type</code> is not specified. See also, the multipart reader property <a href="dataweave-formats-multipart#properties">defaultContentType</a>. <em>Introduced in DataWeave 2.3 (2.3.0-20210720) for the August 2021 release of Mule 4.3.0-20210719.</em></p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>String</code></p></li><li><p>Default: <code>application/octet-stream</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.scheduler.size</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the size of DataWeave scheduler thread pool.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Number</code></p></li><li><p>Default: <code>100</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.stableOrderBy</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>When set to <code>true</code>, DataWeave throws <code>InvalidComparisonException</code> if the <code>orderBy</code> function attempts to compare values of different types. If set to <code>false</code>, coercions are used when comparing values of different types, which can cause the resulting order to be different from the expected one as in: .DataWeave script:</p></div><div><div><pre><code data-lang="xml">%dw 2.4
output application/json
---
[5, 1, "10"] orderBy $</code></pre></div></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>com.mulesoft.dw.stableOrderBy</code> is set to <code>false</code>, the script returns the following output: <code>[1, "10", 5]</code></p></li><li><p>If <code>com.mulesoft.dw.stableOrderBy</code> is set to <code>true</code>, the script throws <code>InvalidComparisonException</code>.</p></li><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default:</p><div><ul><li><p><code>false</code> for language levels: 2.9</p></li><li><p><code>true</code> for language levels: 2.10, 2.11, 2.12</p></li></ul></div></li></ul></div><div><p>Available for language levels: 2.9, 2.10, 2.11, 2.12</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.stacksize</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Sets the maximum size of the stack. When a function recurses too deeply, DataWeave throws an error, such as Stack Overflow. The maximum size limit is 256.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Number</code></p></li><li><p>Default: <code>256</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.stripTrailingZeroes</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>When set to <code>false</code>, DataWeave restores the behavior of preserving trailing zeroes for some formats (such as DataWeave, JSON, and YAML) present in 2.4.0. Starting with 2.5.0, trailing zeroes are removed by default. .DataWeave script:</p></div><div><div><pre><code data-lang="xml">%dw 2.4
output application/json
---
1.00</code></pre></div></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>com.mulesoft.dw.stripTrailingZeroes</code> is set to <code>false</code>, the script returns the following output: <code>1.00</code></p></li><li><p>If <code>com.mulesoft.dw.stripTrailingZeroes</code> is set to <code>true</code>, the script returns the following output: <code>1</code></p></li><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default:</p><div><ul><li><p><code>true</code> for language levels: 2.5, 2.6, 2.7, 2.8, 2.9</p></li></ul></div></li></ul></div><div><p>Available for language levels: 2.5, 2.6, 2.7, 2.8, 2.9</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.surrogateCharCodeSupport</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>When set to <code>true</code>, the <code>fromCharCode</code> function supports surrogate characters and throws an exception if the code point isnât a valid Unicode character.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default:</p><div><ul><li><p><code>false</code> for language levels: 2.6, 2.7, 2.8, 2.9</p></li><li><p><code>true</code> for language levels: 2.10</p></li></ul></div></li></ul></div><div><p>Available for language levels: 2.6, 2.7, 2.8, 2.9, 2.10</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.telemetry.bufferSize</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Determines the size in bytes of the telemetry event buffer.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Number</code></p></li><li><p>Default: <code>1048576</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.telemetry.enabled</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enables DataWeave telemetry service.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default: <code>false</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.telemetry.memory</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>When set to <code>true</code>, the telemetry service collects memory usage statistics.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default: <code>false</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.telemetry.sync</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>When set to <code>true</code>, telemetry events are published synchronously.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default: <code>false</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.track.cursor.close</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>When set to <code>true</code>, tracks the stack trace from which the {CursorProvider#close()} method is called. Use this property for troubleshooting, for example, if {CursorProvider#openCursor()} is called on a cursor that is already closed.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default: <code>false</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.valueSelector.selectsAlwaysFirst</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>When set to <code>true</code>, returns the first occurrence of an element (even if the element appears more than once). Enabling this behavior degrades performance.</p></div><div><p>This example illustrates the behavior that is controlled by this property. (Assume that the DataWeave script acts on the XML input.)</p></div><div><div>XML input:</div><div><pre><code data-lang="xml">&lt;root&gt;
  &lt;users&gt;
     &lt;user&gt;
       &lt;lname&gt;chibana&lt;/lname&gt;
       &lt;name&gt;Shoki&lt;/name&gt;
     &lt;/user&gt;
     &lt;user&gt;
       &lt;name&gt;Shoki&lt;/name&gt;
       &lt;name&gt;Tomo&lt;/name&gt;
     &lt;/user&gt;
  &lt;/users&gt;
&lt;/root&gt;</code></pre></div></div><div><div>DataWeave script:</div><div><pre><code data-lang="xml">%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
{
    shokis: payload.root.users.*user map $.name
}</code></pre></div></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>com.mulesoft.dw.valueSelector.selectsAlwaysFirst</code> is set to <code>true</code>, the script returns the following output:</p><div><div><pre><code data-lang="json">{
  "shokis": [
    "Shoki",
    "Shoki"
  ]
}</code></pre></div></div></li><li><p>If <code>com.mulesoft.dw.valueSelector.selectsAlwaysFirst</code> is set to <code>false</code>, the script returns the following output:</p><div><div><pre><code data-lang="json">{
  "shokis": [
    "Shoki",
    "Tomo"
  ]
}</code></pre></div></div></li><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default:</p><div><ul><li><p><code>false</code> for language levels: 2.3, 2.4, 2.5</p></li></ul></div></li></ul></div><div><p>Available for language levels: 2.3, 2.4, 2.5</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.workingdirectory.base</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the base directory in which temporary files are written.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>String</code></p></li><li><p>Default: <code>java.io.tmpdir (system property value)</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.workingdirectory.delete_sync</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>File deletion occurs synchronously when this property is set to <code>true</code>.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default: <code>false</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.workingdirectory.max_output_buffer_size</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the maximum size of a file written in bytes.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Long</code></p></li><li><p>Default: <code>-1</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.workingdirectory.tracking</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Each script generates temporary files in their own directory when this property is set to <code>true</code>.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default: <code>false</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.xml.supportDTD</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Controls whether DTD handling is enabled or disabled. When this property is set to <code>false</code>, DataWeave skips processing of both internal and external subsets.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default: <code>false</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.xml_reader.allowIndexedLatin1</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>When set to <code>true</code>, DataWeave lets you use the indexed reader for Latin1(ISO-8859-1) encoding. Use this for backward compatibility only, as Latin1 isnât fully supported.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default:</p><div><ul><li><p><code>true</code> for language levels: 2.6, 2.7, 2.8, 2.9</p></li><li><p><code>false</code> for language levels: 2.10, 2.11, 2.12</p></li></ul></div></li></ul></div><div><p>Available for language levels: 2.6, 2.7, 2.8, 2.5</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>com.mulesoft.dw.xml_reader.honourXmlDeclarationEncoding</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>When this property is set to <code>true</code>, DataWeave uses the encoding declared in the XML if present.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Type: <code>Boolean</code></p></li><li><p>Default:</p><div><ul><li><p><code>false</code> for language levels: 2.4, 2.5</p></li><li><p><code>true</code> for language levels: 2.6, 2.7, 2.8, 2.9, 2.10, 2.11, 2.12</p></li></ul></div></li></ul></div><div><p>Available for language levels: 2.4, 2.5, 2.6, 2.7, 2.8, 2.9,
