# take

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## take<T>(array: Array<T>, n: Number): Array<T>

Selects the first `n` elements. It returns an empty array when `n <= 0` and the original array when
`n > sizeOf(array)`.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>array</code></p></td><td><p>The array of elements.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>n</code></p></td><td><p>The number of elements to select.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example outputs an array that contains the values of first two elements of the input array.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Arrays
var users = ["Mariano", "Leandro", "Julian"]
output application/json
---
take(users, 2)
```

#### Output

```json
["Mariano", "Leandro"]
```

## take(array: Null, n: Any): Null

Helper function that enables `take` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._
---

# takeWhile

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## takeWhile<T>(array: Array<T>, condition: (item: T) -> Boolean): Array<T>

Selects elements from the array while the condition is met but stops the selection process when it
reaches an element that fails to satisfy the condition.

To select all elements that meet the condition, use the `filter` function.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>array</code></p></td><td><p>The array of elements.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>condition</code></p></td><td><p>The condition (or expression) used to match an element in the array.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example iterates over the elements in the array and selects only those with an index that is
`<= 1` and stops selecting elements when it reaches one that is greater than `2`. Notice that it
does not select the second `1` because of the `2` that precedes it in the array. The function
outputs the result into an array.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Arrays
output application/json
var arr = [0,1,2,1]
---
arr takeWhile $ <= 1
```

#### Output

```json
[0, 1]
```

## takeWhile(array: Null, condition: (item: Nothing) -> Any): Null

Helper function that enables `takeWhile` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._
---

# Arrays (dw::core::Arrays)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

This module contains helper functions for working with arrays.

To use this module, you must import it to your DataWeave code, for example, by adding the line
`import * from dw::core::Arrays` to the header of your DataWeave script.

## Functions

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><a href="dw-arrays-functions-countby">countBy</a></p></td><td><p>Counts the elements in an array that return <code>true</code> when the matching function is applied to the value of each element.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-arrays-functions-divideby">divideBy</a></p></td><td><p>Breaks up an array into sub-arrays that contain the specified number of elements.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-arrays-functions-drop">drop</a></p></td><td><p>Drops the first <code>n</code> elements. It returns the original array when <code>n &lt;= 0</code> and an empty array when <code>n &gt; sizeOf(array)</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-arrays-functions-dropwhile">dropWhile</a></p></td><td><p>Drops elements from the array while the condition is met but stops the selection process when it reaches an element that fails to satisfy the condition.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-arrays-functions-every">every</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if every element in the array matches the condition.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-arrays-functions-firstwith">firstWith</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the first element that satisfies the condition, or returns <code>null</code> if no element meets the condition.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-arrays-functions-indexof">indexOf</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the index of the first occurrence of an element within the array. If the value is not found, the function returns <code>-1</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-arrays-functions-indexwhere">indexWhere</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the index of the first occurrence of an element that matches a condition within the array. If no element matches the condition, the function returns <code>-1</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-arrays-functions-join">join</a></p></td><td><p>Joins two arrays of objects by a given ID criteria.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-arrays-functions-leftjoin">leftJoin</a></p></td><td><p>Joins two arrays of objects by a given ID criteria.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-arrays-functions-outerjoin">outerJoin</a></p></td><td><p>Joins two array of objects by a given <code>ID</code> criteria.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-arrays-functions-partition">partition</a></p></td><td><p>Separates the array into the elements that satisfy the condition from those that do not.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-arrays-functions-slice">slice</a></p></td><td><p>Selects the interval of elements that satisfy the condition: <code>from &lt;= indexOf(array) &lt; until</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-arrays-functions-some">some</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if at least one element in the array matches the specified condition.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-arrays-functions-splitat">splitAt</a></p></td><td><p>Splits an array into two at a given position.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-arrays-functions-splitwhere">splitWhere</a></p></td><td><p>Splits an array into two at the first position where the condition is met.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-arrays-functions-sumby">sumBy</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the sum of the values of the elements in an array.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-arrays-functions-take">take</a></p></td><td><p>Selects the first <code>n</code> elements. It returns an empty array when <code>n &lt;= 0</code> and the original array when <code>n &gt; sizeOf(array)</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-arrays-functions-takewhile">takeWhile</a></p></td><td><p>Selects elements from the array while the condition is met but stops the selection process when it reaches an element that fails to satisfy the condition.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# concatWith

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## concatWith(source: Binary, with: Binary): Binary

Concatenates the content of two binaries.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.5.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>source</code></p></td><td><p>Binary</p></td><td><p>The source binary content.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>with</code></p></td><td><p>Binary</p></td><td><p>The binary to append.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example concatenates two base-16 values into one binary value.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Binaries
output application/dw
---
"CAFE" as Binary {base: "16"} concatWith "ABCD" as Binary {base: "16"}
```

#### Output

```Json
"yv6rzQ==" as Binary {base: "64"}
```

## concatWith(source: Binary, with: Null): Binary

Helper function that enables `concatWith` to work with a `null` value.

## concatWith(source: Null, with: Binary): Binary

Helper function that enables `concatWith` to work with a `null` value.
---

# fromBase64

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## fromBase64(base64String: String): Binary

Transforms a Base64 string into a binary value.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>base64String</code></p></td><td><p>The Base64 string to transform.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example takes a Base64 encoded string and transforms it into a binary value. This example
assumes that the `payload` contains the Base64 string generated from an image in example
[toBase64](dw-binaries-functions-tobase64#toBase64-example). The output of this function is a binary
value that represents the image generated in example
[toBase64](https://docs.mulesoft.com/dataweave/latest/dw-binaries-functions-tobase64).

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Binaries
output application/octet-stream
---
fromBase64(payload)
```
---

# fromHex

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## fromHex(hexString: String): Binary

Transforms a hexadecimal string into a binary.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>hexString</code></p></td><td><p>A hexadecimal string to transform.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example transforms a hexadecimal string to "Mule". To make the resulting type clear, it outputs
data in the `application/dw` format.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Binaries
output application/dw
---
{ "hexToBinary": fromHex("4D756C65") }
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
   hexToBinary: "TXVsZQ==" as Binary {base: "64"}
}
```
---

# readLinesWith

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## readLinesWith(content: Binary, charset: String): Array<String>

Splits the specified binary content into lines and returns the results in an array.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>content</code></p></td><td><p>Binary data to read and split.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>charset</code></p></td><td><p>String representing the encoding to read.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example transforms binary content, which is separated into new lines (`\n`), in a
comma-separated array.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Binaries
var content = read("Line 1\nLine 2\nLine 3\nLine 4\nLine 5\n", "application/octet-stream")
output application/json
---
{
   lines : (content readLinesWith "UTF-8"),
   showType: typeOf(content)
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
   "lines": [ "Line 1", "Line 2", "Line 3", "Line 4", "Line 5" ],
   "showType": "Binary"
}
```
---

# toBase64

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## toBase64(content: Binary): String

Transforms a binary value into a Base64 string.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>content</code></p></td><td><p>The binary value to transform.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example transforms a binary value into a Base64 encoded string. In this case, the binary value
represents an image.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0

import dw::Crypto
import toBase64 from dw::core::Binaries

var emailChecksum = Crypto::MD5("achaval@gmail.com" as Binary)
var image = readUrl(log("https://www.gravatar.com/avatar/$(emailChecksum)"), "application/octet-stream")

output application/json
---
toBase64(image)
```

#### Output

This example outputs a Base64 encoded string. The resulting string was shortened for readability
purposes:

```JSON
"/9j/4AAQSkZJRgABAQEAYABgAAD//..."
```
---

# toHex

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## toHex(content: Binary): String

Transforms a binary value into a hexadecimal string.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>content</code></p></td><td><p>The <code>Binary</code> value to transform.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example transforms a binary version of "Mule" (defined in the variable, `myBinary`) to
hexadecimal.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Binaries
output application/json
var myBinary = "Mule" as Binary
var testType = typeOf(myBinary)
---
{
   "binaryToHex" : toHex(myBinary)
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "binaryToHex": "4D756C65" }
```
---

# writeLinesWith

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## writeLinesWith(content: Array<String>, charset: String): Binary

Writes the specified lines and returns the binary content.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>content</code></p></td><td><p>Array of items to write.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>charset</code></p></td><td><p>String representing the encoding to use when writing.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example inserts a new line (`\n`) after each iteration. Specifically, it uses `map` to iterate
over the result of `to(1, 10)`, `[1,2,3,4,5]`, then writes the specified content ("Line
$"), which includes the unnamed variable `$` for each number in the array.

Note that without `writeLinesWith "UTF-8"`, the expression `{ lines: to(1, 10) map "Line $" }`
simply returns an array of line numbers as the value of an object:
`{ "lines": [ "line 1", "line 2", "line 3", "line 4", "line 5" ] }`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Binaries
output application/json
---
{ lines: to(1, 10) map "Line $" writeLinesWith  "UTF-8" }
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
  "lines": "Line 1\nLine 2\nLine 3\nLine 4\nLine 5\n"
}
```
---

# Binaries (dw::core::Binaries)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

This module contains helper functions for working with binaries.

To use this module, you must import it to your DataWeave code, for example, by adding the line
`import * from dw::core::Binaries` to the header of your DataWeave script.

## Functions

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><a href="dw-binaries-functions-concatwith">concatWith</a></p></td><td><p>Concatenates the content of two binaries.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-binaries-functions-frombase64">fromBase64</a></p></td><td><p>Transforms a Base64 string into a binary value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-binaries-functions-fromhex">fromHex</a></p></td><td><p>Transforms a hexadecimal string into a binary.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-binaries-functions-readlineswith">readLinesWith</a></p></td><td><p>Splits the specified binary content into lines and returns the results in an array.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-binaries-functions-tobase64">toBase64</a></p></td><td><p>Transforms a binary value into a Base64 string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-binaries-functions-tohex">toHex</a></p></td><td><p>Transforms a binary value into a hexadecimal string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-binaries-functions-writelineswith">writeLinesWith</a></p></td><td><p>Writes the specified lines and returns the binary content.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# toArray

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## toArray(@StreamCapable text: String): Array<String>

Splits a `String` value into an `Array` of characters.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The <code>String</code> value to transform into an <code>Array</code> of characters (a <code>Array&lt;String&gt;</code> type).</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `toArray` behaves with different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Coercions
output application/json indent=false
---
{
  a: toArray(""),
  b: toArray("hola")
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{"a": [],"b": ["h","o","l","a"]}
```
---

# toBinary

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## toBinary(str: String, encoding: String): Binary

Transform a `String` value into a `Binary` value using the specified encoding.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>str</code></p></td><td><p>The <code>String</code> value to transform into a <code>Binary</code> value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>encoding</code></p></td><td><p>The encoding to apply to the <code>String</code> value. Accepts encodings that are supported by your JDK. For example, <code>encoding</code> accepts Java canonical names and aliases for the basic and extended encoding sets in Oracle JDK 8 and JDK 11.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `toBinary` behaves with different inputs. It produces output in the
`application/dw` format.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Coercions
output application/dw
---
{
  'UTF-16Ex': toBinary("DW", "UTF-16"),
  'utf16Ex': toBinary("DW", "utf16"),
  'UnicodeBigEx': toBinary("DW", "UnicodeBig"),
  'UTF-32Ex': toBinary("DW", "UTF-32"),
  'UTF_32Ex': toBinary("DW", "UTF_32")
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "UTF-16Ex": "/v8ARABX" as Binary {base: "64"},
  utf16Ex: "/v8ARABX" as Binary {base: "64"},
  UnicodeBigEx: "/v8ARABX" as Binary {base: "64"},
  "UTF-32Ex": "AAAARAAAAFc=" as Binary {base: "64"},
  UTF_32Ex: "AAAARAAAAFc=" as Binary {base: "64"}
}
```
---

# toDate

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## toDate(str: String, formatters: Array<Formatter>): Date

Transforms a `String` value into a `Date` value using the first `Formatter` that matches with the
given value to transform.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.5.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>str</code></p></td><td><p>String</p></td><td><p>The <code>String</code> value to transform into a <code>Date</code> value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>formatters</code></p></td><td><p>Array&lt;Formatter&gt;</p></td><td><p>The <code>array</code> of formatting to use on the <code>Date</code> value.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `toDate` behaves with different inputs. It produces output in the
`application/dw` format.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Coercions
import * from dw::Runtime
output application/dw
---
{
  a: toDate("2023-28-03", [{format: "yyyy/MM/dd"}, {format: "yyyy-dd-MM", locale: "en_US"}]),
  b: try(() -> toDate("2023-28-03", [{format: "yyyy/MM/dd"}])).error.message
}
```

#### Output

```DataWeave
{
  a: |2023-03-28| as Date {format: "yyyy-dd-MM", locale: "en_US"},
  b: "Could not find a valid formatter for '2023-28-03'"
}
```

## toDate(str: String, format: String | Null = null, locale: String | Null = null): Date

Transforms a `String` value into a `Date` value and accepts a format and locale.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>str</code></p></td><td><p>The <code>String</code> value to transform into a <code>Date</code> value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>format</code></p></td><td><p>The formatting to use on the <code>Date</code> value. A <code>null</code> value has no effect on the <code>Date</code> value. This parameter accepts Java character patterns based on ISO-8601. A <code>Date</code> value, such as <code>2011-12-03</code>, has the format <code>uuuu-MM-dd</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>locale</code></p></td><td><p>Optional ISO 3166 country code to use, such as <code>US</code>, <code>AR</code>, or <code>ES</code>. A <code>null</code> or absent value uses your JVM default.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `toDate` behaves with different inputs. It produces output in the
`application/dw` format.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Coercions
output application/dw
---
{
  a: toDate("2015-10-01"),
  b: toDate("2003/10/01","uuuu/MM/dd")
}
```

#### Output

```DataWeave
{
  a: |2015-10-01|,
  b: |2003-10-01| as Date {format: "uuuu/MM/dd"}
}
```
---

# toDateOrNull

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## toDateOrNull(str: String, formatters: Array<Formatter>): Date | Null

Transforms a `String` value into a `Date` value using the first `Formatter` that matches with the
given value to transform.

If none of the `Formatter` matches with the given value, the function returns a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.5.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>str</code></p></td><td><p>String</p></td><td><p>The <code>String</code> value to transform into a <code>Date</code> value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>formatters</code></p></td><td><p>Array&lt;Formatter&gt;</p></td><td><p>The <code>array</code> of formatting to use on the <code>Date</code> value.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `toDateOrNull` behaves with different inputs. It produces output in the
`application/dw` format.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Coercions
output application/dw
---
{
  a: toDateOrNull("2023-28-03", [{format: "yyyy/MM/dd"}, {format: "yyyy-dd-MM", locale: "en_US"}]),
  b: toDateOrNull("2023-28-03", [{format: "yyyy/MM/dd"}])
}
```

#### Output

```DataWeave
{
  a: |2023-03-28| as Date {format: "yyyy-dd-MM", locale: "en_US"},
  b: null
}
```
---

# toDateTime

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## toDateTime(str: String, formatters: Array<Formatter>): DateTime

Transforms a `String` value into a `DateTime` value using the first `Formatter` that matches with
the given value to transform.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.5.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>str</code></p></td><td><p>String</p></td><td><p>The <code>String</code> value to transform into a <code>DateTime</code> value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>formatters</code></p></td><td><p>Array&lt;Formatter&gt;</p></td><td><p>The <code>array</code> of formatting to use on the <code>DateTime</code> value.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `toDateTime` behaves with different inputs. It produces output in the
`application/dw` format.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Coercions
import * from dw::Runtime
output application/dw
---
{
  a: toDateTime("2003-10-01 23:57:59Z", [{format: "uuuu/MM/dd HH:mm:ssz"}, {format: "uuuu-MM-dd HH:mm:ssz"}]),
  b: try(() -> toDateTime("2003-10-01 23:57:59Z", [{format: "uuuu/MM/dd HH:mm:ssz"}])).error.message
}
```

#### Output

```DataWeave
{
  a: |2003-10-01T23:57:59Z| as DateTime {format: "uuuu-MM-dd HH:mm:ssz"},
  b: "Could not find a valid formatter for '2003-10-01 23:57:59Z'"
}
```

## toDateTime(number: Number, unit: MillisOrSecs | Null = null): DateTime

Transforms a `Number` value into a `DateTime` value using `milliseconds` or `seconds` as the unit.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>number</code></p></td><td><p>The <code>Number</code> value to transform into a <code>DateTime</code> value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>unit</code></p></td><td><p>The unit to use for the conversion: <code>"milliseconds"</code> or <code>"seconds"</code>. A <code>null</code> value for the <code>unit</code> field defaults to <code>"seconds"</code>.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `toDateTime` behaves with different inputs. It produces output in the
`application/dw` format.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Coercions
output application/dw
---
{
    fromEpoch: toDateTime(1443743879),
    fromMillis: toDateTime(1443743879000, "milliseconds")
}
```

#### Output

```DataWeave
{
  fromEpoch: |2015-10-01T23:57:59Z|,
  fromMillis: |2015-10-01T23:57:59Z| as DateTime {unit: "milliseconds"}
}
```

## toDateTime(str: String, format: String | Null = null, locale: String | Null = null): DateTime

Transforms a `String` value into a `DateTime` value and accepts a format and locale.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>str</code></p></td><td><p>The <code>String</code> value to transform into a <code>DateTime</code> value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>format</code></p></td><td><p>The formatting to use on the <code>DateTime</code> value. A <code>null</code> value has no effect on the <code>DateTime</code> value. This parameter accepts Java character patterns based on ISO-8601. A <code>DateTime</code> value, such as <code>2011-12-03T10:15:30.000000+01:00</code>, has the format <code>uuuu-MM-dd HH:mm:ssz</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>locale</code></p></td><td><p>Optional ISO 3166 country code to use, such as <code>US</code>, <code>AR</code>, or <code>ES</code>. A <code>null</code> or absent value uses your JVM default.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `toDateTime` behaves with different inputs. It produces output in the
`application/dw` format.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Coercions
output application/dw
---
{
   a: toDateTime("2015-10-01T23:57:59Z"),
   b: toDateTime("2003-10-01 23:57:59Z","uuuu-MM-dd HH:mm:ssz")
}
```

#### Output

```DataWeave
{
  a: |2015-10-01T23:57:59Z|,
  b: |2003-10-01T23:57:59Z| as DateTime {format: "uuuu-MM-dd HH:mm:ssz"}
}
```
---

# toDateTimeOrNull

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## toDateTimeOrNull(str: String, formatters: Array<Formatter>): DateTime | Null

Transforms a `String` value into a `DateTime` value using the first `Formatter` that matches with
the given value to transform.

If none of the `Formatter` matches with the given value, the function returns a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.5.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>str</code></p></td><td><p>String</p></td><td><p>The <code>String</code> value to transform into a <code>DateTime</code> value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>formatters</code></p></td><td><p>Array&lt;Formatter&gt;</p></td><td><p>The <code>array</code> of formatting to use on the <code>DateTime</code> value.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `toDateTimeOrNull` behaves with different inputs. It produces output in the
`application/dw` format.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Coercions
output application/dw
---
{
  a: toDateTimeOrNull("2003-10-01 23:57:59Z", [{format: "uuuu/MM/dd HH:mm:ssz"}, {format: "uuuu-MM-dd HH:mm:ssz"}]),
  b: toDateTimeOrNull("2003-10-01 23:57:59Z", [{format: "uuuu/MM/dd HH:mm:ssz"}])
}
```

#### Output

```DataWeave
{
  a: |2003-10-01T23:57:59Z| as DateTime {format: "uuuu-MM-dd HH:mm:ssz"},
  b: null
}
```
---

# toLocalDateTime

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## toLocalDateTime(str: String, formatters: Array<Formatter>): LocalDateTime

Transforms a `String` value into a `LocalDateTime` value using the first `Formatter` that matches
with the given value to transform.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.5.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>str</code></p></td><td><p>String</p></td><td><p>The <code>String</code> value to transform into a <code>LocalDateTime</code> value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>formatters</code></p></td><td><p>Array&lt;Formatter&gt;</p></td><td><p>The <code>array</code> of formatting to use on the <code>LocalDateTime</code> value.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `toLocalDateTime` behaves with different inputs. It produces output in the
`application/dw` format.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Coercions
import * from dw::Runtime
output application/dw
---
{
  a: toLocalDateTime("2003-10-01 23:57:59", [{format: "uuuu/MM/dd HH:mm:ss"}, {format: "uuuu-MM-dd HH:mm:ss"}]),
  b: try(() -> toLocalDateTime("2003-10-01 23:57:59", [{format: "uuuu/MM/dd HH:mm:ss"}])).error.message
}
```

#### Output

```DataWeave
{
  a: |2003-10-01T23:57:59| as LocalDateTime {format: "uuuu-MM-dd HH:mm:ss"},
  b: "Could not find a valid formatter for '2003-10-01 23:57:59'"
}
```

## toLocalDateTime(str: String, format: String | Null = null, locale: String | Null = null): LocalDateTime

Transforms a `String` value into a `LocalDateTime` value and accepts a format and locale.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>str</code></p></td><td><p>The <code>String</code> value to transform into a <code>LocalDateTime</code> value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>format</code></p></td><td><p>The formatting to use on the <code>LocalDateTime</code> value. A <code>null</code> value has no effect on the <code>LocalDateTime</code> value. This parameter accepts Java character patterns based on ISO-8601. A <code>LocalDateTime</code> value, such as <code>2011-12-03T10:15:30.000000</code> has the format <code>uuuu-MM-dd HH:mm:ss</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>locale</code></p></td><td><p>Optional ISO 3166 country code to use, such as <code>US</code>, <code>AR</code>, or <code>ES</code>. A <code>null</code> or absent value uses your JVM default.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `toLocalDateTime` behaves with different inputs. It produces output in the
`application/dw` format.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0

output application/dw
---
{
  a: toLocalDateTime("2015-10-01T23:57:59"),
  b: toLocalDateTime("2003-10-01 23:57:59","uuuu-MM-dd HH:mm:ss")
```

#### Output

```DataWeave
{
  a: |2015-10-01T23:57:59|,
```

## toLocalTime(str: String, format: String | Null = null, locale: String | Null = null): LocalTime

Transforms a `String` value into a `LocalTime` value and accepts a format and locale.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>str</code></p></td><td><p>The <code>String</code> value to transform into a <code>LocalTime</code> value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>format</code></p></td><td><p>The formatting to use on the <code>LocalTime</code> value. A <code>null</code> value has no effect on the <code>LocalTime</code> value. This parameter accepts Java character patterns based on ISO-8601. A <code>LocalTime</code> value, such as <code>22:15:30.000000</code>, has the format <code>HH:mm:ss.n</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>locale</code></p></td><td><p>Optional ISO 3166 country code to use, such as <code>US</code>, <code>AR</code>, or <code>ES</code>. ---
```DataWeave
{
   toLocalTimeEx: toLocalTime("23:57:59"),
   toLocalTimeEx2: toLocalTime("13:44:12.283","HH:mm:ss.n")
}
```

#### Output

```DataWeave
{
  "toLocalTimeEx": "23:57:59",
  "toLocalTimeEx2": "13:44:12.283"
}
```

# toNumber

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## toNumber(str: String, formatters: Array<Formatter>): Number

Transforms a `String` value into a `Number` value using the first `Formatter` that matches with the
given value to transform.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.5.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>str</code></p></td><td><p>String</p></td><td><p>The <code>String</code> value to transform into a <code>Number</code> value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>formatters</code></p></td><td><p>Array&lt;DatesFormatter&gt;</p></td><td><p>The <code>array</code> of formatting to use on the <code>Number</code> value.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `toNumber` behaves with different inputs. It produces output in the
`application/dw` format.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Coercions
import * from dw::Runtime
output application/dw
---
{
  a: toNumber("0.005", [{format: "seconds"}, {format: ".00"}]),
  b: try(() -> toNumber("0.005", [{format: "seconds"}])).error.message
}
```

#### Output

```DataWeave
{
  a: 0.005 as Number {format: ".00"},
  b: "Could not find a valid formatter for '0.005'"
}
```

## toNumber(dateTime: DateTime, unit: MillisOrSecs | Null = null): Number

A variant of `toNumber` that transforms a `DateTime` value into a number of seconds or milliseconds,
depending on the selected unit.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>dateTime</code></p></td><td><p>The <code>DateTime</code> value to transform into a <code>Number</code> value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>unit</code></p></td><td><p>The unit of time (<code>"milliseconds"</code> or <code>"seconds"</code>) to use Given a <code>null</code> value, the function uses <code>"seconds"</code>.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `toNumber` behaves with different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Coercions
output application/json
---
{
    epoch: toNumber(|2015-10-01T23:57:59Z|),
    millis: toNumber(|2015-10-01T23:57:59Z|, "milliseconds")
}
```

#### Output

```DataWeave
{
  "epoch": 1443743879,
  "millis": 1443743879000
}
```

## toNumber(period: Period, unit: PeriodUnits | Null = null): Number

A variant of `toNumber` that transforms a `Period` value into a number of hours, minutes, seconds,
milliseconds or nanoseconds (`nanos`).

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>period</code></p></td><td><p>The <code>Period</code> value to transform into a <code>Number</code> value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>unit</code></p></td><td><p>The unit to apply to the specified <code>period</code>: <code>hours</code>, <code>minutes</code>, <code>seconds</code>, <code>milliseconds</code>, or <code>nanos</code>.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `toNumber` behaves with different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Coercions
output application/json
---
{
    toSecondsEx1: toNumber(|PT1H10M|, "seconds"),
    toSecondsEx2: toNumber(|PT1M7S|, "milliseconds")
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "toSecondsEx1": 4200,
  "toSecondsEx2": 67000
}
```

## toNumber(value: String | Key, format: String | Null = null, locale: String | Null = null): Number

A variant of `toNumber` that transforms a `String` or `Key` value into a `Number` value and that
accepts a format and locale.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>value</code></p></td><td><p>The <code>String</code> or <code>Key</code> value to transform into a <code>Number</code> value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>format</code></p></td><td><p>Optional formatting to apply to the <code>value</code>. A <code>format</code> accepts <code>#</code> or <code>0</code> (but not both) as placeholders for <em>decimal</em> values and a single whole number that is less than <code>10</code>. Only one decimal point is permitted. A <code>null</code> or empty <code>String</code> value has no effect on the <code>Number</code> value. Other characters produce an error.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>locale</code></p></td><td><p>Optional ISO 3166 country code to use, such as <code>US</code>, <code>AR</code>, or <code>ES</code>. A <code>null</code> or absent value uses your JVM default.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `toNumber` behaves with different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Coercions
var myKey = keysOf({"123" : "myValue"})
output application/json
---
 {
     "default": toNumber("1.0"),
     "withFormat": toNumber("0.005",".00"),
     "withLocal": toNumber("1,25","#.##","ES"),
     "withExtraPlaceholders": toNumber("5.55","####.####"),
     "keyToNumber": toNumber(myKey[0])
 }
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "default": 1.0,
  "withFormat": 0.005,
  "withLocal": 1.25,
  "withExtraPlaceholders": 5.55,
  "keyToNumber": 123
}
```
---

# toString

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## toString(number: Number, format: String | Null = null, locale: String | Null = null, roundMode: RoundingMode | Null = null): String

A variant of `toString` that transforms a `Number` value (whole or decimal) into a `String` value
and accepts a format, locale, and rounding mode value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>number</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>The <code>Number</code> value to format.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>format</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>The formatting to apply to the <code>Number</code> value. A <code>format</code> accepts <code>#</code> or <code>0</code> (but not both) as placeholders for <em>decimal</em> values, and only one decimal point is permitted. A <code>null</code> or empty <code>String</code> value has no effect on the <code>Number</code> value. Most other values are treated as literals, but you must escape special characters, such as a dollar sign (for example, <code>\$</code>). Inner quotations must be closed and differ from the surrounding quotations.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>locale</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Optional ISO 3166 country code to use, such as <code>US</code>, <code>AR</code>, or <code>ES</code>. A <code>null</code> or absent value uses your JVM default. When you pass a translatable format, such as <code>eeee</code> and <code>MMMM</code>, a <code>locale</code> (such as <code>ES</code>) transforms the corresponding numeric values to a localized string.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>roundMode</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Optional parameter for rounding decimal values when the formatting presents a rounding choice, such as a format of <code>0.#</code> for the decimal <code>0.15</code>. The default is <code>HALF_UP</code>, and a <code>null</code> value behaves like <code>HALF_UP</code>. Only one of the following values is permitted:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>UP</code>: Always rounds away from zero (for example, <code>0.01</code> to <code>"0.1"</code> and <code>-0.01</code> to <code>"-0.1"</code>). Increments the preceding digit to a non-zero fraction and never decreases the magnitude of the calculated value.</p></li><li><p><code>DOWN</code>: Always rounds towards zero (for example, <code>0.19</code> to <code>"0.1"</code> and <code>-0.19</code> to <code>"-0.1"</code>). Never increments the digit before a discarded fraction (which truncates to the preceding digit) and never increases the magnitude of the calculated value.</p></li><li><p><code>CEILING</code>: Rounds towards positive infinity and behaves like <code>UP</code> if the result is positive (for example, <code>0.35</code> to <code>"0.4"</code>). If the result is negative, this mode behaves like <code>DOWN</code> (for example, <code>-0.35</code> to <code>"-0.3"</code>). This mode never decreases the calculated value.</p></li><li><p><code>FLOOR</code>: Rounds towards negative infinity and behaves like DOWN if the result is positive (for example, <code>0.35</code> to <code>"0.3"</code>). If the result is negative, this mode behaves like <code>UP</code> (for example, <code>-0.35</code> to <code>"-0.4"</code>). The mode never increases the calculated value.</p></li><li><p><code>HALF_UP</code>: Default mode, which rounds towards the nearest "neighbor" unless both neighbors are equidistant, in which case, this mode rounds up. For example, <code>0.35</code> rounds to <code>"0.4"</code>, <code>0.34</code> rounds to <code>"0.3"</code>, and <code>0.36</code> rounds to <code>"0.4"</code>. Negative decimals numbers round similarly. For example, <code>-0.35</code> rounds to `"-0.4`".</p></li><li><p><code>HALF_DOWN</code>: Rounds towards the nearest numeric "neighbor" unless both neighbors are equidistant, in which case, this mode rounds down. For example, <code>0.35</code> rounds to <code>"0.3"</code>, <code>0.34</code> rounds to <code>"0.3"</code>, and <code>0.36</code> rounds to <code>"0.4"</code>. Negative decimals numbers round similarly. For example, <code>-0.35</code> rounds to <code>"-0.3"</code>.</p></li><li><p><code>HALF_EVEN</code>: For decimals that end in a <code>5</code> (such as, <code>1.125</code> and <code>1.135</code>), the behavior depends on the number that precedes the <code>5</code>. <code>HALF_EVEN</code> rounds up when the next-to-last digit before the <code>5</code> is an odd number but rounds down when the next-to-last digit is even. For example, <code>0.225</code> rounds to <code>"0.22"</code>, <code>0.235</code> <em>and</em> <code>0.245</code> round to <code>"0.24"</code>, and <code>0.255</code> rounds to <code>"0.26"</code>. Negative decimals round similarly, for example, <code>-0.225</code> to <code>"-0.22"</code>. When the last digit is not <code>5</code>, the setting behaves like <code>HALF_UP</code>. Rounding of monetary values sometimes follows the <code>HALF_EVEN</code> pattern.</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `toString` behaves with different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Coercions
output application/json
---
{
    a: toString(1.0),
    b: toString(0.005,".00"),
    c: toString(0.035,"#.##","ES"),
    d: toString(0.005,"#.##","ES","HALF_EVEN"),
    e: toString(0.035,"#.00",null,"HALF_EVEN"),
    f: toString(1.1234,"\$.## 'in my account'")
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "a": "1",
  "b": ".01",
  "c": "0,04",
  "d": "0",
  "e": ".04",
  "f": "$1.12 in my account"
}
```

## toString(date: Date | DateTime | LocalDateTime | LocalTime | Time, format: String | Null = null, locale: String | Null = null): String

A variant of `toString` that transforms a `Date`, `DateTime`, `LocalTime`, `LocalDateTime`, or
`Time` value into a `String` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>date</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>The <code>Date</code>, <code>DateTime</code>, <code>LocalTime</code>, <code>LocalDateTime</code>, or <code>Time</code> value to coerce to a <code>String</code> type.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>format</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>The ISO-8601 formatting to use on the date or time. For example, this parameter accepts character patterns based on the Java 8 <code>java.time.format</code>. A <code>null</code> value has no effect on the value. Defaults:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>Date</code> example: <code>2011-12-03</code> (equivalent format: <code>uuuu-MM-dd</code>)</p></li><li><p><code>DateTime</code> example: <code>2011-12-03T10:15:30.000000+01:00</code> (equivalent format: <code>uuuu-MM-dd HH:mm:ssz</code>)</p></li><li><p><code>LocalDateTime</code> example: <code>2011-12-03T10:15:30.000000</code> (equivalent format: <code>uuuu-MM-dd HH:mm:ss</code>)</p></li><li><p><code>LocalTime</code> example: <code>10:15:30.000000</code> (equivalent format: <code>HH:mm:ss.n</code>)</p></li><li><p><code>Time</code> example: <code>10:15:30.000000Z</code> (equivalent format: <code>HH:mm:ss.nxxxz</code>)</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>locale</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Optional ISO 3166 country code to use, such as <code>US</code>, <code>AR</code>, or <code>ES</code>. A <code>null</code> or absent value uses your JVM default. When you pass a translatable format, such as <code>eeee</code> and <code>MMMM</code>, a <code>locale</code> (such as <code>ES</code>) transforms the corresponding numeric values to a localized string.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `toString` behaves with different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Coercions
output application/json
---
{
   aDate: toString(|2003-10-01|, "uuuu/MM/dd"),
   aDateTime: toString(|2018-09-17T22:13:00-03:00|),
   aLocalTime: toString(|23:57:59|, "HH-mm-ss"),
   aLocalDateTime : toString(|2015-10-01T23:57:59|),
   aLocalDateTimeFormatted: toString(|2003-10-01T23:57:59|, "uuuu-MM-dd HH:mm:ss a"),
   aLocalDateTimeFormattedAndLocalizedSpain: toString(|2003-01-01T23:57:59|, "eeee, dd MMMM, uuuu HH:mm:ss a", "ES"),
   aTime: typeOf(|22:10:18Z|),
   aTimeZone: toString(|-03:00|)
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "aDate": "2003/10/01",
  "aDateTime": "2018-09-17T22:13:00-03:00",
  "aLocalTime": "23-57-59",
  "aLocalDateTime": "2015-10-01T23:57:59",
  "aLocalDateTimeFormatted": "2003-10-01 23:57:59 PM",
  "aLocalDateTimeFormattedAndLocalizedSpain": "miÃ©rcoles, 01 enero, 2003 23:57:59 p. m.",
  "aTime": "Time",
  "aTimeZone": "-03:00"
}
```

## toString(binary: Binary, encoding: String): String

A variant of `toString` that transforms a `Binary` value into a `String` value with the specified
encoding.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>binary</code></p></td><td><p>The <code>Binary</code> value to coerce to a <code>String</code> value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>encoding</code></p></td><td><p>The encoding to apply to the <code>String</code> value. Accepts encodings that are supported by your JDK. For example, <code>encoding</code> accepts Java canonical names and aliases for the basic and extended encoding sets in Oracle JDK 8 and JDK 11.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `toString` behaves with different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Coercions
var binaryData= "DW Test" as Binary {encoding: "UTF-32"}
output application/json
---
{
  a: toString(binaryData, "UTF-32"),
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "a": "DW Test"
}
```

## toString(data: TimeZone | Uri | Boolean | Period | Regex | Key): String

A variant of `toString` that transforms a `TimeZone`, `Uri`, `Boolean`, `Period`, `Regex`, or `Key`
value into a string.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>data</code></p></td><td><p>The <code>TimeZone</code>, <code>Uri</code>, <code>Boolean</code>, <code>Period</code>, <code>Regex</code>, or <code>Key</code> value to coerce to a <code>String</code> value.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `toString` behaves with different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Coercions
output application/json
---
{
  transformTimeZone: toString(|Z|),
  transformBoolean: toString(true),
  transformPeriod: toString(|P1D|),
  transformRegex: toString(/a-Z/),
  transformPeriod: toString(|PT8M10S|),
  transformUri: toString("https://docs.mulesoft.com/" as Uri)
}  ++
{ transformKey : toString((keysOf({ "aKeyToString" : "aValue"})[0])) }
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "transformTimeZone": "Z",
  "transformBoolean": "true",
  "transformPeriod": "P1D",
  "transformRegex": "a-Z",
  "transformPeriod": "PT8M10S",
  "transformUri": "https://docs.mulesoft.com/",
  "transformKey": "aKeyToString"
}
```

## toString(arr: Array<String>): String

A variant of `toString` that joins an `Array` of characters into a single `String` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>arr</code></p></td><td><p>The <code>Array</code> of characters to transform into a <code>String</code> value.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `toString` behaves with different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Coercions
output application/json
---
{
  a: toString([]),
  b: toString(["h", "o", "l", "a"])
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "a": "",
  "b": "hola"
}
```
---

# toTime

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## toTime(str: String, formatters: Array<Formatter>): Time

Transforms a `String` value into a `Time` value using the first `Formatter` that matches with the
given value to transform.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.5.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>str</code></p></td><td><p>String</p></td><td><p>The <code>String</code> value to transform into a <code>Time</code> value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>formatters</code></p></td><td><p>Array&lt;Formatter&gt;</p></td><td><p>The <code>array</code> of formatting to use on the <code>Time</code> value.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `toTime` behaves with different inputs. It produces output in the
`application/dw` format.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Coercions
import * from dw::Runtime
output application/dw
---
{
  a: toTime("13:44:12.283-08:00", [{format: "HH:mm:ss.xxx"}, {format: "HH:mm:ss.nxxx"}]),
  b: try(() -> toTime("13:44:12.283-08:00", [{format: "HH:mm:ss.xxx"}]).error.message
}
```

#### Output

```DataWeave
{
  a: |13:44:12.000000283-08:00| as Time {format: "HH:mm:ss.nxxx"},
  b: "Could not find a valid formatter for '13:44:12.283-08:00'"
}
```

## toTime(str: String, format: String | Null = null, locale: String | Null = null): Time

Transforms a `String` value into a `Time` value and accepts a format and locale.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>str</code></p></td><td><p>The <code>String</code> value to transform into a <code>Time</code> value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>format</code></p></td><td><p>The formatting to use on the <code>Time</code> value. A <code>null</code> value has no effect on the <code>Time</code> value. This parameter accepts Java character patterns based on ISO-8601. A <code>Time</code> value, such as <code>10:15:30.000000</code>, has the format <code>HH:mm:ss.nxxx</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>locale</code></p></td><td><p>Optional ISO 3166 country code to use, such as <code>US</code>, <code>AR</code>, or <code>ES</code>. A <code>null</code> or absent value uses your JVM default.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `toTime` behaves with different inputs. It produces output in the
`application/dw` format.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Coercions
output application/dw
---
{
   a: toTime("23:57:59Z"),
   b: toTime("13:44:12.283-08:00","HH:mm:ss.nxxx")
}
```

#### Output

```DataWeave
{
  a: |23:57:59Z|,
  b: |13:44:12.000000283-08:00| as Time {format: "HH:mm:ss.nxxx"}
}
```
---

# toTimeOrNull

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## toTimeOrNull(str: String, formatters: Array<Formatter>): Time | Null

Transforms a `String` value into a `Time` value using the first `Formatter` that matches with the
given value to transform.

If none of the `Formatter` matches with the given value, the function returns a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.5.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>str</code></p></td><td><p>String</p></td><td><p>The <code>String</code> value to transform into a <code>Time</code> value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>formatters</code></p></td><td><p>Array&lt;Formatter&gt;</p></td><td><p>The <code>array</code> of formatting to use on the <code>Time</code> value.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `toTimeOrNull` behaves with different inputs. It produces output in the
`application/dw` format.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Coercions
output application/dw
---
{
  a: toTimeOrNull("13:44:12.283-08:00", [{format: "HH:mm:ss.xxx"}, {format: "HH:mm:ss.nxxx"}]),
  b: toTimeOrNull("13:44:12.283-08:00", [{format: "HH:mm:ss.xxx"}])
}
```

#### Output

```DataWeave
{
  a: |13:44:12.000000283-08:00| as Time {format: "HH:mm:ss.nxxx"},
  b: null
}
```
---

# toTimeZone

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## toTimeZone(str: String): TimeZone

Transform a `String` value into a `TimeZone` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>str</code></p></td><td><p>The <code>String</code> value to transform into a <code>TimeZone</code> value.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

`application/dw` format.

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0

output application/dw

{
   toTimeZoneOffset: toTimeZone("-03:00"),
   toTimeZoneAbbreviation: toTimeZone("Z"),
   toTimeZoneName: toTimeZone("America/Argentina/Buenos_Aires")
}
```

#### Output

```DataWeave
  toTimeZoneAbbreviation: |Z|,

}
```

# Coercions Types (dw::util::Coercions)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Type</th><th>Definition</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Formatter</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Formatter = { format?: String, locale?: String }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Type used for formatting <code>Dates</code> types and <code>Number</code>. Supports the following fields:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>format</code>: (optional) The ISO-8601 formatting to use on the date or time. For example, this parameter accepts character patterns based on the Java 8 <code>java.time.format</code>. A <code>null</code> value has no effect on the value.</p></li><li><p><code>locale</code>: (optional) ISO 3166 country code to use, such as <code>US</code>, <code>AR</code>, or <code>ES</code>. A <code>null</code> or absent value uses your JVM default. When you pass a translatable format, such as <code>eeee</code> and <code>MMMM</code>, a <code>locale</code> (such as <code>ES</code>) transforms the corresponding numeric values to a localized string.</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>MillisOrSecs</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type MillisOrSecs = "milliseconds" | "seconds"</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Type used for setting units to <code>"milliseconds"</code> or <code>"seconds"</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>PeriodUnits</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type PeriodUnits = "hours" | "minutes" | "seconds" | "milliseconds" | "nanos"</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Type used for setting units of a <code>Period</code> value to <code>"hours"</code>, <code>"minutes"</code>, <code>"seconds"</code>, <code>"milliseconds"</code>, or <code>"nanos"</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>RoundingMode</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type RoundingMode = "UP" | "DOWN" | "CEILING" | "FLOOR" | "HALF_UP" | "HALF_DOWN" | "HALF_EVEN"</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Type used when rounding decimal values up or down.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# Coercions (dw::util::Coercions)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

This utility module assists with type coercions.

To use this module, you must import it to your DataWeave code, for example, by adding the line
`import * from dw::util::Coercions` to the header of your DataWeave script.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

## Functions

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><a href="dw-coercions-functions-toarray">toArray</a></p></td><td><p>Splits a <code>String</code> value into an <code>Array</code> of characters.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-coercions-functions-tobinary">toBinary</a></p></td><td><p>Transform a <code>String</code> value into a <code>Binary</code> value using the specified encoding.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-coercions-functions-toboolean">toBoolean</a></p></td><td><p>Transform a <code>String</code> value into a <code>Boolean</code> value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-coercions-functions-todate">toDate</a></p></td><td><p>Transforms a <code>String</code> value into a <code>Date</code> value using the first <code>Formatter</code> that matches with the given value to transform.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-coercions-functions-todateornull">toDateOrNull</a></p></td><td><p>Transforms a <code>String</code> value into a <code>Date</code> value using the first <code>Formatter</code> that matches with the given value to transform.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-coercions-functions-todatetime">toDateTime</a></p></td><td><p>Transforms a <code>String</code> value into a <code>DateTime</code> value using the first <code>Formatter</code> that matches with the given value to transform.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-coercions-functions-todatetimeornull">toDateTimeOrNull</a></p></td><td><p>Transforms a <code>String</code> value into a <code>DateTime</code> value using the first <code>Formatter</code> that matches with the given value to transform.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-coercions-functions-tolocaldatetime">toLocalDateTime</a></p></td><td><p>Transforms a <code>String</code> value into a <code>LocalDateTime</code> value using the first <code>Formatter</code> that matches with the given value to transform.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-coercions-functions-tolocaldatetimeornull">toLocalDateTimeOrNull</a></p></td><td><p>Transforms a <code>String</code> value into a <code>LocalDateTime</code> value using the first <code>Formatter</code> that matches with the given value to transform.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-coercions-functions-tolocaltime">toLocalTime</a></p></td><td><p>Transforms a <code>String</code> value into a <code>LocalTime</code> value using the first <code>Formatter</code> that matches with the given value to transform.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-coercions-functions-tolocaltimeornull">toLocalTimeOrNull</a></p></td><td><p>Transforms a <code>String</code> value into a <code>LocalTime</code> value using the first <code>Formatter</code> that matches with the given value to transform.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-coercions-functions-tonumber">toNumber</a></p></td><td><p>Transforms a <code>String</code> value into a <code>Number</code> value using the first <code>Formatter</code> that matches with the given value to transform.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-coercions-functions-tonumberornull">toNumberOrNull</a></p></td><td><p>Transforms a <code>String</code> value into a <code>Number</code> value using the first <code>Formatter</code> that matches with the given value to transform.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-coercions-functions-toperiod">toPeriod</a></p></td><td><p>Transform a <code>String</code> value into a <code>Period</code> value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-coercions-functions-toregex">toRegex</a></p></td><td><p>Transforms a <code>String</code> value into a <code>Regex</code> value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-coercions-functions-tostring">toString</a></p></td><td><p>A variant of <code>toString</code> that transforms a <code>Number</code> value (whole or decimal) into a <code>String</code> value and accepts a format, locale, and rounding mode value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-coercions-functions-totime">toTime</a></p></td><td><p>Transforms a <code>String</code> value into a <code>Time</code> value using the first <code>Formatter</code> that matches with the given value to transform.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-coercions-functions-totimeornull">toTimeOrNull</a></p></td><td><p>Transforms a <code>String</code> value into a <code>Time</code> value using the first <code>Formatter</code> that matches with the given value to transform.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-coercions-functions-totimezone">toTimeZone</a></p></td><td><p>Transform a <code>String</code> value into a <code>TimeZone</code> value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-coercions-functions-touri">toUri</a></p></td><td><p>Transforms a <code>String</code> value into a <code>Uri</code> value.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Types

- [Coercions Types](dw-coercions-types)
---

# Core Annotations (dw::Core)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Annotation</th><th>Definition</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>AnnotationTarget</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>@AnnotationTarget(targets: Array&lt;"Function" | "Parameter" | "Variable" | "Import" | "Type" | "Version" | "Namespace" | "KeyType" | "TypeExpression" | "Expression" | "Annotation"&gt;)</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Annotation that limits the application of an annotation. An example is <code>@AnnotationTarget(targets = ["Function", "Variable"])</code>, which limits the scope of the annotation <code>annotation TailRec()</code> to functions and variables. If no <code>AnnotationTarget</code> is specified, an annotation can apply to any valid target.</p></div><div><p>Annotation Targets:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>Parameter</code>: For function parameters.</p></li><li><p><code>Function</code>: For function definitions.</p></li><li><p><code>Variable</code>: For variable definitions.</p></li><li><p><code>Import</code>: For import definitions.</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Deprecated</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>@Deprecated(since: String, replacement: String)</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Annotation that marks a function as deprecated.</p></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0.</em></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>DesignOnlyType</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>@DesignOnlyType()</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Annotation that marks a parameter type as <em>design only</em> to indicate that the field type is validated only at design time. At runtime, only minimal type validation takes place. This annotation is useful for performance, especially with complex Object types.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Experimental</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>@Experimental()</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Annotation that identifies a feature as experimental and subject to change or removal in the future.</p></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0.</em></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>GlobalDescription</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>@GlobalDescription()</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Annotation used to identify the function description to use for the functionâs documentation. This annotation is useful for selecting the correct function description when the function is overloaded.</p></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0.</em></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Interceptor</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>@Interceptor(interceptorFunction: String | (annotationArgs: Object, targetFunctionName: String, args: Array&lt;Any&gt;, callback: (args: Array&lt;Any&gt;) -&gt; Any) -&gt; Any)</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Annotation that marks another annotation as an Interceptor so that the marked annotation will wrap an annotated function with an <code>interceptorFunction</code>. An example is the <code>RuntimePrivilege</code> annotation, which is annotated by <code>@Interceptor(interceptorFunction = "@native system::SecurityManagerCheckFunctionValue")</code>. The <code>readUrl</code> function definition is annotated by <code>@RuntimePrivilege(requires = "Resource")</code>.</p></div><div><p><em>Experimental:</em> This experimental feature is subject to change or removal from future versions of DataWeave.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Internal</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>@Internal(permits: Array&lt;String&gt;)</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Annotation that marks a function as <em>internal</em> and not to be used.</p></div><div><div><pre>_Introduced in DataWeave 2.4.0. Supported by Mule 4.4.0 and later._</pre></div></div><div><p><em>Experimental:</em> This experimental feature is subject to change or removal from future versions of DataWeave.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Labels</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>@Labels(labels: Array&lt;String&gt;)</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Annotation for labeling a function or variable definition so that it becomes more easy to discover. An example is <code>@Labels(labels =["append", "concat"])</code>.</p></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0.</em></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Lazy</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>@Lazy()</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Annotation that marks a variable declaration for lazy initialization.</p></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.3.0.</em></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Metadata</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>@Metadata(key: String)</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Annotation used to mark annotations that represent metadata.</p></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.8.0.</em></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>RuntimePrivilege</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>@RuntimePrivilege(requires: String)</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Annotation used to indicate that a function requires runtime privileges to execute. An example is <code>@RuntimePrivilege(requires = "Resource")</code>, which annotates the <code>readUrl</code> function definition.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Since</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>@Since(version: String)</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Annotation that identifies the DataWeave version in which the annotated functionality was introduced. An example is <code>@Since(version = "2.4.0")</code>.</p></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave 2.3.0. Supported by Mule 4.3 and later.</em></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>StreamCapable</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>@StreamCapable()</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Annotation that marks a parameter as stream capable, which means that this field will consume an array of objects in a forward-only manner. Examples of functions with <code>@StreamCapable</code> fields are <code>map</code>, <code>mapObject</code>, and <code>pluck</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>TailRec</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>@TailRec()</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Annotation that marks a function as tail recursive. If a function with this annotation is not tail recursive, the function will fail.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>UntrustedCode</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>@UntrustedCode(privileges: Array&lt;String&gt;)</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Annotation that marks a script as untrusted, which means that the script has no privileges. For example, such a script cannot gain access to environment variables or read a resource from a URL.</p></div><div><p><em>Experimental:</em> This experimental feature is subject to change or removal from future versions of DataWeave.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# abs

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## abs(number: Number): Number

Returns the absolute value of a number.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>number</code></p></td><td><p>The number to evaluate.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example returns the absolute value of the specified numbers.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
[ abs(-2), abs(2.5), abs(-3.4), abs(3) ]
```

#### Output

```JSON
[ 2, 2.5, 3.4, 3 ]
```
---

# avg

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## avg(values: Array<Number>): Number

Returns the average of numbers listed in an array.

An array that is empty or that contains a non-numeric value results in an error.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>values</code></p></td><td><p>The input array of numbers.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example returns the average of multiple arrays.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
{ a: avg([1, 1000]), b: avg([1, 2, 3]) }
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "a": 500.5, "b": 2 }
```
---

# ceil

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## ceil(number: Number): Number

Rounds a number up to the nearest whole number.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>number</code></p></td><td><p>The number to round.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example rounds numbers up to the nearest whole numbers. Notice that `2.1` rounds up to `3`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---

[ ceil(1.5), ceil(2.1), ceil(3) ]
```

#### Output

```JSON
[ 2, 3, 3 ]
```
