# contains

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## contains<T>(@StreamCapable items: Array<T>, element: Any): Boolean

Returns `true` if an input contains a given value, `false` if not.

This version of `contains` accepts an array as input. Other versions accept a string and can use
another string or regular expression to determine whether there is a match.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>items</code></p></td><td><p>The input array.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>elements</code></p></td><td><p>Element to find in the array. Can be any supported data type.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example finds that `2` is in the input array, so it returns `true`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
[ 1, 2, 3, 4 ] contains(2)
```

#### Output

```JSON
true
```

### Example

This example indicates whether the input array contains '"3"'.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
ContainsRequestedItem: payload.root.*order.*items contains "3"
```

#### Input

```XML
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<root>
    <order>
      <items>155</items>
    </order>
    <order>
      <items>30</items>
    </order>
    <order>
      <items>15</items>
    </order>
    <order>
      <items>5</items>
    </order>
    <order>
      <items>4</items>
      <items>7</items>
    </order>
    <order>
      <items>1</items>
      <items>3</items>
    </order>
    <order>
        null
    </order>
</root>
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "ContainsRequestedItem": true }
```

## contains(text: String, toSearch: String): Boolean

Indicates whether a string contains a given substring. Returns `true` or `false`.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>An input string (a <code>String</code>).</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>toSearch</code></p></td><td><p>The substring (a <code>String</code>) to find in the input string.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example finds "mule" in the input string "mulesoft", so it returns `true`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
"mulesoft" contains("mule")
```

#### Output

```JSON
true
```

### Example

This example finds that the substring `"me"` is in `"some string"`, so it returns `true`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
{ ContainsString : payload.root.mystring contains("me") }
```

#### Input

```XML
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<root><mystring>some string</mystring></root>
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "ContainsString": true }
```

## contains(text: String, matcher: Regex): Boolean

Returns `true` if a string contains a match to a regular expression, `false` if not.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>An input string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>matcher</code></p></td><td><p>A Java regular expression for matching characters in the input <code>text</code>.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example checks for any of the letters `e` through `g` in the input `mulesoft`, so it returns
`true`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
contains("mulesoft", /[e-g]/)
```

#### Output

```JSON
true
```

### Example

This example finds a match to `/s[t|p]rin/` within `"A very long string"`, so it returns `true`. The
`[t|p]` in the regex means `t` or `p`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
ContainsString: payload.root.mystring contains /s[t|p]rin/
```

#### Input

```XML
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<root><mystring>A very long string</mystring></root>
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "ContainsString": true }
```

## contains(text: Null, matcher: Any): false

Helper function that enables `contains` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._
---

# daysBetween

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## daysBetween(from: Date, to: Date): Number

Returns the number of days between two dates.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>from</code></p></td><td><p>From date (a <code>Date</code> type).</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>to</code></p></td><td><p>To date (a <code>Date</code> type). Note that if the <code>to</code> date is <em>earlier</em> than the <code>from</code> date, the function returns a negative number equal to the number of days between the two dates.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example returns the number of days between the specified dates.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
{ days : daysBetween('2016-10-01T23:57:59-03:00', '2017-10-01T23:57:59-03:00') }
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "days" : 365 }
```
---

# distinctBy

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## distinctBy<T>(@StreamCapable items: Array<T>, criteria: (item: T, index: Number) -> Any): Array<T>

Iterates over the input and returns the unique elements in it.

DataWeave uses the result of the provided lambda as the uniqueness criteria.

This version of `distinctBy` finds unique values in an array. Other versions act on an object and
handle a `null` value.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>items</code></p></td><td><p>The array to evaluate.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>criteria</code></p></td><td><p>The criteria used to select an <code>item</code> and/or <code>index</code> from the array.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example inputs an array that contains duplicate numbers and returns an array with unique
numbers from that input. Note that you can write the same expression using an anonymous parameter
for the values: `[0, 1, 2, 3, 3, 2, 1, 4] distinctBy $`

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
[0, 1, 2, 3, 3, 2, 1, 4] distinctBy (value) -> { "unique" : value }
```

#### Output

```JSON
[ 0, 1, 2, 3, 4]
```

### Example

This example removes duplicates of `"Kurt Cagle"` from an array.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
var record =  {
  "title": "XQuery Kick Start",
  "author": [
    "James McGovern",
    "Per Bothner",
    "Kurt Cagle",
    "James Linn",
    "Kurt Cagle",
    "Kurt Cagle",
    "Kurt Cagle",
    "Vaidyanathan Nagarajan"
  ],
  "year":"2000"
}
---
{
    "book" : {
      "title" : record.title,
      "year" : record.year,
      "authors" : record.author distinctBy $
    }
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
  "book": {
    "title": "XQuery Kick Start",
    "year": "2000",
    "authors": [
      "James McGovern",
      "Per Bothner",
      "Kurt Cagle",
      "James Linn",
      "Vaidyanathan Nagarajan"
    ]
  }
}
```

## distinctBy<K, V>(object: { (K)?: V }, criteria: (value: V, key: K) -> Any): Object

Removes duplicate key-value pairs from an object.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>object</code></p></td><td><p>The object from which to remove the key-value pairs.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>criteria</code></p></td><td><p>The <code>key</code> and/or <code>value</code> used to identify the key-value pairs to remove.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example inputs an object that contains duplicate key-value pairs and returns an object with
key-value pairs from that input. Notice that the keys (`a` and `A`) are not treated with case
sensitivity, but the values (`b` and `B`) are. Also note that you can write the same expression
using an anonymous parameter for the values: `{a : "b", a : "b", A : "b", a : "B"} distinctBy $`

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
{a : "b", a : "b", A : "b", a : "B"} distinctBy (value) -> { "unique" : value }
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "a": "b", "a": "B" }
```

### Example

This example removes duplicates (`<author>James McGovern</author>`) from `<book/>`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/xml
---
{
   book : {
     title : payload.book.title,
     authors: payload.book.&author distinctBy $
   }
}
```

#### Input

```XML
<book>
  <title> "XQuery Kick Start"</title>
  <author>James Linn</author>
  <author>Per Bothner</author>
  <author>James McGovern</author>
  <author>James McGovern</author>
  <author>James McGovern</author>
</book>
```

#### Output

```XML
<book>
  <title> "XQuery Kick Start"</title>
  <authors>
      <author>James Linn</author>
      <author>Per Bothner</author>
      <author>James McGovern</author>
  </authors>
</book>
```

## distinctBy(@StreamCapable items: Null, criteria: (item: Nothing, index: Nothing) -> Any): Null

Helper function that enables `distinctBy` to work with a `null` value.
---

# endsWith

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## endsWith(text: String, suffix: String): Boolean

Returns `true` if a string ends with a provided substring, `false` if not.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The input string (a <code>String</code>).</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>suffix</code></p></td><td><p>The suffix string to find at the end of the input string.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example finds "no" (but not "to") at the end of "Mariano".

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
[ "Mariano" endsWith "no", "Mariano" endsWith "to" ]
```

#### Output

```JSON
[ true, false ]
```

## endsWith(text: Null, suffix: Any): false

Helper function that enables `endsWith` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

# entriesOf

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## entriesOf<T <: Object>(obj: T): Array<{| key: Key, value: Any, attributes: Object |}>

Returns an array of key-value pairs that describe the key, value, and any attributes in the input
object.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.3.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>obj</code></p></td><td><p>The object to describe.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example returns the key, value, and attributes from the object specified in the variable
`myVar`. The object is the XML input to the `read` function.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
var myVar = read('<xml attr="x"><a>true</a><b>1</b></xml>', 'application/xml')
output application/json
---
{ "entriesOf" : entriesOf(myVar) }
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
  "entriesOf": [
    {
       "key": "xml",
       "value": {
         "a": "true",
         "b": "1"
       },
       "attributes": {
         "attr": "x"
       }
    }
  ]
}
```

## entriesOf(obj: Null): Null

Helper function that enables `entriesOf` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._
---

# evaluateCompatibilityFlag

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## evaluateCompatibilityFlag(flag: String): Boolean

Returns the value of the compatibility flag with the specified name.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.5.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>flag</code></p></td><td><p>String</p></td><td><p>The name of the compatibility flag to evaluate.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example gets the `com.mulesoft.dw.xml_reader.honourMixedContentStructure` compatibility flag
value in the current DataWeave version.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
{
  "com.mulesoft.dw.xml_reader.honourMixedContentStructure": evaluateCompatibilityFlag("com.mulesoft.dw.xml_reader.honourMixedContentStructure")
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "com.mulesoft.dw.xml_reader.honourMixedContentStructure": true
}
```

# filter

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## filter<T>(@StreamCapable items: Array<T>, criteria: (item: T, index: Number) -> Boolean): Array<T>

Iterates over an array and applies an expression that returns matching values.

The expression must return `true` or `false`. If the expression returns `true` for a value or index
in the array, the value gets captured in the output array. If it returns `false` for a value or
index in the array, that item gets filtered out of the output. If there are no matches, the output
array will be empty.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>items</code></p></td><td><p>The array to filter.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>criteria</code></p></td><td><p>Boolean expression that selects an <code>item</code> and/or <code>index</code>.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example returns an array of values in the array that are greater than `2`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
[9,2,3,4,5] filter (value, index) -> (value > 2)
```

#### Output

```JSON
[9,3,4,5]
```

### Example

This example returns an array of all the users with age bigger or equal to 30. The script accesses
data of each element from within the lambda expression.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
---
[{name: "Mariano", age: 37}, {name: "Shoki", age: 30}, {name: "Tomo", age: 25}, {name: "Ana", age: 29}]
          filter ((value, index) -> value.age >= 30)
```

#### Output

```JSON
[
   {
     "name": "Mariano",
     "age": 37
   },
   {
     "name": "Shoki",
     "age": 30
   }
]
```

### Example

This example returns an array of all items found at an index (`$$`) greater than `1` where the value
of the element is less than `5`. Notice that it is using anonymous parameters as selectors instead
of using named parameters in an anonymous function.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
[9, 2, 3, 4, 5] filter (($$ > 1) and ($ < 5))
```

#### Output

```JSON
[3,4]
```

### Example

This example reads a JSON array that contains objects with `user` and `error` keys, and uses the
`filter` function to return only the objects in which the value of the `error` key is `null`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json

var users = [
   {
      "user": {
         "name": "123",
         "lastName": "Smith"
      },
      "error": "That name doesn't exists"
   },
   {
      "user": {
         "name": "John",
         "lastName": "Johnson"
      },
      "error": null
   }
]
---
users filter ((item, index) -> item.error == null)
```

#### Output

```JSON
[
  {
    "user": {
      "name": "John",
      "lastName": "Johnson"
    },
    "error": null
  }
]
```

### Example

This example reads a JSON array and uses the `filter` function to extract the phone numbers that are
active.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
{
    id: payload.Id,
    markCode: payload.marketCode,
    languageCode: payload.languageCode,
    username: payload.profile.base.username,
    phoneNumber: (payload.profile.base.phone filter ($.activeInd == "Y" and $.primaryInd== "Y")).number default []
}
```

#### Input

```JSON
{
  "Id": "1184001100000000517",
  "marketCode": "US",
  "languageCode": "en-US",
  "profile": {
  "base": {
     "username": "TheMule",
     "activeInd": "R",
     "phone": [
       {
          "activeInd": "Y",
          "type": "mobile",
          "primaryInd": "Y",
          "number": "230678123"
       },
       {
         "activeInd": "N",
         "type": "mobile",
         "primaryInd": "N",
         "number": ""
       },
       {
          "activeInd": "Y",
          "type": "mobile",
          "primaryInd": "Y",
          "number": "154896523"
       }
      ]
    }
  }
 }
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
  "id": "1184001100000000517",
  "markCode": "US",
  "languageCode": "en-US",
  "username": "TheMule",
  "phoneNumber": [
    "230678123"
    "154896523"
  ]
}
```

## filter(@StreamCapable text: String, criteria: (character: String, index: Number) -> Boolean): String

Iterates over a string and applies an expression that returns matching values.

The expression must return `true` or `false`. If the expression returns `true` for a character or
index in the array, the character gets captured in the output string. If it returns `false` for a
character or index in the array, that character gets filtered out of the output. If there are no
matches, the output string will be empty.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The text to filter.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>criteria</code></p></td><td><p>The criteria to use.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `filter` can be used to remove all characters in odd positions.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
"hello world" filter ($$ mod 2) == 0
```

#### Output

```Json
"hlowrd"
```

## filter(@StreamCapable value: Null, criteria: (item: Nothing, index: Nothing) -> Any): Null

Helper function that enables `filter` to work with a `null` value.
---

# filterObject

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## filterObject<K, V>(@StreamCapable value: { (K)?: V }, criteria: (value: V, key: K, index: Number) -> Boolean): { (K)?: V }

Iterates a list of key-value pairs in an object and applies an expression that returns only matching
objects, filtering out the rest from the output.

The expression must return `true` or `false`. If the expression returns `true` for a key, value, or
index of an object, the object gets captured in the output. If it returns `false` for any of them,
the object gets filtered out of the output. If there are no matches, the output array will be empty.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>value</code></p></td><td><p>The source object to evaluate.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>criteria</code></p></td><td><p>Boolean expression that selects a <code>value</code>, <code>key</code>, or <code>index</code> of the object.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example outputs an object if its value equals `"apple"`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
{"a" : "apple", "b" : "banana"} filterObject ((value) -> value == "apple")
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "a": "apple" }
```

### Example

This example only outputs an object if the key starts with "letter". The DataWeave `startsWith`
function returns `true` or `false`. Note that you can use the anonymous parameter for the key to
write the expression `((value, key) â key startsWith "letter")`: ($$ startsWith "letter")\`

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
{"letter1": "a", "letter2": "b", "id": 1} filterObject ((value, key) -> key startsWith "letter")
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "letter1": "a", "letter2": "b" }
```

### Example

This example only outputs an object if the index of the object in the array is less than 1, which is
always true of the first object. Note that you can use the anonymous parameter for the index to
write the expression `((value, key, index) â index < 1)`: `($$$ < 1)`

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
{ "1": "a", "2": "b", "3": "c"} filterObject ((value, key, index) -> index < 1)
```

#### Output

```json
{ "1": "a" }
```

### Example

This example outputs an object that contains only the values that are not `null` in the input JSON
object.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
var myObject = {
    str1 : "String 1",
    str2 : "String 2",
    str3 : null,
    str4 : "String 4",
}
---
myObject filterObject $ != null
```

#### Output

```json
{
  "str1": "String 1",
  "str2": "String 2",
  "str4": "String 4"
}
```

## filterObject(value: Null, criteria: (value: Nothing, key: Nothing, index: Nothing) -> Any): Null

Helper function that enables `filterObject` to work with a `null` value.
---

# find

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## find<T>(@StreamCapable() elements: Array<T>, elementToFind: Any): Array<Number>

Returns indices of an input that match a specified value.

This version of the function returns indices of an array. Others return indices of a string.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>elements</code></p></td><td><p>An array with elements of any type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>elementToFind</code></p></td><td><p>Value to find in the input array.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example finds the index of an element in a string array.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
["Bond", "James", "Bond"] find "Bond"
```

#### Output

```JSON
[0,2]
```

## find(@StreamCapable() text: String, matcher: Regex): Array<Array<Number>>

Returns the indices in the text that match the specified regular expression (regex), followed by the
capture groups.

The first element in each resulting sub-array is the index in the text that matches the regex, and
the next ones are the capture groups in the regex (if present).

Note: To retrieve parts of the text that match a regex. use the `scan` function.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>A string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>matcher</code></p></td><td><p>A Java regular expression for matching characters in the <code>text</code>.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example finds the beginning and ending indices of words that contain `ea`

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
"I heart DataWeave" find /\w*ea\w*(\b)/
```

#### Output

```JSON
[ [2,7], [8,17] ]
```

## find(@StreamCapable() text: String, textToFind: String): Array<Number>

Lists indices where the specified characters of a string are present.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>A source string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>textToFind</code></p></td><td><p>The string to find in the source string.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example lists the indices of "a" found in "aabccdbce".

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
"aabccdbce" find "a"
```

#### Output

```JSON
[0,1]
```

## find(@StreamCapable() text: Null, textToFind: Any): Array<Nothing>

Helper function that enables `find` to work with a `null` value.
---

# flatMap

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## flatMap<T, R>(@StreamCapable items: Array<T>, mapper: (item: T, index: Number) -> Array<R>): Array<R>

Iterates over each item in an array and flattens the results.

Instead of returning an array of arrays (as `map` does when you iterate over the values within an
input like `[ [1,2], [3,4] ]`), `flatMap` returns a flattened array that looks like this:
`[1, 2, 3, 4]`. `flatMap` is similar to `flatten`, but `flatten` only acts on the values of the
arrays, while `flatMap` can act on values and indices of items in the array.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>items</code></p></td><td><p>The array to map.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>mapper</code></p></td><td><p>Expression or selector for an <code>item</code> and/or <code>index</code> in the array to flatten.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example returns an array containing each value in order. Though it names the optional `index`
parameter in its anonymous function `(value, index) â value`, it does not use `index` as a selector
for the output, so it is possible to write the anonymous function using `(value) â value`. You can
also use an anonymous parameter for the value to write the example like this:
`[ [3,5], [0.9,5.5] ] flatMap $`. Note that this example produces the same result as
`flatten([ [3,5], [0.9,5.5] ])`, which uses `flatten`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
[ [3,5], [0.9,5.5] ] flatMap (value, index) -> value
```

#### Output

```JSON
[ 3, 5, 0.9, 5.5]
```

## flatMap<T, R>(@StreamCapable value: Null, mapper: (item: Nothing, index: Nothing) -> Any): Null

Helper function that enables `flatMap` to work with a `null` value.
---

# flatten

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## flatten<T, Q>(@StreamCapable items: Array<Array<T> | Q>): Array<T | Q>

Turns a set of subarrays (such as `[ [1,2,3], [4,5,[6]], [], [null] ]`) into a single, flattened
array (such as `[ 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, [6], null ]`).

Note that it flattens only the first level of subarrays and omits empty subarrays.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>items</code></p></td><td><p>The input array of arrays made up of any supported types.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example defines three arrays of numbers, creates another array containing those three arrays,
and then uses the flatten function to convert the array of arrays into a single array with all
values.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
var array1 = [1,2,3]
var array2 = [4,5,6]
var array3 = [7,8,9]
var arrayOfArrays = [array1, array2, array3]
---
flatten(arrayOfArrays)
```

#### Output

```JSON
[ 1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9 ]
```

### Example

This example returns a single array from nested arrays of objects.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
var myData =
{ user : [
   {
     group : "dev",
     myarray : [
       { name : "Shoki", id : 5678 },
       { name : "Mariano", id : 9123 }
     ]
   },
   {
     group : "test",
     myarray : [
       { name : "Sai", id : 2001 },
       { name : "Peter", id : 2002 }
     ]
   }
 ]
}
output application/json
---
flatten(myData.user.myarray)
```

#### Output

```JSON
[
  {
    "name": "Shoki",
    "id": 5678
  },
  {
    "name": "Mariano",
    "id": 9123
  },
  {
    "name": "Sai",
    "id": 2001
  },
  {
    "name": "Peter",
    "id": 2002
  }
]
```

Note that if you use `myData.user.myarray` to select the array of objects in `myarray`, instead of
using `flatten(myData.user.myarray)`, the output is a nested array of objects:

```JSON
[
  [
    {
      "name": "Shoki",
      "id": 5678
    },
    {
      "name": "Mariano",
      "id": 9123
    }
  ]
]
```

## flatten(@StreamCapable value: Null): Null

Helper function that enables `flatten` to work with a `null` value.
---

# floor

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## floor(number: Number): Number

Rounds a number down to the nearest whole number.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>number</code></p></td><td><p>The number to evaluate.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example rounds numbers down to the nearest whole numbers. Notice that `1.5` rounds down to `1`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
[ floor(1.5), floor(2.2), floor(3) ]
```

#### Output

```JSON

```

# groupBy

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## groupBy<T, R>(items: Array<T>, criteria: (item: T, index: Number) -> R): {| (R): Array<T> |}

Returns an object that groups items from an array based on specified criteria, such as an expression
or matching selector.

This version of `groupBy` groups the elements of an array using the `criteria` function. Other
versions act on objects and handle null values.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>items</code></p></td><td><p>The array to group.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>criteria</code></p></td><td><p>Expression providing the criteria by which to group the items in the array.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example groups items from the input array `["a","b","c"]` by their indices. Notice that it
returns the numeric indices as strings and that items (or values) of the array are returned as
arrays, in this case, with a single item each. The items in the array are grouped based on an
anonymous function `(item, index) â index` that uses named parameters (`item` and `index`). Note
that you can produce the same result using the anonymous parameter `$$` to identify the indices of
the array like this: `["a","b","c"] groupBy $$`

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
["a","b","c"] groupBy (item, index) -> index
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "2": [ "c" ], "1": [ "b" ], "0": [ "a" ] }
```

### Example

This example groups the elements of an array based on the language field. Notice that it uses the
`item.language` selector to specify the grouping criteria. So the resulting object uses the
"language" values (`"Scala"` and `"Java"`) from the input to group the output. Also notice that the
output places the each input object in an array.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
var myArray = [
   { "name": "Foo", "language": "Java" },
   { "name": "Bar", "language": "Scala" },
   { "name": "FooBar", "language": "Java" }
]
output application/json
---
myArray groupBy (item) -> item.language
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
  "Scala": [
    { "name": "Bar", "language": "Scala" }
  ],
  "Java": [
    { "name": "Foo", "language": "Java" },
    { "name": "FooBar", "language": "Java" }
  ]
}
```

### Example

This example uses ``groupBy "myLabels"`to return an object where `"mylabels"`` is the key, and an
array of selected values (`["Open New", "Zoom In", "Zoom Out", "Original View" ]`) is the value. It
uses the selectors (`myVar.menu.items.*label`) to create that array. Notice that the selectors
retain all values where `"label"` is the key but filter out values where `"id"` is the key.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
var myVar = { menu: {
    header: "Move Items",
    items: [
        {"id": "internal"},
        {"id": "left", "label": "Move Left"},
        {"id": "right", "label": "Move Right"},
        {"id": "up", "label": "Move Up"},
        {"id": "down", "label": "Move Down"}
    ]
}}
output application/json
---
(myVar.menu.items.*label groupBy "myLabels")
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "myLabels": [ "Move Left", "Move Right", "Move Up", "Move Down" ] }
```

## groupBy<R>(text: String, criteria: (character: String, index: Number) -> R): { (R): String }

Returns an object that groups characters from a string based on specified criteria, such as an
expression or matching selector.

This version of `groupBy` groups the elements of an array using the `criteria` function. Other
versions act on objects and handle `null` values.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The string to group by.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>criteria</code></p></td><td><p>The criteria to use.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows howyou can use `groupBy` to split a string into vowels and not vowels.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
"hello world!" groupBy (not isEmpty($ find /[aeiou]/))
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "false": "hll wrld!",
  "true": "eoo"
}
```

## groupBy<K, V, R>(object: { (K)?: V }, criteria: (value: V, key: K) -> R): { (R): { (K)?: V } }

Groups elements of an object based on criteria that the `groupBy` uses to iterate over elements in
the input.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>object</code></p></td><td><p>The object containing objects to group.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>criteria</code></p></td><td><p>The grouping criteria to apply to elements in the input object, such as a <code>key</code> and/or <code>value</code> of the object to use for grouping.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example groups objects within an array of objects using the anonymous parameter `$` for the
value of each key in the input objects. It applies the DataWeave `upper` function to those values.
In the output, these values become upper-case keys. Note that you can also write the same example
using a named parameter for the within an anonymous function like this:
`{ "a" : "b", "c" : "d"} groupBy (value) â upper(value)`

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
{ "a" : "b", "c" : "d"} groupBy upper($)
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "D": { "c": "d" }, "B": { "a": "b" } }
```

### Example

This example uses `groupBy "costs"` to produce a JSON object from an XML object where `"costs"` is
the key, and the selected values of the XML element `prices` becomes the JSON value
(`{ "price": "9.99", "price": "10.99" }`).

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
var myRead =
read("<prices><price>9.99</price><price>10.99</price></prices>","application/xml")
output application/json
---
myRead.prices groupBy "costs"
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "costs" : { "price": "9.99", "price": "10.99" } }
```

## groupBy(value: Null, criteria: (Nothing, Nothing) -> Any): Null

Helper function that enables `groupBy` to work with a `null` value.
---

# indexOf

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## indexOf(array: Array, value: Any): Number

Returns the index of the _first_ occurrence of the specified element in this array, or `-1` if this
list does not contain the element.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>array</code></p></td><td><p>The array of elements to search.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>value</code></p></td><td><p>The value to search.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `indexOf` behaves given different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
{
  present: ["a","b","c","d"] indexOf "c",
  notPresent: ["x","w","x"] indexOf "c",
  presentMoreThanOnce: ["a","b","c","c"] indexOf "c",
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
   "present": 2,
   "notPresent": -1,
   "presentMoreThanOnce": 2
 }
```

## indexOf(theString: String, search: String): Number

Returns the index of the **first** occurrence of the specified String in this String.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>theString</code></p></td><td><p>The string to search.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>search</code></p></td><td><p>The string to find within <code>theString</code>.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how the `indexOf` behaves under different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
{
  present: "abcd" indexOf "c",
  notPresent: "xyz" indexOf "c",
  presentMoreThanOnce: "abcdc" indexOf "c",
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
   "present": 2,
   "notPresent": -1,
   "presentMoreThanOnce": 2
 }
```

## indexOf(array: Null, value: Any): Number

Helper method to make indexOf null friendly

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

# isBlank

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## isBlank(text: String | Null): Boolean

Returns `true` if the given string is empty (`""`), completely composed of whitespaces, or `null`.
Otherwise, the function returns `false`.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>An input string to evaluate.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example indicates whether the given values are blank. It also uses the `not` and `!` operators
to check that a value is not blank. The `!` operator is supported starting in Dataweave 2.2.0. Use
`!` only in Mule 4.2 and later versions.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output  application/json
var someString = "something"
var nullString = null
---
{
  // checking if the string is blank
  "emptyString" : isBlank(""),
  "stringWithSpaces" : isBlank("      "),
  "textString" : isBlank(someString),
  "somePayloadValue" : isBlank(payload.nonExistingValue),
  "nullString" : isBlank(nullString),

  // checking if the string is not blank
  "notEmptyTextString" : not isBlank(" 1234"),
  "notEmptyTextStringTwo" : ! isBlank("")
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
  "emptyString": true,
  "stringWithSpaces": true,
  "textString": false,
  "somePayloadValue": true,
  "nullString": true,
  "notEmptyTextString": true,
  "notEmptyTextStringTwo": false
}
```
---

# isDecimal

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## isDecimal(number: Number): Boolean

Returns `true` if the given number contains a decimal, `false` if not.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>number</code></p></td><td><p>The number to evaluate.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example indicates whether a number has a decimal. Note that numbers within strings get coerced
to numbers.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
[ isDecimal(1.1), isDecimal(1), isDecimal("1.1") ]
```

#### Output

```JSON
[ true, false, true ]
```
---

# isEmpty

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## isEmpty(elements: Array<Any>): Boolean

Returns `true` if the given input value is empty, `false` if not.

This version of `isEmpty` acts on an array. Other versions act on a string or object, and handle
null values.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>elements</code></p></td><td><p>The input array to evaluate.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example indicates whether the input array is empty.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
[ isEmpty([]), isEmpty([1]) ]
```

#### Output

```JSON
[ true, false ]
```

Returns `true` if the input string is empty, `false` if not.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>value</code></p></td><td><p>A string to evaluate.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

#### Source

#### Output

```JSON
[ true, false ]
```


## isEmpty(value: Binary): Boolean

Returns `true` if the input binary is empty, `false` if not.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.11.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>value</code></p></td><td><p>Binary</p></td><td><p>The input binary value.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## isEmpty(value: Null): true

Returns `true` if the input is `null`.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>value</code></p></td><td><p><code>null</code> is the value in this case.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

# isLeapYear

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## isLeapYear(dateTime: DateTime): Boolean

Returns `true` if it receives a date for a leap year, `false` if not.

This version of `leapYear` acts on a `DateTime` type. Other versions act on the other date and time
formats that DataWeave supports.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>dateTime</code></p></td><td><p>The <code>DateTime</code> value to evaluate.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example indicates whether the input is a leap year.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
[ isLeapYear(|2016-10-01T23:57:59|), isLeapYear(|2017-10-01T23:57:59|) ]
```

#### Output

```JSON
[ true, false ]
```

## isLeapYear(date: Date): Boolean

Returns `true` if the input `Date` is a leap year, 'false' if not.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>date</code></p></td><td><p>The <code>Date</code> value to evaluate.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example indicates whether the input is a leap year.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
[ isLeapYear(|2016-10-01|), isLeapYear(|2017-10-01|) ]
```

#### Output

```JSON
[ true, false ]
```

## isLeapYear(datetime: LocalDateTime): Boolean

Returns `true` if the input local date-time is a leap year, 'false' if not.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>datetime</code></p></td><td><p>A <code>LocalDateTime</code> value to evaluate.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example indicates whether the input is a leap year. It uses a `map` function to iterate through
the array of its `LocalDateTime` values, applies the `isLeapYear` to those values, returning the
results in an array.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
[ |2016-10-01T23:57:59-03:00|, |2016-10-01T23:57:59Z| ] map isLeapYear($)
```

#### Output

```JSON
[ true, true ]
```
---

# joinBy

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## joinBy(@StreamCapable elements: Array<StringCoerceable>, separator: String): String

Merges an array into a single string value and uses the provided string as a separator between each
item in the list.

Note that `joinBy` performs the opposite task of `splitBy`.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>elements</code></p></td><td><p>The input array.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>separator</code></p></td><td><p>A <code>String</code> used to join elements in the list.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example joins the elements with a hyphen (`-`).

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
{ "hyphenate" : ["a","b","c"] joinBy "-" }
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "hyphenate": "a-b-c" }
```

## joinBy(n: Null, separator: Any): Null

Helper function that enables `joinBy` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._
---

# keysOf

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## keysOf<K, V>(obj: { (K)?: V }): Array<K>

Returns an array of keys from key-value pairs within the input object.

The returned keys belong to the Key type. To return each key as a string, you can use `namesOf`,
instead.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.3.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>object</code></p></td><td><p>The object to evaluate.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example returns the keys from the key-value pairs within the input object.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
{ "keysOf" : keysOf({ "a" : true, "b" : 1}) }
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "keysOf" : ["a","b"] }
```

### Example

This example illustrates a difference between `keysOf` and `namesOf`. Notice that `keysOf` retains
the attributes (`name` and `lastName`) and namespaces (`xmlns`) from the XML input, while `namesOf`
returns `null` for them because it does not retain them.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
var myVar = read('<users xmlns="http://test.com">
                     <user name="Mariano" lastName="Achaval"/>
                     <user name="Stacey" lastName="Duke"/>
                  </users>', 'application/xml')
output application/json
---
{ keysOfExample: flatten([keysOf(myVar.users) map $.#,
                          keysOf(myVar.users) map $.@])
}
++
{ namesOfExample: flatten([namesOf(myVar.users) map $.#,
                    namesOf(myVar.users) map $.@])
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
  "keysOfExample": [
    "http://test.com",
    "http://test.com",
    {
      "name": "Mariano",
      "lastName": "Achaval"
    },
    {
      "name": "Stacey",
      "lastName": "Duke"
    }
  ],
  "namesOfExample": [
    null,
    null,
    null,
    null
  ]
}
```

## keysOf(obj: Null): Null

Helper function that enables `keysOf` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._
---

# lastIndexOf

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## lastIndexOf(array: Array, value: Any): Number

Returns the index of the _last_ occurrence of the specified element in a given array or `-1` if the
array does not contain the element.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>array</code></p></td><td><p>The array of elements to search.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>value</code></p></td><td><p>The value to search.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `indexOf` behaves given different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
{
  present: ["a","b","c","d"] lastIndexOf "c",
  notPresent: ["x","w","x"] lastIndexOf "c",
  presentMoreThanOnce: ["a","b","c","c"] lastIndexOf "c",
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "present": 2,
  "notPresent": -1,
  "presentMoreThanOnce": 3
}
```

## lastIndexOf(array: String, value: String): Number

Takes a string as input and returns the index of the _last_ occurrence of a given search string
within the input. The function returns `-1` if the search string is not present in the input.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>string</code></p></td><td><p>The string to search.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>value</code></p></td><td><p>A string value to search for within the input string.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how the `indexOf` behaves given different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
{
  present: "abcd" lastIndexOf "c",
  notPresent: "xyz" lastIndexOf "c",
  presentMoreThanOnce: "abcdc" lastIndexOf "c",
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "present": 2,
  "notPresent": -1,
  "presentMoreThanOnce": 4
}
```

## lastIndexOf(array: Null, value: Any): Number

Helper function that enables `lastIndexOf` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._
---

# log

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## log<T>(prefix: String = "", value: T): T

Without changing the value of the input, `log` returns the input as a system log. So this makes it
very simple to debug your code, because any expression or subexpression can be wrapped with **log**
and the result will be printed out without modifying the result of the expression. The output is
going to be printed in application/dw format.

The prefix parameter is optional and allows to easily find the log output.

Use this function to help with debugging DataWeave scripts. A Mule app outputs the results through
the `DefaultLoggingService`, which you can see in the Studio console.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>prefix</code></p></td><td><p>An optional string that typically describes the log.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>value</code></p></td><td><p>The value to log.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example logs the specified message. Note that the `DefaultLoggingService` in a Mule app that is
running in Studio returns the message `WARNING - "Houston, we have a problem,"` adding the dash `-`
between the prefix and value. The Logger componentâs `LoggerMessageProcessor` returns the input
string `"Houston, we have a problem."`, without the `WARNING` prefix.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
log("WARNING", "Houston, we have a problem")
```

#### Output

`Console Output`

```XML
"WARNING - Houston, we have a problem"
```

`Expression Output`

```XML
"Houston, we have a problem"
```

### Example

This example shows how to log the result of expression `myUser.user` without modifying the original
expression `myUser.user.friend.name`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json

var myUser = {user: {friend: {name: "Shoki"}, id: 1, name: "Tomo"}, accountId: "leansh" }
---
log("User", myUser.user).friend.name
```

#### Output

`Console output`

```console
User - {
  friend: {
    name: "Shoki"
  },
  id: 1,
  name: "Tomo"
}
```

`Expression Output`

```DataWeave
"Shoki"
```
---

# logWith

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## logWith<T>(level: LogLevel, prefix: String, value: T): T

Without changing the value of the input, `logWith` returns the input as a system log at the
specified level. This makes it simple to debug your code, because any expression or subexpression
can be wrapped with **log** and the result is printed out without modifying the result of the
expression. The output is printed in application/dw format.

The prefix parameter is optional and allows finding the log output easily.

Use the `LogLevel` to categorize log events by severity and control the verbosity of the logs.

Use this function to help with debugging DataWeave scripts. A Mule app outputs the results through
the `DefaultLoggingService`, which you can see in the Studio console.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.10.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th><th><code>level</code></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Log level that indicates the severity of the event.</p></td><td><p><code>prefix</code></p></td><td><p>An optional string that typically describes the log.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# lower

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## lower(text: String): String

Returns the provided string in lowercase characters.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The input string.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example converts uppercase characters to lower-case.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
{ "name" : lower("MULESOFT") }
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "name": "mulesoft" }
```

## lower(value: Null): Null

Helper function that enables `lower` to work with a `null` value.

# map

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## map<T, R>(@StreamCapable items: Array<T>, mapper: (item: T, index: Number) -> R): Array<R>

Iterates over items in an array and outputs the results into a new array.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>items</code></p></td><td><p>The array to map.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>mapper</code></p></td><td><p>Expression or selector used to act on each <code>item</code> and optionally, each <code>index</code> of that item.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example iterates over an input array (`["jose", "pedro", "mateo"]`) to produce an array of
DataWeave objects. The anonymous function `(value, index) â {index: value}` maps each item in the
input to an object. As `{index: value}` shows, each index from the input array becomes a key for an
output object, and each value of the input array becomes the value of that object.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
["jose", "pedro", "mateo"] map (value, index) -> { (index) : value}
```

#### Output

```JSON
[ { "0": "jose" }, { "1": "pedro" }, { "2": "mateo" } ]
```

### Example

This example iterates over the input array (`['a', 'b', 'c']`) using an anonymous function that acts
on the items and indices of the input. For each item in the input array, it concatenates the
`index + 1` (`index` plus 1) with an underscore (`_`), and the corresponding `value` to return the
array, `[ "1_a", "2_b", "3_c" ]`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
['a', 'b', 'c'] map ((value, index) -> (index + 1) ++ '_' ++ value)
```

#### Output

```JSON
[ "1_a", "2_b", "3_c" ]
```

### Example

If the parameters of the `mapper` function are not named, the index can be referenced with `$$`, and
the value with `$`. This example iterates over each item in the input array
`['joe', 'pete', 'matt']` and returns an array of objects where the index is selected as the key.
The value of each item in the array is selected as the value of the returned object. Note that the
quotes around `$$` are necessary to convert the numeric keys to strings.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
['joe', 'pete', 'matt'] map ( "$$" : $)
```

#### Output

```JSON
[
  { "0": "joe" },
  { "1": "pete" },
  { "2": "matt" }
]
```

### Example

This example iterates over a list of objects and transform the values into CSV. Each of these
objects represent a CSV row. The `map` operation generates an object with `age` and `address` for
each entry in the list. `$` represents the implicit variable under iteration.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/csv
---
[{
 "age": 14 ,
 "name": "Claire"
}, {
 "age": 56,
 "name": "Max"
}, {
 "age": 89,
 "name": "John"
}] map {
   age: $.age,
   name: $.name
}
```

#### Output

```CSV
age,name
14,Claire
56,Max
89,John
```

## map(@StreamCapable value: Null, mapper: (item: Nothing, index: Nothing) -> Any): Null

Helper function that enables `map` to work with a `null` value.
---

# mapObject

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## mapObject<K, V>(@StreamCapable object: { (K)?: V }, mapper: (value: V, key: K, index: Number) -> Object): Object

Iterates over an object using a mapper that acts on keys, values, or indices of that object.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>object</code></p></td><td><p>The object to map.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>mapper</code></p></td><td><p>Expression or selector that provides the <code>key</code>, <code>value</code>, or <code>index</code> used for mapping the specified object into an output object.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example iterates over the input `{ "a":"b","c":"d"}` and uses the anonymous mapper function
(`(value,key,index) â { (index) : { (value):key} }`) to invert the keys and values in each specified
object and to return the indices of the objects as keys. The mapper uses named parameters to
identify the keys, values, and indices of the input object. Note that you can write the same
expression using anonymous parameters, like this:
`{"a":"b","c":"d"} mapObject { ($$$) : { ($):$$} }`

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
{"a":"b","c":"d"} mapObject (value,key,index) -> { (index) : { (value):key} }
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "0": { "b": "a" }, "1": { "d": "c" } }
```

### Example

This example increases each price by 5 and formats the numbers to always include 2 decimals.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/xml
---
{
    prices: payload.prices mapObject (value, key) -> {
        (key): (value + 5) as Number {format: "##.00"}
    }
}
```

#### Input

```XML
<?xml version='1.0' encoding='UTF-8'?>
<prices>
    <basic>9.99</basic>
    <premium>53</premium>
    <vip>398.99</vip>
</prices>
```

#### Output

```XML
<?xml version='1.0' encoding='UTF-8'?>
<prices>
  <basic>14.99</basic>
  <premium>58.00</premium>
  <vip>403.99</vip>
</prices>
```

## mapObject(value: Null, mapper: (value: Nothing, key: Nothing, index: Nothing) -> Any): Null

Helper function that enables `mapObject` to work with a `null` value.

### Example

Using the previous example, you can test that if the input of the `mapObject` is `null`, the output
result is `null` as well. In XML `null` values are written as empty tags. You can change these
values by using the writer property `writeNilOnNull=true`.

#### Input

```XML
<?xml version='1.0' encoding='UTF-8'?>
<prices>
</prices>
```

#### Output

```XML
<?xml version='1.0' encoding='UTF-8'?>
<prices>
</prices>
```
---

# matches

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## matches(text: String, matcher: Regex): Boolean

Checks if an expression matches the entire input string.

For use cases where you need to output or conditionally process the matched value, see
[Pattern Matching in DataWeave](https://docs.mulesoft.com/dataweave/latest/dataweave-pattern-matching).

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The input string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>matcher</code></p></td><td><p>A Java regular expression for matching characters in the string.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example indicates whether the regular expression matches the input text. Note that you can also
use the `matches(text,matcher)` notation (for example, `matches("admin123", /a.*\d+/)`).

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
[ ("admin123" matches /a.*\d+/), ("admin123" matches /^b.+/) ]
```

#### Output

```JSON
[ true, false ]
```

## matches(text: Null, matcher: Any): false

Helper function that enables `matches` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._
---

# max

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## max<T <: Comparable>(@StreamCapable values: Array<T>): T | Null

Returns the highest `Comparable` value in an array.

The items must be of the same type, or the function throws an error. The function returns `null` if
the array is empty.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>values</code></p></td><td><p>The input array. The elements in the array can be any supported type.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example returns the maximum value of each input array.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
{ a: max([1, 1000]), b: max([1, 2, 3]), c: max([1.5, 2.5, 3.5]) }
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "a": 1000, "b": 3, "c": 3.5 }
```
---

# maxBy

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## maxBy<T>(@StreamCapable array: Array<T>, criteria: (item: T) -> Comparable): T | Null

Iterates over an array and returns the highest value of `Comparable` elements from it.

The items must be of the same type. `maxBy` throws an error if they are not, and the function
returns `null` if the array is empty.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>array</code></p></td><td><p>The input array.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>criteria</code></p></td><td><p>Expression for selecting an item from the array, where the item is a <code>Number</code>, <code>Boolean</code>, <code>DateTime</code>, <code>LocalDateTime</code>, <code>Date</code>, <code>LocalTime</code>, <code>Time</code>, or <code>TimeZone</code> data type. Can be referenced with <code>$</code>.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example: Numeric Values

This example returns the greatest numeric value within objects (key-value pairs) in an array. Notice
that it uses `item.a` to select the value of the object. You can also write the same expression like
this, using an anonymous parameter: `[ { "a" : 1 }, { "a" : 3 }, { "a" : 2 } ] maxBy $.a`

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output  application/json
---
[ { "a" : 1 }, { "a" : 3 }, { "a" : 2 } ] maxBy ((item) -> item.a)
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "a" : 3 }
```

### Example: Date and Time Values

This example gets the latest `DateTime`, `Date`, and `Time` from inputs defined in the variables
`myDateTime1` and `myDateTime2`. It also shows that the function returns null on an empty array.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
var myDateTime1 = |2017-10-01T22:57:59-03:00|
var myDateTime2 = |2018-10-01T23:57:59-03:00|
output application/json
---
{
  myMaxBy: {
    byDateTime: [ myDateTime1, myDateTime2 ] maxBy ((item) -> item),
    byDate: [ myDateTime1 as Date, myDateTime2 as Date ] maxBy ((item) -> item),
    byTime: [ myDateTime1 as Time, myDateTime2 as Time ] maxBy ((item) -> item),
    emptyArray: [] maxBy ((item) -> item)
  }
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
  "myMaxBy": {
    "byDateTime": "2018-10-01T23:57:59-03:00",
    "byDate": "2018-10-01",
    "byTime": "23:57:59-03:00",
    "emptyArray": null
  }
}
```

### Example: String Values

`maxBy` can also compare strings. The following example demonstrates this using simple string
elements.

#### Source

```DataWeave
[ "A", "B", "C" ] maxBy ((item) -> item)
```

#### Output

```JSON
"C"
```
---

# min

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## min<T <: Comparable>(@StreamCapable values: Array<T>): T | Null

Returns the lowest `Comparable` value in an array.

The items must be of the same type or `min` throws an error. The function returns `null` if the
array is empty.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>values</code></p></td><td><p>The input array. The elements in the array can be any supported type.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example returns the lowest numeric value of each input array.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
```

# minBy

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## minBy<T>(@StreamCapable array: Array<T>, criteria: (item: T) -> Comparable): T | Null

Iterates over an array to return the lowest value of comparable elements from it.

The items need to be of the same type. `minBy` returns an error if they are not, and it returns null
when the array is empty.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>item</code></p></td><td><p>Element in the input array (of type <code>Number</code>, <code>Boolean</code>, <code>DateTime</code>, <code>LocalDateTime</code>, <code>Date</code>, <code>LocalTime</code>, <code>Time</code>, or <code>TimeZone</code>). Can be referenced with <code>$</code>.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example returns the lowest numeric value within objects (key-value pairs) in an array. Notice
that it uses `item.a` to select the value of the object. You can also write the same expression like
this, using an anonymous parameter: `[ { "a" : 1 }, { "a" : 3 }, { "a" : 2 } ] minBy $.a`

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output  application/json
---
[ { "a" : 1 }, { "a" : 2 }, { "a" : 3 } ] minBy (item) -> item.a
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "a" : 1 }
```

### Example

This example gets the latest `DateTime`, `Date`, and `Time` from inputs defined in the variables
`myDateTime1` and `myDateTime2`. It also shows that the function returns null on an empty array.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
var myDateTime1 = "2017-10-01T22:57:59-03:00"
var myDateTime2 = "2018-10-01T23:57:59-03:00"
output application/json
---
{
  myMinBy: {
    byDateTime: [ myDateTime1, myDateTime2 ] minBy ((item) -> item),
    byDate: [ myDateTime1 as Date, myDateTime2 as Date ] minBy ((item) -> item),
    byTime: [ myDateTime1 as Time, myDateTime2 as Time ] minBy ((item) -> item),
    aBoolean: [ true, false, (0 > 1), (1 > 0) ] minBy $,
    emptyArray: [] minBy ((item) -> item)
  }
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
  "myMinBy": {
    "byDateTime": "2017-10-01T22:57:59-03:00",
    "byDate": "2017-10-01",
    "byTime": "22:57:59-03:00",
    "aBoolean": false,
    "emptyArray": null
  }
}
```

# \--

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## \--<S>(source: Array<S>, toRemove: Array<Any>): Array<S>

Removes specified values from an input value.

This version of `--` removes all instances of the specified items from an array. Other versions act
on objects, strings, and the various date and time formats that are supported by DataWeave.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>source</p></td><td><p>The array containing items to remove.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>toRemove</p></td><td><p>Items to remove from the source array.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example removes specified items from an array. Specifically, it removes all instances of the
items listed in the array on the right side of `--` from the array on the left side of the function,
leaving `[0]` as the result.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
{ "a" : [0, 1, 1, 2] -- [1,2] }
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "a": [0] }
```

## \--<K, V>(source: { (K)?: V }, toRemove: Object): { (K)?: V }

Removes specified key-value pairs from an object.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>source</code></p></td><td><p>The source object (an <code>Object</code> type).</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>toRemove</code></p></td><td><p>Object that contains the key-value pairs to remove from the source object.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example removes a key-value pair from the source object.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
{ "hello" : "world", "name" : "DW" } -- { "hello" : "world"}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "name": "DW" }
```

## \--(source: Object, keys: Array<String>)

Removes all key-value pairs from the source object that match the specified search key.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>source</code></p></td><td><p>The source object (an <code>Object</code> type).</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>toRemove</code></p></td><td><p>An array of keys to specify the key-value pairs to remove from the source object.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example removes two key-value pairs from the source object.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
{ "yes" : "no", "good" : "bad", "old" : "new" } -- ["yes", "old"]
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "good": "bad" }
```

## \--(source: Object, keys: Array<Key>)

Removes specified key-value pairs from an object.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>source</code></p></td><td><p>The source object (an <code>Object</code> type).</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>keys</code></p></td><td><p>A keys for the key-value pairs to remove from the source object.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example specifies the key-value pair to remove from the source object.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
{ "hello" : "world", "name" : "DW" } -- ["hello" as Key]
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "name": "DW" }
```

## \--(source: Null, keys: Any)

Helper function that enables `--` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._
---

# mod

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## mod(dividend: Number, divisor: Number): Number

Returns the modulo (the remainder after dividing the `dividend` by the `divisor`).

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>dividend</code></p></td><td><p>The number that serves as the dividend for the operation.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>divisor</code></p></td><td><p>The number that serves as the divisor for the operation.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example returns the modulo of the input values. Note that you can also use the
`mod(dividend, divisor)` notation (for example, `mod(3, 2)` to return `1`).

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
[ (3 mod 2), (4 mod 2), (2.2 mod 2) ]
```

#### Output

```JSON
[ 1, 0, 0.2]
```
---

# namesOf

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## namesOf(obj: Object): Array<String>
