# Core Types (dw::Core)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Type</th><th>Definition</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Any</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Any = Any</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The top-level type. <code>Any</code> extends all of the system types, which means that anything can be assigned to a <code>Any</code> typed variable.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Array</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Array = Array</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Array type that requires a <code>Type(T)</code> to represent the elements of the list. Example: <code>Array&lt;Number&gt;</code> represents an array of numbers, and <code>Array&lt;Any&gt;</code> represents an array of any type.</p></div><div><p>Example: <code>[1, 2, "a", "b", true, false, { a : "b"}, [1, 2, 3] ]</code></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Binary</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Binary = Binary</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A blob.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Boolean</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Boolean = Boolean</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A <code>Boolean</code> type of <code>true</code> or <code>false</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>CData</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type CData = String {cdata: true}</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>XML defines a <code>CData</code> custom type that extends from <code>String</code> and is used to identify a CDATA XML block.</p></div><div><p>It can be used to tell the writer to wrap the content inside CDATA or to check if the string arrives inside a CDATA block. <code>CData</code> inherits from the type <code>String</code>.</p></div><div><p><strong>Source</strong>:</p></div><div><p><code>output application/xml --- { "user" : "Shoki" as CData }</code></p></div><div><p><strong>Output</strong>:</p></div><div><p><code>&lt;?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?&gt;&lt;user&gt;&lt;![CDATA[Shoki]]&gt;&lt;/user&gt;</code></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Comparable</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Comparable = String | Number | Boolean | DateTime | LocalDateTime | Date | LocalTime | Time | TimeZone</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A union type that represents all the types that can be compared to each other.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Date</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Date = Date</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A date represented by a year, month, and day. For example: <code>|2018-09-17|</code></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>DateTime</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type DateTime = DateTime</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A <code>Date</code> and <code>Time</code> within a <code>TimeZone</code>. For example: <code>|2018-09-17T22:13:00Z|</code></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Dictionary</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Dictionary = { _?: T }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Generic dictionary interface.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Enum</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Enum = String {enumeration: true}</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>This type is based on the <a href="https://docs.oracle.com/javase/7/docs/api/java/lang/Enum.html">Enum Java class</a>.</p></div><div><p>It must always be used with the <code>class</code> property, specifying the full Java class name of the class, as shown in the example below.</p></div><div><p><strong>Source:</strong></p></div><div><p><code>"Max" as Enum {class: "com.acme.MuleyEnum"}</code></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Iterator</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Iterator = Array {iterator: true}</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>This type is based on the <a href="https://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/api/java/util/Iterator.html">iterator Java class</a>. The iterator contains a collection and includes methods to iterate through and filter it.</p></div><div><p>Just like the Java class, <code>Iterator</code> is designed to be consumed only once. For example, if you pass it to a <a href="https://docs.mulesoft.com/mule-runtime/latest/logger-component-reference">Logger component</a>, the Logger consumes it, so it becomes unreadable by further elements in the flow.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Key</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Key = Key</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A key of an <code>Object</code>.</p></div><div><p>Examples: <code>{ myKey : "a value" }</code>, <code>{ myKey : { a : 1, b : 2} }</code>, <code>{ myKey : [1,2,3,4] }</code></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>LocalDateTime</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type LocalDateTime = LocalDateTime</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A <code>DateTime</code> in the current <code>TimeZone</code>. For example: <code>|2018-09-17T22:13:00|</code></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>LocalTime</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type LocalTime = LocalTime</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A <code>Time</code> in the current <code>TimeZone</code>. For example: <code>|22:10:18|</code></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>LogLevel</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type LogLevel = "Debug" | "Info" | "Warn" | "Error"</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A type that represents the severity level of logging operations. For example: <code>Debug</code>, <code>Info</code>, <code>Warn</code>, <code>Error</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>NaN</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type NaN = Null {NaN: true}</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>java.lang.Float</code> and <code>java.lang.Double</code> have special cases for <code>NaN</code> and <code>Infinit</code>. DataWeave does not have these concepts for its number multi-precision nature. So when it is mapped to DataWeave values, it is wrapped in a Null with a Schema marker.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Namespace</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Namespace = Namespace</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A <code>Namespace</code> type represented by a <code>URI</code> and a prefix.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Nothing</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Nothing = Nothing</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Bottom type. This type can be assigned to all the types.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Null</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Null = Null</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A Null type, which represents the <code>null</code> value.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Number</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Number = Number</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A number type: Any number, decimal, or integer is represented by the Number` type.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Object</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Object = Object</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Type that represents any object, which is a collection of <code>Key</code> and value pairs.</p></div><div><p>Examples: <code>{ myKey : "a value" }</code>, <code>{ myKey : { a : 1, b : 2} }</code>, <code>{ myKey : [1,2,3,4] }</code></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Pair</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Pair = { l: LEFT, r: RIGHT }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A type used to represent a pair of values.</p></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0.</em></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Period</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Period = Period</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A period.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Range</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Range = Range</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A <code>Range</code> type represents a sequence of numbers.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Regex</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Regex = Regex</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A Java regular expression (regex) type.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Result</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Result = { success: true, result: T } | { success: false, error: E }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A type for representing an execution result.</p></div><div><p>Supports the following fields:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>success</code>: Determine if the execution ends successfully. If <code>true</code>, the data type provides the <code>result</code>. If <code>false</code>, the data type provides the <code>error</code>.</p></li><li><p><code>result</code>: The success result data.</p></li><li><p><code>error</code>: The error data.</p></li></ul></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.7.0.</em></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>SimpleType</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type SimpleType = String | Boolean | Number | DateTime | LocalDateTime | Date | LocalTime | Time | TimeZone | Period</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A union type that represents all the simple types.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>String</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type String = String</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>String</code> type</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>StringCoerceable</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type StringCoerceable = String | Boolean | Number | DateTime | LocalDateTime | Date | LocalTime | Time | TimeZone | Period | Key | Binary | Uri | Type&lt;Any&gt; | Regex | Namespace</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A union type of all the types that can be coerced to String type.</p></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.3.0.</em></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Time</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Time = Time</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A time in a specific <code>TimeZone</code>. For example: <code>|22:10:18Z|</code></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>TimeZone</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type TimeZone = TimeZone</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A time zone.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Type</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Type = Type</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A type in the DataWeave type system.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Uri</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Uri = Uri</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A URI.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# Core (dw::Core)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

This module contains core DataWeave functions for data transformations. It is automatically imported
into any DataWeave script.

## Functions

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-plusplus">++</a></p></td><td><p>Concatenates two values.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-minusminus">--</a></p></td><td><p>Removes specified values from an input value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-abs">abs</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the absolute value of a number.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-avg">avg</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the average of numbers listed in an array.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-ceil">ceil</a></p></td><td><p>Rounds a number up to the nearest whole number.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-contains">contains</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if an input contains a given value, <code>false</code> if not.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-daysbetween">daysBetween</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the number of days between two dates.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-distinctby">distinctBy</a></p></td><td><p>Iterates over the input and returns the unique elements in it.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-endswith">endsWith</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if a string ends with a provided substring, <code>false</code> if not.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-entriesof">entriesOf</a></p></td><td><p>Returns an array of key-value pairs that describe the key, value, and any attributes in the input object.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-evaluatecompatibilityflag">evaluateCompatibilityFlag</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the value of the compatibility flag with the specified name.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-filter">filter</a></p></td><td><p>Iterates over an array and applies an expression that returns matching values.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-filterobject">filterObject</a></p></td><td><p>Iterates a list of key-value pairs in an object and applies an expression that returns only matching objects, filtering out the rest from the output.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-find">find</a></p></td><td><p>Returns indices of an input that match a specified value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-flatmap">flatMap</a></p></td><td><p>Iterates over each item in an array and flattens the results.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-flatten">flatten</a></p></td><td><p>Turns a set of subarrays (such as <code>[ [1,2,3], [4,5,[6]], [], [null] ]</code>) into a single, flattened array (such as <code>[ 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, [6], null ]</code>).</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-floor">floor</a></p></td><td><p>Rounds a number down to the nearest whole number.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-groupby">groupBy</a></p></td><td><p>Returns an object that groups items from an array based on specified criteria, such as an expression or matching selector.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-indexof">indexOf</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the index of the <em>first</em> occurrence of the specified element in this array, or <code>-1</code> if this list does not contain the element.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-isblank">isBlank</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if the given string is empty (<code>""</code>), completely composed of whitespaces, or <code>null</code>. Otherwise, the function returns <code>false</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-isdecimal">isDecimal</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if the given number contains a decimal, <code>false</code> if not.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-isempty">isEmpty</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if the given input value is empty, <code>false</code> if not.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-iseven">isEven</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if the number or numeric result of a mathematical operation is even, <code>false</code> if not.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-isinteger">isInteger</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if the given number is an integer (which lacks decimals), <code>false</code> if not.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-isleapyear">isLeapYear</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if it receives a date for a leap year, <code>false</code> if not.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-isodd">isOdd</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if the number or numeric result of a mathematical operation is odd, <code>false</code> if not.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-joinby">joinBy</a></p></td><td><p>Merges an array into a single string value and uses the provided string as a separator between each item in the list.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-keysof">keysOf</a></p></td><td><p>Returns an array of keys from key-value pairs within the input object.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-lastindexof">lastIndexOf</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the index of the <em>last</em> occurrence of the specified element in a given array or <code>-1</code> if the array does not contain the element.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-log">log</a></p></td><td><p>Without changing the value of the input, <code>log</code> returns the input as a system log. So this makes it very simple to debug your code, because any expression or subexpression can be wrapped with <strong>log</strong> and the result will be printed out without modifying the result of the expression. The output is going to be printed in application/dw format.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-logdebug">logDebug</a></p></td><td><p>Helper function that logs messages at <code>Debug</code> level.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-logerror">logError</a></p></td><td><p>Helper function that logs messages at <code>Error</code> level.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-loginfo">logInfo</a></p></td><td><p>Helper function that logs messages at <code>Info</code> level.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-logwarn">logWarn</a></p></td><td><p>Helper function that logs messages at <code>Warn</code> level.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-logwith">logWith</a></p></td><td><p>Without changing the value of the input, <code>logWith</code> returns the input as a system log at the specified level. This makes it simple to debug your code, because any expression or subexpression can be wrapped with <strong>log</strong> and the result is printed out without modifying the result of the expression. The output is printed in application/dw format.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-lower">lower</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the provided string in lowercase characters.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-map">map</a></p></td><td><p>Iterates over items in an array and outputs the results into a new array.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-mapobject">mapObject</a></p></td><td><p>Iterates over an object using a mapper that acts on keys, values, or indices of that object.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-match">match</a></p></td><td><p>Uses a Java regular expression (regex) to match a string and then separates it into capture groups. Returns the results in an array.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-matches">matches</a></p></td><td><p>Checks if an expression matches the entire input string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-max">max</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the highest <code>Comparable</code> value in an array.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-maxby">maxBy</a></p></td><td><p>Iterates over an array and returns the highest value of <code>Comparable</code> elements from it.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-min">min</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the lowest <code>Comparable</code> value in an array.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-minby">minBy</a></p></td><td><p>Iterates over an array to return the lowest value of comparable elements from it.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-mod">mod</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the modulo (the remainder after dividing the <code>dividend</code> by the <code>divisor</code>).</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-namesof">namesOf</a></p></td><td><p>Returns an array of strings with the names of all the keys within the given object.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-now">now</a></p></td><td><p>Returns a <code>DateTime</code> value for the current date and time.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-onnull">onNull</a></p></td><td><p>Executes a callback function if the preceding expression returns a <code>null</code> value and then replaces the <code>null</code> value with the result of the callback.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-orderby">orderBy</a></p></td><td><p>Reorders the elements of an input using criteria that acts on selected elements of that input.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-pluck">pluck</a></p></td><td><p>Useful for mapping an object into an array, <code>pluck</code> iterates over an object and returns an array of keys, values, or indices from the object.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-pow">pow</a></p></td><td><p>Raises the value of a <code>base</code> number to the specified <code>power</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-random">random</a></p></td><td><p>Returns a pseudo-random number greater than or equal to <code>0.0</code> and less than <code>1.0</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-randomint">randomInt</a></p></td><td><p>Returns a pseudo-random whole number from <code>0</code> to the specified number (exclusive).</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-read">read</a></p></td><td><p>Reads a string or binary and returns parsed content.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-readurl">readUrl</a></p></td><td><p>Reads a URL, including a classpath-based URL, and returns parsed content. This function works similar to the <code>read</code> function.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-reduce">reduce</a></p></td><td><p>Applies a reduction expression to the elements in an array.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-replace">replace</a></p></td><td><p>Performs string replacement.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-round">round</a></p></td><td><p>Rounds a number up or down to the nearest whole number.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-scan">scan</a></p></td><td><p>Returns an array with all of the matches found in an input string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-sizeof">sizeOf</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the number of elements in an array. It returns <code>0</code> if the array is empty.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-splitby">splitBy</a></p></td><td><p>Splits a string into a string array based on a value that matches part of that string. It filters out the matching part from the returned array.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-sqrt">sqrt</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the square root of a number.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-startswith">startsWith</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> or <code>false</code> depending on whether the input string starts with a matching prefix.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-sum">sum</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the sum of numeric values in an array.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-then">then</a></p></td><td><p>This function works as a pipe that passes the value returned from the preceding expression to the next (a callback) only if the value returned by the preceding expression is not <code>null</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-to">to</a></p></td><td><p>Returns a range with the specified boundaries.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-trim">trim</a></p></td><td><p>Removes any blank spaces from the beginning and end of a string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-typeof">typeOf</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the primitive data type of a value, such as <code>String</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-unzip">unzip</a></p></td><td><p>Performs the opposite of <code>zip</code>. It takes an array of arrays as input.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-upper">upper</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the provided string in uppercase characters.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-uuid">uuid</a></p></td><td><p>Returns a v4 UUID using random numbers as the source.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-valuesof">valuesOf</a></p></td><td><p>Returns an array of the values from key-value pairs in an object.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-with">with</a></p></td><td><p>Helper function that specifies a replacement element. This function is used with <code>replace</code>, <code>update</code> or <code>mask</code> to perform data substitutions.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-write">write</a></p></td><td><p>Writes a value as a string or binary in a supported format.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-xsitype">xsiType</a></p></td><td><p>Creates a <code>xsi:type</code> type attribute. This method returns an object, so it must be used with dynamic attributes.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-core-functions-zip">zip</a></p></td><td><p>Merges elements from two arrays into an array of arrays.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

# Crypto Annotations (dw::Crypto)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Parameters annotated with `@CryptographicSink` are subject to taint analysis during type checking.
When a literal algorithm value is passed to a parameter marked with this annotation, the type
checker validates whether the algorithm is permitted. If the algorithm matches a forbidden or
insecure algorithm (such as MD5 or SHA-1 in FIPS-compliant environments), a compilation error is
raised.

This annotation can only be applied to function parameters.

## Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Annotation</th><th>Definition</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>CryptographicSink</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>@CryptographicSink()</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Marks a function parameter as a cryptographic algorithm sink for security validation.</p></div><div><h2 id="error"><a href="#error"></a>Error</h2><div><div><p>When an insecure algorithm is detected, DataWeave produces this error:</p></div><div><div><pre>Cryptographic violation: Forbidden use of algorithm &lt;algorithm&gt; in &lt;module&gt;.</pre></div></div><div><h3 id="example"><a href="#example"></a>Example</h3><div><p>This function declares an algorithm parameter as a cryptographic sink.</p></div><div><h4 id="source"><a href="#source"></a>Source</h4><div><div><pre><code data-lang="DataWeave">fun hashWith(content: Binary, @CryptographicSink algorithm: String = "SHA-1"): Binary = native("system::HashFunctionValue")</code></pre></div></div><div><p>When called with a forbidden algorithm in a security-restricted environment, DataWeave produces this error. For example, this error occurs when MD5 is configured as a forbidden algorithm.</p></div><div><div><pre><code data-lang="DataWeave">hashWith("data" as Binary, "MD5")</code></pre></div></div></div></div></div></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# hashWith

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## hashWith(content: Binary, @CryptographicSink algorithm: String = "SHA-1"): Binary

Computes the hash value of binary content using a specified algorithm.

The first argument specifies the binary content to use to calculate the hash value, and the second
argument specifies the hashing algorithm to use. The second argument must be any of the accepted
Algorithm names:

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Algorithm names</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>MD2</code></p></td><td><p>The MD2 message digest algorithm as defined in <a href="https://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc1319.txt">RFC 1319</a>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>MD5</code></p></td><td><p>The MD5 message digest algorithm as defined in <a href="https://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc1321.txt">RFC 1321</a>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>SHA-1</code>, <code>SHA-256</code>, <code>SHA-384</code>, <code>SHA-512</code></p></td><td><p>Hash algorithms defined in the <a href="https://csrc.nist.gov/publications/fips">FIPS PUB 180-2</a>. SHA-256 is a 256-bit hash function intended to provide 128 bits of security against collision attacks, while SHA-512 is a 512-bit hash function intended to provide 256 bits of security. A 384-bit hash may be obtained by truncating the SHA-512 output.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>content</code></p></td><td><p>The binary input value to hash.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>algorithm</code></p></td><td><p>The name of the algorithm to use for calculating the hash value of <code>content</code>. This value is a string. Defaults to <code>SHA-1</code>.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example uses the MD2 algorithm to encrypt a binary value.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import dw::Crypto
output application/json
---
{ "md2" : Crypto::hashWith("hello" as Binary, "MD2") }
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "md2": "\ufffd\u0004ls\ufffd\u00031\ufffdh\ufffd}8\u0004\ufffd\u0006U" }
```
---

# HMACBinary

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## HMACBinary(secret: Binary, content: Binary, @CryptographicSink algorithm: String = "HmacSHA1"): Binary

Computes an HMAC hash (with a secret cryptographic key) on input content.

See also, `HMACWith`.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>secret</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>The secret cryptographic key (a binary value) used when encrypting the <code>content</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>content</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>The binary input value.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>algorithm</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>The hashing algorithm. <code>HmacSHA1</code> is the default. Valid values depend on the JDK version you are using. For JDK 8 and JDK 11, <code>HmacMD5</code>, <code>HmacSHA1</code>, <code>HmacSHA224</code>, <code>HmacSHA256</code>, <code>HmacSHA384</code>, and <code>HmacSHA512</code> are valid algorithms. For JDK 11, <code>HmacSHA512/224</code> and <code>HmacSHA512/256</code> are also valid.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example uses HMAC with a secret value to encrypt the input content.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import dw::Crypto
output application/json
---
{
  "HMACBinary" : Crypto::HMACBinary("confidential" as Binary, "xxxxx" as Binary, "HmacSHA512")
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
  "HMACBinary": "\ufffd\ufffd\ufffd\ufffd^h\ufffd!3\u0005\ufffdÖ\u00017\ufffd\ufffd\ufffd`\ufffd8?\ufffdjn7\ufffdbs;\t\ufffdÆ\ufffd\ufffd\ufffdx&g\ufffd~\ufffd\ufffd%\ufffd7>1\ufffdK\u000e@\ufffdC\u0011\ufffdT\ufffd}W"
}
```
---

# HMACWith

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## HMACWith(secret: Binary, content: Binary, @Since(version = "2.2.0") algorithm: String = "HmacSHA1"): String

Computes an HMAC hash (with a secret cryptographic key) on input content, then transforms the result
into a lowercase, hexadecimal string.

See also, `HMACBinary`.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>secret</code></p></td><td><p>The secret cryptographic key (a binary value) used when encrypting the <code>content</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>content</code></p></td><td><p>The binary input value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>algorithm</code></p></td><td><p>(<em>Introduced in DataWeave 2.2.0. Supported by Mule 4.2 and later.</em>) The hashing algorithm. By default, <code>HmacSHA1</code> is used. Other valid values are <code>HmacSHA256</code> and <code>HmacSHA512</code>.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example uses HMAC with a secret value to encrypt the input content using the `HmacSHA256`
algorithm.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import dw::Crypto
output application/json
---
{ "HMACWith" : Crypto::HMACWith("secret_key" as Binary, "Some value to hash" as Binary, "HmacSHA256") }
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "HMACWith": "b51b4fe8c4e37304605753272b5b4321f9644a9b09cb1179d7016c25041d1747" }
```
---

# MD5

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## MD5(content: Binary): String

Computes the MD5 hash and transforms the binary result into a hexadecimal lower case string.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>content</code></p></td><td><p>A binary input value to encrypt.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

This example uses the MD5 algorithm to encrypt a binary value.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0

output application/json

```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "md5": "7815696ecbf1c96e6894b779456d330e" }
```

# SHA1

## SHA1(content: Binary): String

Computes the SHA1 hash and transforms the result into a hexadecimal, lowercase string.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>content</code></p></td><td><p>A binary input value to encrypt.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

# Crypto (dw::Crypto)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

This module provide functions that perform encryptions through common algorithms, such as MD5, SHA1,
and so on.

To use this module, you must import it to your DataWeave code, for example, by adding the line
`import * from dw::Crypto` to the header of your DataWeave script.

## Functions

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><a href="dw-crypto-functions-hmacbinary">HMACBinary</a></p></td><td><p>Computes an HMAC hash (with a secret cryptographic key) on input content.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-crypto-functions-hmacwith">HMACWith</a></p></td><td><p>Computes an HMAC hash (with a secret cryptographic key) on input content, then transforms the result into a lowercase, hexadecimal string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-crypto-functions-md5">MD5</a></p></td><td><p>Computes the MD5 hash and transforms the binary result into a hexadecimal lower case string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-crypto-functions-sha1">SHA1</a></p></td><td><p>Computes the SHA1 hash and transforms the result into a hexadecimal, lowercase string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-crypto-functions-hashwith">hashWith</a></p></td><td><p>Computes the hash value of binary content using a specified algorithm.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Annotations

- [Crypto Annotations](dw-crypto-annotations)
---

For an example, see the <a href="https://github.com/mulesoft-labs/data-weave-custom-data-format/blob/master/README.md">Custom Data Formats Example README</a>.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

# DataFormat Types (dw::extension::DataFormat)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Type</th><th>Definition</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>DataFormat</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type DataFormat = { binaryFormat?: Boolean, defaultCharset?: String, fileExtensions?: Array&lt;String&gt;, acceptedMimeTypes: Array&lt;MimeType&gt;, reader: (content: Binary, charset: String, settings: ReaderSettings) -&gt; Any, writer: (value: Any, settings: WriterSettings) -&gt; Binary }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Represents the <code>DataFormat</code> definition and contains the following fields:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>binaryFormat</code>: True if this is data format is represented as binary representation instead of text. False if not present.</p></li><li><p><code>defaultCharset</code>: Default character set of this format, if any.</p></li><li><p><code>fileExtensions</code>: Returns the list of file extensions with the <code>.</code> (for example, <code>.json</code>, <code>.xml</code>) to assign to this data format.</p></li><li><p><code>acceptedMimeTypes</code>: The list of MIME types to accept.</p></li><li><p><code>reader</code>: Function that reads raw content and transforms it into the canonical DataWeave model.</p></li><li><p><code>writer</code>: Function that writes the canonical DataWeave model into binary content.</p></li></ul></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0.</em></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>EmptySettings</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type EmptySettings = Object</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Represents a configuration with no settings.</p></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0.</em></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>EncodingSettings</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type EncodingSettings = { encoding?: String {defaultValue: "UTF-8"} }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Represents encoding settings and contains the following field:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>encoding</code>: Encoding that the writer uses for output. Defaults to "UTF-8".</p></li></ul></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0.</em></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>MimeType</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type MimeType = String</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Represents a MIME type, such as <code>application/json</code>.</p></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0.</em></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Settings</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Settings = Object</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Reader or writer configuration settings.</p></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0.</em></p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# DataFormat (dw::extension::DataFormat)

[Custom Data Formats Example](https://github.com/mulesoft-labs/data-weave-custom-data-format).

# atBeginningOfDay

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## atBeginningOfDay(dateTime: DateTime): DateTime

Returns a new `DateTime` value that changes the `Time` value in the input to the beginning of the
specified _day_.

The hours, minutes, and seconds in the input change to `00:00:00`.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>dateTime</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>The <code>DateTime</code> value to reference.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example changes the `Time` value within the `DateTime` input to the beginning of the specified
day.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Dates
output application/json
---
{
  "atBeginningOfDayDateTime": atBeginningOfDay(|2020-10-06T18:23:20.351-03:00|)
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "atBeginningOfDayDateTime": "2020-10-06T00:00:00-03:00"
}
```

## atBeginningOfDay(localDateTime: LocalDateTime): LocalDateTime

Returns a new `LocalDateTime` value that changes the `Time` value within the input to the start of
the specified _day_.

The hours, minutes, and seconds in the input change to `00:00:00`.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>localDateTime</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>The <code>LocalDateTime</code> value to reference.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example changes the `Time` value within the `LocalDateTime` input to the beginning of the
specified day.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Dates
output application/json
---
{
  "atBeginningOfDayLocalDateTime": atBeginningOfDay(|2020-10-06T18:23:20.351|)
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "atBeginningOfDayLocalDateTime": "2020-10-06T00:00:00"
}
```
---

## atBeginningOfHour(localTime: LocalTime): LocalTime

The minutes and seconds in the input change to `00:00`.

```DataWeave
}
```

```Json
{
 "atBeginningOfHourTime":  "18:00:00-03:00"
}
```

# atBeginningOfMonth

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## atBeginningOfMonth(dateTime: DateTime): DateTime

Returns a new `DateTime` value that changes the `Day` value from the input to the first day of the
specified _month_. It also sets the `Time` value to `00:00:00`.

The day and time in the input changes to `01T00:00:00`.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>dateTime</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>The <code>DateTime</code> value to reference.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example changes the `Day` value within the `DateTime` input to the first day of the specified
month and sets the `Time` value to `00:00:00`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Dates
output application/json
---
{
  "atBeginningOfMonthDateTime": atBeginningOfMonth(|2020-10-06T18:23:20.351-03:00|)
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "atBeginningOfMonthDateTime": "2020-10-01T00:00:00-03:00"
}
```

## atBeginningOfMonth(localDateTime: LocalDateTime): LocalDateTime

Returns a new `LocalDateTime` value that changes the `Day` and `LocalTime` values from the input to
the beginning of the specified _month_.

The day and time in the input changes to `01T00:00:00`.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>localDateTime</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>The <code>LocalDateTime</code> value to reference.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example changes the `Day` and `LocalTime` values within the `LocalDateTime` input to the
beginning of the specified month.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Dates
output application/json
---
{
 "atBeginningOfMonthLocalDateTime": atBeginningOfMonth(|2020-10-06T18:23:20.351|)
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "atBeginningOfMonthLocalDateTime": "2020-10-01T00:00:00"
}
```

## atBeginningOfMonth(date: Date): Date

Returns a new `Date` value that changes the `Day` value from the input to the first day of the
specified _month_.

The day in the input changes to `01`.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>date</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>The <code>Date</code> value to reference.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example changes the `Day` value within the `Date` input to the first day of the specified
month.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Dates
output application/json
---
{
  atBeginningOfMonthDate: atBeginningOfMonth(|2020-10-06|)
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "atBeginningOfMonthDate": "2020-10-01"
}
```
---

# atBeginningOfWeek

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## atBeginningOfWeek(dateTime: DateTime): DateTime

Returns a new `DateTime` value that changes the `Day` and `Time` values from the input to the
beginning of the first day of the specified _week_.

The function treats Sunday as the first day of the week.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>dateTime</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>The <code>DateTime</code> value to reference.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example changes the `Day` and `Time` values (`06T18:23:20.351`) within the `DateTime` input to
the beginning of the first day of the specified _week_ (`04T00:00:00`).

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Dates
output application/json
---
{
  atBeginningOfWeekDateTime: atBeginningOfWeek(|2020-10-06T18:23:20.351-03:00|)
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "atBeginningOfWeekDateTime": "2020-10-04T00:00:00-03:00"
}
```

## atBeginningOfWeek(localDateTime: LocalDateTime): LocalDateTime

Returns a new `LocalDateTime` value that changes the `Day` and `Time` values from the input to the
beginning of the first day of the specified _week_.

The function treats Sunday as the first day of the week.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>localDateTime</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>The <code>LocalDateTime</code> value to reference.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example changes the `Day` and `Time` values (`06T18:23:20.351`) within the `LocalDateTime`
input to the beginning of the first day of the specified _week_ (`04T00:00:00`).

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Dates
output application/json
---
{
  atBeginningOfWeekLocalDateTime: atBeginningOfWeek(|2020-10-06T18:23:20.351|)
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "atBeginningOfWeekLocalDateTime": "2020-10-04T00:00:00"
}
```

## atBeginningOfWeek(date: Date): Date

Returns a new `Date` value that changes the `Date` input input to the first day of the specified
_week_.

The function treats Sunday as the first day of the week.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>date</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>The <code>Date</code> value to reference.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example changes the `Day` value (`06`) within the `Date` input to the first day of the week
that contains `2020-10-06` (a Tuesday), which is `2020-10-04` (a Sunday). The `Day` value changes
from `06` to `04`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Dates
output application/json
---
{
  atBeginningOfWeekDate: atBeginningOfWeek(|2020-10-06|)
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "atBeginningOfWeekDate": "2020-10-04"
}
```

# atBeginningOfYear

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## atBeginningOfYear(dateTime: DateTime): DateTime

Takes a `DateTime` value as input and returns a `DateTime` value for the first day of the _year_
specified in the input. It also sets the `Time` value to `00:00:00`.

The month, day, and time in the input changes to `01-01T00:00:00`.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>dateTime</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>The <code>DateTime</code> value to reference.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example transforms the `DateTime` input (`|2020-10-06T18:23:20.351-03:00|`) to the date of the
first day of the `Year` value (`2020`) in the input.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Dates
output application/json
---
{
  atBeginningOfYearDateTime: atBeginningOfYear(|2020-10-06T18:23:20.351-03:00|)
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "atBeginningOfYearDateTime": "2020-01-01T00:00:00.000-03:00"
}
```

## atBeginningOfYear(localDateTime: LocalDateTime): LocalDateTime

Takes a `LocalDateTime` value as input and returns a `LocalDateTime` value for the first day of the
_year_ specified in the input. It also sets the `Time` value to `00:00:00`.

The month, day, and time in the input changes to `01-01T00:00:00`.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>localDateTime</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>The <code>LocalDateTime</code> value to reference.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example transforms the `LocalDateTime` input (`|2020-10-06T18:23:20.351|`) to the date of the
first day of the `Year` value (`2020`) in the input.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Dates
output application/json
---
{
  atBeginningOfYearLocalDateTime: atBeginningOfYear(|2020-10-06T18:23:20.351|)
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "atBeginningOfYearLocalDateTime": "2020-01-01T00:00:00"
}
```

## atBeginningOfYear(date: Date): Date

Takes a `Date` value as input and returns a `Date` value for the first day of the _year_ specified
in the input.

The month and day in the input changes to `01-01`.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>date</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>The <code>Date</code> value to reference.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example transforms `Date` input (`|2020-10-06|`) to the date of the first day of the `Year`
value (`2020`) in the input.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Dates
output application/json
---
{
  atBeginningOfYearDate: atBeginningOfYear(|2020-10-06|)
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "atBeginningOfYearDate": "2020-01-01"
}
```

# date

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## date(parts: DateFactory): Date

Creates a `Date` value from values specified for `year`, `month`, and `day` fields.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>parts</code></p></td><td><div><div><p><code>Number</code> values for <code>year</code>, <code>month</code>, and <code>day</code> fields. The <code>month</code> must be a value between 1 and 12, and the <code>day</code> value must be between 1 and 31. You can specify the name-value pairs in any order, but the output is ordered by default as a Date value, such as <code>2012-10-11</code>. The input fields are parts of a <code>DateFactory</code> type.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how to create a value of type `Date`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Dates
output application/json
---
{
   newDate: date({year: 2012, month: 10, day: 11})
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
   "newDate": "2012-10-11"
}
```
---

# dateTime

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## dateTime(parts: DateTimeFactory): DateTime

Creates a `DateTime` value from values specified for `year`, `month`, `day`, `hour`, `minutes`,
`seconds`, and `timezone` fields.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>parts</code></p></td><td><div><div><p><code>Number</code> values for <code>year</code>, <code>month</code>, <code>day</code>, <code>hour</code>, <code>minutes</code>, and <code>seconds</code> fields followed by a <code>TimeZone</code> value for the <code>timezone</code> field. Valid values are numbers between 1 and 12 for the <code>month</code>, 1 through 31 for the <code>day</code>, 0 through 23 for the <code>hour</code>, 0 through 59 for <code>minutes</code>, and 0 through 59 (including decimals, such as 59.99) for seconds. You can specify the name-value pairs in any order, but the output is ordered by default as a <code>DateTime</code> value, such as <code>2012-10-11T10:10:10-03:00</code>. The input fields are parts of a <code>DateTimeFactory</code> type.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how to create a value of type `DateTime`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Dates
output application/json
---
{
    newDateTime: dateTime({year: 2012, month: 10, day: 11, hour: 12, minutes: 30, seconds: 40 , timeZone: |-03:00|})
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
   "newDateTime": "2012-10-11T12:30:40-03:00"
}
```
---

# localDateTime

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## localDateTime(parts: LocalDateTimeFactory): LocalDateTime

Creates a `LocalDateTime` value from values specified for `year`, `month`, `day`, `hour`, `minutes`,
and `seconds` fields.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>parts</code></p></td><td><div><div><p><code>Number</code> values for <code>year</code>, <code>month</code>, <code>day</code>, <code>hour</code>, <code>minutes</code>, and <code>seconds</code> fields. Valid values are numbers between 1 and 12 for the <code>month</code>, 1 through 31 for the <code>day</code>, 0 through 23 for the <code>hour</code>, 0 through 59 for <code>minutes</code>, and 0 through 59 (including decimals, such as 59.99) for <code>seconds</code> fields. You can specify the name-value pairs in any order, but the output is ordered as a default <code>LocalDateTime</code> value, such as <code>2012-10-11T10:10:10</code>. The input fields are parts of a <code>LocalDateTimeFactory</code> type.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how to create a value of type `LocalDateTime`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Dates
output application/json
---
{
    newLocalDateTime: localDateTime({year: 2012, month: 10, day: 11, hour: 12, minutes: 30, seconds: 40})
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
   "newLocalDateTime": "2012-10-11T12:30:40"
}
```
---

# localTime

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## localTime(parts: LocalTimeFactory): LocalTime

Creates a `LocalTime` value from values specified for `hour`, `minutes`, and `seconds` fields.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>parts</code></p></td><td><div><div><p><code>Number</code> values for <code>hour</code>, <code>minutes</code>, and <code>seconds</code> fields. Valid values are 0 through 23 for the <code>hour</code>, 0 through 59 for <code>minutes</code>, and 0 through 59 (including decimals, such as 59.99) for <code>seconds</code> fields. You can specify the name-value pairs in any order, but the output is ordered as a default <code>LocalTime</code> value, such as <code>10:10:10</code>. The input fields are parts of a <code>LocalDateTimeFactory</code> type.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

# time

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## time(parts: TimeFactory): Time

Creates a `Time` value from values specified for `hour`, `minutes`, `seconds`, and `timezone`
fields.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>parts</code></p></td><td><div><div><p><code>Number</code> values for <code>hour</code>, <code>minutes</code>, and <code>seconds</code> fields, and a <code>TimeZone</code> value for the <code>timezone</code> field. Valid values are 0 through 23 for the <code>hour</code>, 0 through 59 for <code>minutes</code>, and 0 through 59 (including decimals, such as 59.99) for <code>seconds</code> fields. The <code>timezone</code> must be a valid <code>TimeZone</code> value, such as <code>|-03:00|</code> You can specify the name-value pairs in any order, but the output is ordered as a default <code>Time</code> value, such as <code>10:10:10-03:00</code>. The input fields are parts of a <code>TimeFactory</code> type.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how to create a value of type `Time`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Dates
output application/json
---
{
  newTime: time({ hour: 12, minutes: 30, seconds: 40 , timeZone: |-03:00| })
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
   "newTime": "12:30:40-03:00"
}
```

# Dates Types (dw::core::Dates)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Type</th><th>Definition</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>DateFactory</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type DateFactory = { day: Number, month: Number, year: Number }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Type containing selectable <code>day</code>, <code>month</code>, and <code>year</code> keys and corresponding <code>Number</code> values, such as <code>{day: 21, month: 1, year: 2021}</code>. The fields accept a <code>Number</code> value. Numbers preceded by <code>0</code>, such as <code>01</code>, are not valid.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>DateTimeFactory</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type DateTimeFactory = DateFactory &amp; LocalTimeFactory &amp; Zoned</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Type that combines <code>DateFactory</code>, <code>LocalTimeFactory</code>, and <code>Zoned</code> types. For example, <code>{day: 21, month: 1, year: 2021, hour: 8, minutes: 31, seconds: 55, timeZone : |-03:00|} as DateTimeFactory</code> is a valid <code>DateTimeFactory</code> value.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>LocalDateTimeFactory</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type LocalDateTimeFactory = DateFactory &amp; LocalTimeFactory</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Type that combines <code>DateFactory</code> and <code>LocalTimeFactory</code> types. For example, <code>{day: 21, month: 1, year: 2021, hour: 8, minutes: 31, seconds: 55, timeZone : |-03:00|} as LocalDateTimeFactory</code> is a valid <code>LocalDateTimeFactory</code> value. The <code>timeZone</code> field is optional.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>LocalTimeFactory</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type LocalTimeFactory = { hour: Number, minutes: Number, seconds: Number }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Type containing selectable <code>hour</code>, <code>minutes</code>, and <code>seconds</code> keys and corresponding <code>Number</code> values, such as <code>{hour: 8, minutes: 31, seconds: 55}</code>. The fields accept any <code>Number</code> value.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>TimeFactory</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type TimeFactory = LocalTimeFactory &amp; Zoned</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Type that combines <code>LocalTimeFactory</code> and <code>Zoned</code> types. For example, <code>{hour: 8, minutes: 31, seconds: 55, timeZone : |-03:00|} as TimeFactory</code> is a valid <code>TimeFactory</code> value.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Zoned</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Zoned = { timeZone: TimeZone }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Type containing a selectable <code>timeZone</code> key and <code>TimeZone</code> value, such as <code>{ timezone : |-03:00|}</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# Dates (dw::core::Dates)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

This module contains functions for creating and manipulating dates.

To use this module, you must import it to your DataWeave code, for example, by adding the line
`import * from dw::core::Dates` to the header of your DataWeave script.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

## Functions

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><a href="dw-dates-functions-atbeginningofday">atBeginningOfDay</a></p></td><td><p>Returns a new <code>DateTime</code> value that changes the <code>Time</code> value in the input to the beginning of the specified <em>day</em>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-dates-functions-atbeginningofhour">atBeginningOfHour</a></p></td><td><p>Returns a new <code>DateTime</code> value that changes the <code>Time</code> value in the input to the beginning of the specified <em>hour</em>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-dates-functions-atbeginningofmonth">atBeginningOfMonth</a></p></td><td><p>Returns a new <code>DateTime</code> value that changes the <code>Day</code> value from the input to the first day of the specified <em>month</em>. It also sets the <code>Time</code> value to <code>00:00:00</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-dates-functions-atbeginningofweek">atBeginningOfWeek</a></p></td><td><p>Returns a new <code>DateTime</code> value that changes the <code>Day</code> and <code>Time</code> values from the input to the beginning of the first day of the specified <em>week</em>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-dates-functions-atbeginningofyear">atBeginningOfYear</a></p></td><td><p>Takes a <code>DateTime</code> value as input and returns a <code>DateTime</code> value for the first day of the <em>year</em> specified in the input. It also sets the <code>Time</code> value to <code>00:00:00</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-dates-functions-date">date</a></p></td><td><p>Creates a <code>Date</code> value from values specified for <code>year</code>, <code>month</code>, and <code>day</code> fields.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-dates-functions-datetime">dateTime</a></p></td><td><p>Creates a <code>DateTime</code> value from values specified for <code>year</code>, <code>month</code>, <code>day</code>, <code>hour</code>, <code>minutes</code>, <code>seconds</code>, and <code>timezone</code> fields.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-dates-functions-localdatetime">localDateTime</a></p></td><td><p>Creates a <code>LocalDateTime</code> value from values specified for <code>year</code>, <code>month</code>, <code>day</code>, <code>hour</code>, <code>minutes</code>, and <code>seconds</code> fields.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-dates-functions-localtime">localTime</a></p></td><td><p>Creates a <code>LocalTime</code> value from values specified for <code>hour</code>, <code>minutes</code>, and <code>seconds</code> fields.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-dates-functions-time">time</a></p></td><td><p>Creates a <code>Time</code> value from values specified for <code>hour</code>, <code>minutes</code>, <code>seconds</code>, and <code>timezone</code> fields.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-dates-functions-today">today</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the date for today as a <code>Date</code> type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-dates-functions-tomorrow">tomorrow</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the date for tomorrow as a <code>Date</code> type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-dates-functions-yesterday">yesterday</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the date for yesterday as a <code>Date</code> type.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Types

- [Dates Types](dw-dates-types)

# diff

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## diff(actual: Any, expected: Any, diffConfig: { unordered?: Boolean } = {}, path: String = "(root)"): Diff

Returns the structural differences between two values.

Differences between objects can be ordered (the default) or unordered. Ordered means that two
objects do not differ if their key-value pairs are in the same order. Differences are expressed as
`Difference` type.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>actual</code></p></td><td><p>The actual value. Can be any data type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>expected</code></p></td><td><p>The expected value to compare to the actual. Can be any data type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>diffConfig</code></p></td><td><p>Setting for changing the default to unordered using `{ "unordered" : true} (explained in the introduction).</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows a variety of uses of `diff`.

#### Source

```Dataweave
import diff from dw::util::Diff
ns ns0 http://locahost.com
ns ns1 http://acme.com
output application/dw
---
{
  "a": diff({a: 1}, {b:1}),
  "b": diff({ns0#a: 1}, {ns1#a:1}),
  "c": diff([1,2,3], []),
  "d": diff([], [1,2,3]),
  "e": diff([1,2,3], [1,2,3, 4]),
  "f": diff([{a: 1}], [{a: 2}]),
  "g": diff({a @(c: 2): 1}, {a @(c: 3): 1}),
  "h": diff(true, false),
  "i": diff(1, 2),
  "j": diff("test", "other test"),
  "k": diff({a: 1}, {a:1}),
  "l": diff({ns0#a: 1}, {ns0#a:1}),
  "m": diff([1,2,3], [1,2,3]),
  "n": diff([], []),
  "o": diff([{a: 1}], [{a: 1}]),
  "p": diff({a @(c: 2): 1}, {a @(c:2): 1}),
  "q": diff(true, true),
  "r": diff(1, 1),
  "s": diff("other test", "other test"),
  "t": diff({a:1 ,b: 2},{b: 2, a:1}, {unordered: true}),
  "u": [{format: "ssn",data: "ABC"}] diff [{ format: "ssn",data: "ABC"}]
}
```

#### Output

```XML
ns ns0 http://locahost.com
ns ns1 http://acme.com
---
{
  a: {
    matches: false,
    diffs: [
      {
        expected: "Entry (root).a with type Number",
        actual: "was not present in object.",
        path: "(root).a"
      }
    ]
  },
  b: {
    matches: false,
    diffs: [
      {
        expected: "Entry (root).ns0#a with type Number",
        actual: "was not present in object.",
        path: "(root).ns0#a"
      }
    ]
  },
  c: {
    matches: false,
    diffs: [
      {
        expected: "Array size is 0",
        actual: "was 3",
        path: "(root)"
      }
    ]
  },
  d: {
    matches: false,
    diffs: [
      {
        expected: "Array size is 3",
        actual: "was 0",
        path: "(root)"
      }
    ]
  },
  e: {
    matches: false,
    diffs: [
      {
        expected: "Array size is 4",
        actual: "was 3",
        path: "(root)"
      }
    ]
  },
  f: {
    matches: false,
    diffs: [
      {
        expected: "1" as String {mimeType: "application/dw"},
        actual: "2" as String {mimeType: "application/dw"},
        path: "(root)[0].a"
      }
    ]
  },
  g: {
    matches: false,
    diffs: [
      {
        expected: "3" as String {mimeType: "application/dw"},
        actual: "2" as String {mimeType: "application/dw"},
        path: "(root).a.@.c"
      }
    ]
  },
  h: {
    matches: false,
    diffs: [
      {
        expected: "false",
        actual: "true",
        path: "(root)"
      }
    ]
  },
  i: {
    matches: false,
    diffs: [
      {
        expected: "2",
        actual: "1",
        path: "(root)"
      }
    ]
  },
  j: {
    matches: false,
    diffs: [
      {
        expected: "\"other test\"",
        actual: "\"test\"",
        path: "(root)"
      }
    ]
  },
  k: {
    matches: true,
    diffs: []
  },
  l: {
    matches: true,
    diffs: []
  },
  m: {
    matches: true,
    diffs: []
  },
  n: {
    matches: true,
    diffs: []
  },
  o: {
    matches: true,
    diffs: []
  },
  p: {
    matches: true,
    diffs: []
  },
  q: {
    matches: true,
    diffs: []
  },
  r: {
    matches: true,
    diffs: []
  },
  s: {
    matches: true,
    diffs: []
  },
  t: {
    matches: true,
    diffs: []
  },
  u: {
    matches: true,
    diffs: []
  }
}
```

# Diff Types (dw::util::Diff)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Type</th><th>Definition</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Diff</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Diff = { matches: Boolean, diffs: Array&lt;Difference&gt; }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Describes the entire difference between two values.</p></div><div><ul><li><p><em>Example with no differences:</em> <code>{ "matches": true, "diffs": [ ] }</code></p></li><li><p><em>Example with differences:</em> <code>{ "matches": true, "diffs": [ "expected": "4", "actual": "2", "path": "(root).a.@.d" ] }</code></p></li></ul></div><div><p>See the <code>diff</code> function for another example.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Difference</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Difference = { expected: String, actual: String, path: String }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Describes a single difference between two values at a given structure.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# Diff (dw::util::Diff)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

This utility module calculates the difference between two values and returns a list of differences.

To use this module, you must import it to your DataWeave code, for example, by adding the line
`import * from dw::util::Diff` to the header of your DataWeave script.

## Functions

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><a href="dw-diff-functions-diff">diff</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the structural differences between two values.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Types

- [Diff Types](dw-diff-types)

# docTypeAsString

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## docTypeAsString(docType: DocType): String

Transforms a `DocType` value to a string representation.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.5.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>docType</code></p></td><td><p>DocType</p></td><td><p>The <code>DocType</code> value to transform to a string.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example transforms a `DocType` value that includes a `systemId` to a string representation.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::xml::Dtd
output application/json
---
docTypeAsString({rootName: "cXML", systemId: "http://xml.cxml.org/schemas/cXML/1.2.014/cXML.dtd"})
```

#### Output

```Json
"cXML SYSTEM http://xml.cxml.org/schemas/cXML/1.2.014/cXML.dtd"
```

### Example

This example transforms a `DocType` value that includes a `publicId` and `systemId` to a string
representation.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::xml::Dtd
output application/json
---
docTypeAsString({rootName: "html", publicId: "-//W3C//DTD XHTML 1.0 Transitional//EN", systemId: "http://www.w3.org/TR/xhtml1/DTD/xhtml1-transitional.dtd"})
```

#### Output

```Json
"html PUBLIC -//W3C//DTD XHTML 1.0 Transitional//EN http://www.w3.org/TR/xhtml1/DTD/xhtml1-transitional.dtd"
```
---

Supports the following fields:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>rootName</code>: Root element of the declaration.</p></li><li><p><code>publicId</code>: Publicly available standard (optional).</p></li><li><p><code>systemId</code>: Local URL (optional).</p></li><li><p><code>internalSubset</code>: Internal DTD subset (optional).</p></li></ul></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.5.0.</em></p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

# DataWeave Reference

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

DataWeave functions are packaged in modules.

Functions in the Core (`dw::Core`) module are imported automatically into your DataWeave scripts. To
use other modules, you need to import the module or functions you want to use by adding the import
directive to the head of your DataWeave script, for example:

- `import dw::core::Strings`
- `import camelize, capitalize from dw::core::Strings`
- `import * from dw::core::Strings`

The way you import a module impacts the way you need to call its functions from a DataWeave script.
If the directive does not list specific functions to import or use `* from` to import all functions
from a function module, you need to specify the module when you call the function from your script.
For example, this import directive does not identify any functions to import from the String module,
so it calls the `pluralize` function like this: `Strings::pluralize("box")`.

Transform

```dataweave
%dw 2.0
import dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
{ 'plural': Strings::pluralize("box") }
```

The next example identifies a specific function to import from the String module, so it can call the
method like this: `pluralize("box")`.

Transform

```dataweave
%dw 2.0
import pluralize from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
{ 'plural': pluralize("box") }
```

The next example imports all functions from the String module, so it can call the method like this:
`pluralize("box")`.

Transform

```dataweave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
{ 'plural': pluralize("box") }
```

## Function Signatures

Each DataWeave function in the DataWeave Reference is identified by its function signature. A
function specifies a function name, zero or more parameters, and a return type.

Basic syntax of a two-parameter function signature:

`function(parameterType,parameterType): returnType`

For example, the signature `contains(String, String): Boolean` for the `contains` function has two
parameters, each of which accepts a value of the String type. The function returns a Boolean value.

Parameters in the function signature correspond, _in order_, to their named parameters, which are
described in Parameters tables for each signature. For example, in
[contains(String, String): Boolean](dw-core-functions-contains#contains2), the Parameters table
indicates that `text` is the first parameter and `toSearch` is the second.

Many DataWeave functions are overloaded to handle different data types. There is a unique function
signature for each variant of the function. For example, [isEmpty](dw-core-functions-isempty) is
overloaded to support an input value of an Array, String, Object, or Null type.

For more information on the data types, see [Type System](dataweave-type-system).

### Type Parameters in Function Signatures

Function signatures can contain type parameters, which are similar to generics in some programming
languages. For example, the `Array<T>` in `contains(Array<T>, Any): Boolean` indicates that the
array can contain elements of any type (`T`) that DataWeave supports. By contrast, an array of a
specific type or types specifies the type, for example, `Array<String>`, `Array<Number>`, or for
both types `Array<String|Number>`.

### Function Types in Function Signatures

Functions can be passed as arguments. Some parameters within function signatures are function types
that take one or more parameters of the form `parameterName:Type` and have a return type
(`â ReturnType`). For example, the function type `(item: T, index: Number) â R)` is a parameter type
of the [map](dw-core-functions-map) function
(`map(Array<T>, (item: T, index: Number) â R): Array<R>`). The function type accepts a value of any
type `T` and a value of type Number, which serve as parameters to return a value of type `R`.

## See Also

[Functions](dataweave-language-guide#functions)

# acos

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## acos(angle: Number): Number | NaN

Returns an arc cosine value that can range from `0.0` through pi.

If the absolute value of the input is greater than `1`, the result is `null`.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>angle</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number to convert into it arc cosine value.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `acos` behaves with different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Math
output application/json
---
{
  "acos0": acos(0),
  "acos13": acos(0.13),
  "acos-1": acos(-1),
  "acos1": acos(1),
  "acos1.1": acos(1.1)
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
   "acos0": 1.5707963267948966,
   "acos13": 1.440427347091751,
   "acos-1": 3.141592653589793,
   "acos1": 0.0,
   "acos1.1": null
 }
```
---

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>angle</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number to convert into its arc sine value.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>angle</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number of radians in an angle.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

```DataWeave
output application/json
---
{
   "tan0": tan(0),
   "tan13": tan(0.13),
   "tan-1": tan(-1)
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
   "tan0": 0.0,
   "tan13": 0.13073731800446006,
   "tan-1": -1.5574077246549023
 }
```
---

# toDegrees

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## toDegrees(angrad: Number): Number

Converts an angle measured in radians to an approximately equivalent number of degrees.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>angrad</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number of radians to convert to degrees.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `toDegrees` behaves with different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Math
output application/json
---
{
  "toDegrees0.17":  toDegrees(0.174),
  "toDegrees0": toDegrees(0),
  "toDegrees-20": toDegrees(-0.20)
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
   "toDegrees0.17": 9.969465635276323832571267395889251,
   "toDegrees0": 0E+19,
   "toDegrees-20": -11.45915590261646417536927286883822
 }
```

# toRadians

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## toRadians(angdeg: Number): Number

Converts a given number of degrees in an angle to an approximately equivalent number of radians.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>angdeg</code></p></td><td><div><div><p>Number of degrees to convert into radians.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

This example shows how `toRadians` behaves with different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Math
output application/json
---
{
  "toRadians10":  toRadians(10),
  "toRadians013": toRadians(0.13),
  "toRadians-20": toRadians(-20)
}
```

```Json
   "toRadians10": 0.1745329251994329576922222222222222,
   "toRadians013": 0.002268928027592628449998888888888889,

 }
```
