# run

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## run(fileToExecute: String, fs: Dictionary<String>, readerInputs: Dictionary<ReaderInput> = {}, inputValues: Dictionary<Any> = {}, configuration: RuntimeExecutionConfiguration = {}): RunResult

Runs the input script under the provided context and executes the script in the current runtime.

_Experimental:_ This function is an experimental feature that is subject to change or removal from
future versions of DataWeave.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>fileToExecute</code></p></td><td><p>Name of the file to execute.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>fs</code></p></td><td><p>File system that contains the file to execute.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>readerInput</code></p></td><td><p>Inputs to read and bind to the execution.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>inputValues</code></p></td><td><p>Inputs to bind directly to the execution.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>configuration</code></p></td><td><p>The runtime configuration.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `run` behaves with different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
import * from dw::Runtime
var jsonValue = {
  value: '{"name": "Mariano"}' as Binary {encoding: "UTF-8"},
  encoding: "UTF-8",
  properties: {},
  mimeType: "application/json"
}

var jsonValue2 = {
  value: '{"name": "Mariano", "lastName": "achaval"}' as Binary {encoding: "UTF-8"},
  encoding: "UTF-8",
  properties: {},
  mimeType: "application/json"
}

var invalidJsonValue = {
  value: '{"name": "Mariano' as Binary {encoding: "UTF-8"},
  encoding: "UTF-8",
  properties: {},
  mimeType: "application/json"
}

var Utils = "fun sum(a,b) = a +b"
---
{
  "execute_ok" : run("main.dwl", {"main.dwl": "{a: 1}"}, {"payload": jsonValue }),
  "logs" : do {
    var execResult = run("main.dwl", {"main.dwl": "{a: log(1)}"}, {"payload": jsonValue })
    ---
    {
        m: execResult.logs.message,
        l: execResult.logs.level
    }
  },
  "grant" : run("main.dwl", {"main.dwl": "{a: readUrl(`http://google.com`)}"}, {"payload": jsonValue }, { securityManager: (grant, args) -> false }),
  "library" : run("main.dwl", {"main.dwl": "Utils::sum(1,2)", "/Utils.dwl": Utils }, {"payload": jsonValue }),
  "timeout" : run("main.dwl", {"main.dwl": "(1 to 1000000000000) map \$ + 1" }, {"payload": jsonValue }, {timeOut: 2}).success,
  "execFail" : run("main.dwl", {"main.dwl": "dw::Runtime::fail('My Bad')" }, {"payload": jsonValue }),
  "parseFail" : run("main.dwl", {"main.dwl": "(1 + " }, {"payload": jsonValue }),
  "writerFail" : run("main.dwl", {"main.dwl": "output application/xml --- 2" }, {"payload": jsonValue }),
  "readerFail" : run("main.dwl", {"main.dwl": "output application/xml --- payload" }, {"payload": invalidJsonValue }),
  "defaultOutput" : run("main.dwl", {"main.dwl": "payload" }, {"payload": jsonValue2}, {outputMimeType: "application/csv", writerProperties: {"separator": "|"}}),
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "execute_ok": {
    "success": true,
    "value": "{\n  a: 1\n}",
    "mimeType": "application/dw",
    "encoding": "UTF-8",
    "logs": [

    ]
  },
  "logs": {
    "m": [
      "1"
    ],
    "l": [
      "INFO"
    ]
  },
  "grant": {
    "success": false,
    "message": "The given required permissions: `Resource` are not being granted for this execution.\nTrace:\n  at readUrl (Unknown)\n  at main::main (line: 1, column: 5)",
    "location": {
      "start": {
        "index": 0,
        "line": 0,
        "column": 0
      },
      "end": {
        "index": 0,
        "line": 0,
        "column": 0
      },
      "content": "Unknown location"
    },
    "stack": [
      "readUrl (anonymous:0:0)",
      "main (main:1:5)"
    ],
    "logs": [

    ]
  },
  "library": {
    "success": true,
    "value": "3",
    "mimeType": "application/dw",
    "encoding": "UTF-8",
    "logs": [

    ]
  },
  "timeout": false,
  "execFail": {
    "success": false,
    "message": "My Bad\nTrace:\n  at fail (Unknown)\n  at main::main (line: 1, column: 1)",
    "location": {
      "start": {
        "index": 0,
        "line": 0,
        "column": 0
      },
      "end": {
        "index": 0,
        "line": 0,
        "column": 0
      },
      "content": "Unknown location"
    },
    "stack": [
      "fail (anonymous:0:0)",
      "main (main:1:1)"
    ],
    "logs": [

    ]
  },
  "parseFail": {
    "success": false,
    "message": "Invalid input \"1 + \", expected parameter or parenEnd (line 1, column 2):\n\n\n1| (1 + \n    ^^^^\nLocation:\nmain (line: 1, column:2)",
    "location": {
      "start": {
        "index": 0,
        "line": 1,
        "column": 2
      },
      "end": {
        "index": 4,
        "line": 1,
        "column": 6
      },
      "content": "\n1| (1 + \n    ^^^^"
    },
    "logs": [

    ]
  },
  "writerFail": {
    "success": false,
    "message": "Trying to output non-whitespace characters outside main element tree (in prolog or epilog), while writing Xml at .",
    "location": {
      "content": ""
    },
    "stack": [

    ],
    "logs": [

    ]
  },
  "readerFail": {
    "success": false,
    "message": "Unexpected end-of-input at payload@[1:18] (line:column), expected '\"', while reading `payload` as Json.\n \n1| {\"name\": \"Mariano\n                    ^",
    "location": {
      "content": "\n1| {\"name\": \"Mariano\n                    ^"
    },
    "stack": [

    ],
    "logs": [

    ]
  },
  "defaultOutput": {
    "success": true,
    "value": "name|lastName\nMariano|achaval\n",
    "mimeType": "application/csv",
    "encoding": "UTF-8",
    "logs": [

    ]
  }
}
```
---

# runUrl

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## runUrl(url: String, readerInputs: Dictionary<ReaderInput> = {}, inputValues: Dictionary<Any> = {}, configuration: RuntimeExecutionConfiguration = {}): RunResult

Runs the script at the specified URL.

_Experimental:_ This function is an experimental feature that is subject to change or removal from
future versions of DataWeave.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>url</code></p></td><td><p>The name of the file to execute.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>readerInputs</code></p></td><td><p>Inputs to read and bind to the execution.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>inputValues</code></p></td><td><p>Inputs to be bind directly to the execution.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>configuration</code></p></td><td><p>The runtime configuration.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `runUrl` behaves with different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
import * from dw::Runtime
var jsonValue = {
  value: '{"name": "Mariano"}' as Binary {encoding: "UTF-8"},
  encoding: "UTF-8",
  properties: {},
  mimeType: "application/json"
}

var Utils = "fun sum(a,b) = a +b"
---
{
  "execute_ok" : runUrl("classpath://org/mule/weave/v2/engine/runtime_runUrl/example.dwl", {"payload": jsonValue })
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
   "execute_ok": {
     "success": true,
     "value": "\"Mariano\"",
     "mimeType": "application/dw",
     "encoding": "UTF-8",
     "logs": [

     ]
   }
 }
```
---

# try

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## try<T>(delegate: () -> T): TryResult<T>

Evaluates the delegate function and returns an object with `success: true` and `result` if the
delegate function succeeds, or an object with `success: false` and `error` if the delegate function
throws an exception.

The `orElseTry` and `orElse` functions will also continue processing if the `try` function fails.
See the `orElseTry` and `orElse` documentation for more complete examples of handling failing `try`
function expressions.

Note: Instead of using the `orElseTry` and `orElse` functions, based on the output of the `try`
function, you can add conditional logic to execute when the result is `success: true` or
`success: false`.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>delegate</code></p></td><td><p>The function to evaluate.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example calls the `try` function using the `randomNumber` function as argument. The function
`randomNumber` generates a random number and calls `fail` if the number is greater than 0.5. The
declaration of this function is in the scriptâs header.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import try, fail from dw::Runtime
output application/json
fun randomNumber() =
if(random() > 0.5)
  fail("This function is failing")
 else
  "OK"
---
try(() -> randomNumber())
```

#### Output

When `randomNumber` fails, the output is:

```JSON
{
  "success": false,
  "error": {
    "kind": "UserException",
    "message": "This function is failing",
    "location": "Unknown location",
    "stack": [
      "fail (anonymous:0:0)",
      "myFunction (anonymous:1:114)",
      "main (anonymous:1:179)"
    ]
  }
}
```

When `randomNumber` succeeds, the output is:

```JSON
{
  "success": true,
  "result": "OK"
}
```
---

# version

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## version(): String

Returns the DataWeave version that is currently running.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.5.0._

### Example

This example returns the DataWeave version.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::Runtime
output application/json
---
version()
```

#### Output

```Json
"2.5"
```
---

# wait

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## wait<T>(value: T, timeout: Number): T

Stops the execution for the specified timeout period (in milliseconds).

> [!WARNING] Stopping the execution blocks the thread, potentially causing slowness, low performance
> and potentially freezing of the entire runtime. This operation is intended for limited functional
> testing purposes. Do not use this function in a production application, performance testing, or
> with multiple applications deployed.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>value</code></p></td><td><p>Input of any type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>timeout</code></p></td><td><p>The number of milliseconds to wait.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example waits 2000 milliseconds (2 seconds) to execute.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::Runtime
output application/json
---
{ "user" : 1 } wait 2000
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "user": 1 }
```
---

# Runtime Types (dw::Runtime)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Type</th><th>Definition</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>DataFormatDescriptor</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type DataFormatDescriptor = { name: String, binary: Boolean, defaultEncoding?: String, extensions: Array&lt;String&gt;, defaultMimeType: String, acceptedMimeTypes: Array&lt;String&gt;, readerProperties: Array&lt;DataFormatProperty&gt;, writerProperties: Array&lt;DataFormatProperty&gt; }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Description of a <code>DataFormat</code> that provides all metadata information.</p></div><div><p><em>Experimental:</em> This type is an experimental feature that is subject to change or removal from future versions of DataWeave.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>DataFormatProperty</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type DataFormatProperty = { name: String, optional: Boolean, defaultValue?: Any, description: String, possibleValues: Array&lt;Any&gt; }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Type that describes a data format property. The fields include a <code>name</code>, <code>description</code>, array of possible values (<code>possibleValues</code>), an optional default value (<code>defaultValue</code>), and an <code>optional</code> flag that indicates whether the property is required or not.</p></div><div><p><em>Experimental:</em> This type is an experimental feature that is subject to change or removal from future versions of DataWeave.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>EvalResult</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type EvalResult = Result&lt;EvalSuccess, ExecutionFailure&gt;</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Data type of the data that returns when an <code>eval</code> function executes successfully.</p></div><div><p><em>Experimental:</em> This type is an experimental feature that is subject to change or removal from future versions of DataWeave.</p></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.7.0.</em></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>EvalSuccess</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type EvalSuccess = { value: Any, logs: Array&lt;LogEntry&gt; }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Data type of the data that returns when an <code>eval</code> function executes successfully.</p></div><div><p><em>Experimental:</em> This type is an experimental feature that is subject to change or removal from future versions of DataWeave.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>ExecutionFailure</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type ExecutionFailure = { message: String, kind: String, stack?: Array&lt;String&gt;, location: Location, logs: Array&lt;LogEntry&gt; }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Data type of the data that returns when a <code>run</code> or <code>eval</code> function fails.</p></div><div><p><em>Experimental:</em> This type is an experimental feature that is subject to change or removal from future versions of DataWeave.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Location</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Location = { start?: Position, end?: Position, locationString: String, text?: String, sourceIdentifier?: String }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Type that represents the location of an expression in a DataWeave file.</p></div><div><p><em>Experimental:</em> This type is an experimental feature that is subject to change or removal from future versions of DataWeave.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>LogEntry</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type LogEntry = { level: LogLevel, timestamp: String, message: String }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Type for a log entry, which consists of a <code>level</code> for a <code>LogLevel</code> value, a <code>timestamp</code>, and <code>message</code>.</p></div><div><p><em>Experimental:</em> This type is an experimental feature that is subject to change or removal from future versions of DataWeave.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>LogLevel</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type LogLevel = "INFO" | "ERROR" | "WARN"</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Identifies the different kinds of log levels (<code>INFO</code>, <code>ERROR</code>, or <code>WARN</code>).</p></div><div><p><em>Experimental:</em> This type is an experimental feature that is subject to change or removal from future versions of DataWeave.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>LoggerService</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type LoggerService = { initialize?: () -&gt; Object, log: (level: LogLevel, msg: String, context: Object) -&gt; Any, shutdown?: () -&gt; Boolean }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Service that handles all logging:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>initialize</code>: Function called when the execution starts. DataWeave sends the result to every <code>log</code> call through the <code>context</code> parameter, so that, for example, a logging header can be sent at initialization and recovered in each log.</p></li><li><p><code>log</code>: Function that is called on every log message.</p></li><li><p><code>shutdown</code>: Function called when the execution completes, which is a common time to flush any buffer or to log out gracefully.</p></li></ul></div><div><p><em>Experimental:</em> This type is an experimental feature that is subject to change or removal from future versions of DataWeave.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>MimeType</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type MimeType = String</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A String representation of a MIME type.</p></div><div><p><em>Experimental:</em> This type is an experimental feature that is subject to change or removal from future versions of DataWeave.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Position</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Position = { index: Number, line: Number, column: Number }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Type that represents a position in a file by its index and its line and column.</p></div><div><p><em>Experimental:</em> This type is an experimental feature that is subject to change or removal from future versions of DataWeave.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>ReaderInput</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type ReaderInput = { value: Binary, encoding?: String, properties?: Dictionary&lt;SimpleType&gt;, mimeType: MimeType }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Input to the DataWeave reader created for the specified MIME type, which includes the Binary input and MIME type, as well as optional encoding and properties values.</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>value</code>: The input, in Binary format.</p></li><li><p><code>encoding</code>: The encoding for the reader to use.</p></li><li><p><code>properties</code>: The reader properties used to parse the input.</p></li><li><p><code>mimeType</code>: The MIME type of the input.</p></li></ul></div><div><p><em>Experimental:</em> This type is an experimental feature that is subject to change or removal from future versions of DataWeave.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>RunResult</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type RunResult = Result&lt;RunSuccess, ExecutionFailure&gt;</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Data type of the data that returns when a <code>run</code> function executes successfully.</p></div><div><p><em>Experimental:</em> This type is an experimental feature that is subject to change or removal from future versions of DataWeave.</p></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.7.0.</em></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>RunSuccess</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type RunSuccess = { value: Binary, mimeType: MimeType, encoding?: String, logs: Array&lt;LogEntry&gt; }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Data type of the data that returns when a <code>run</code> function executes successfully.</p></div><div><p><em>Experimental:</em> This type is an experimental feature that is subject to change or removal from future versions of DataWeave.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>RuntimeExecutionConfiguration</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type RuntimeExecutionConfiguration = { timeOut?: Number, outputMimeType?: MimeType, writerProperties?: Dictionary&lt;SimpleType&gt;, onException?: "HANDLE" | "FAIL", securityManager?: SecurityManager, loggerService?: LoggerService, maxStackSize?: Number, onUnhandledTimeout?: (threadName: String, javaStackTrace: String, code: String) -&gt; Any }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Configuration of the runtime execution that has advanced parameters.</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>timeOut</code>: Maximum amount of time the DataWeave script takes before timing out.</p></li><li><p><code>outputMimeType</code>: Default output MIME type if not specified in the DataWeave script.</p></li><li><p><code>writerProperties</code>: Writer properties to use with the specified the <code>outputMimeType</code> property.</p></li><li><p><code>onException</code> Specifies the behavior that occurs when the execution fails:</p><div><ul><li><p><code>HANDLE</code> (default value) returns <code>ExecutionFailure</code>.</p></li><li><p><code>FAIL</code> propagates an exception.</p></li></ul></div></li><li><p><code>securityManager</code>: Identifies the <code>SecurityManager</code> to use in this execution. This security manager is composed by the current <code>SecurityManager</code>.</p></li><li><p><code>loggerService</code>: The <code>LoggerService</code> to use in this execution.</p></li><li><p><code>maxStackSize</code>: The maximum stack size.</p></li><li><p><code>onUnhandledTimeout</code>: Callback that is called when the watchdog was not able to stop the execution after a timeout, which is useful for logging or reporting the problem. The callback is called with the following:</p><div><ul><li><p><code>threadName</code>: Name of the thread that hanged.</p></li><li><p><code>javaStackTrace</code>: Java stack trace where the hang occurred.</p></li><li><p><code>code</code>: The DataWeave code that caused the hang.</p></li></ul></div></li></ul></div><div><p><em>Experimental:</em> This type is an experimental feature that is subject to change or removal from future versions of DataWeave.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>SecurityManager</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type SecurityManager = (grant: String, args: Array&lt;Any&gt;) -&gt; Boolean</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Function that is called when a privilege must be granted to the current execution.</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>grant</code> is the name of the privilege, such as <code>Resource</code>.</p></li><li><p><code>args</code> provides a list of parameters that the function requesting the privilege calls.</p></li></ul></div><div><p><em>Experimental:</em> This type is an experimental feature that is subject to change or removal from future versions of DataWeave.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>TryResult</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type TryResult = Result&lt;T, TryResultFailure&gt;</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Object with a result or error message. If <code>success</code> is <code>false</code>, data type provides the <code>error</code>. If <code>true</code>, the data type provides the <code>result</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>TryResultFailure</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type TryResultFailure = { kind: String, message: String, stack?: Array&lt;String&gt;, stackTrace?: String, location?: String }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A type for representing failed execution from <code>try</code>.</p></div><div><p>Supports the following fields:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>kind</code>: The error kind.</p></li><li><p><code>message</code>: The error message.</p></li><li><p><code>stack</code>: The stacktrace error (optional).</p></li><li><p><code>stackTrace</code>: The stacktrace string value representation (optional).</p></li><li><p><code>location</code>: The error location (optional).</p></li></ul></div><div><p>Starting in Mule 4.4.0, if the stack is not present, the <code>stackTrace</code> field is available with the native Java stack trace.</p></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.7.0.</em></p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# Runtime (dw::Runtime)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

This module contains functions for interacting with the DataWeave runtime, which executes the
language.

To use this module, you must import it to your DataWeave code, for example, by adding the line
`import * from dw::Runtime` to the header of your DataWeave script.

## Functions

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><a href="dw-runtime-functions-dataformatsdescriptor">dataFormatsDescriptor</a></p></td><td><p>Returns an array of all <code>DataFormatDescriptor</code> values that are installed in the current instance of DataWeave.</p><p><em>Experimental:</em> This function is an experimental feature that is subject to change or removal from future versions.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-runtime-functions-eval">eval</a></p></td><td><p>Evaluates a script with the specified context and returns the result of that evaluation.</p><p><em>Experimental:</em> This function is an experimental feature that is subject to change or removal from future versions.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-runtime-functions-evalurl">evalUrl</a></p></td><td><p>Evaluates the script at the specified URL.</p><p><em>Experimental:</em> This function is an experimental feature that is subject to change or removal from future versions.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-runtime-functions-fail">fail</a></p></td><td><p>Throws an exception with the specified message.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-runtime-functions-failif">failIf</a></p></td><td><p>Produces an error with the specified message if the expression in the evaluator returns <code>true</code>. Otherwise, the function returns the value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-runtime-functions-finddataformatdescriptorbymime">findDataFormatDescriptorByMime</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the <code>DataFormatDescriptor</code> based on the <code>dw::module::Mime::MimeType</code> specified or <code>null</code> if there is no <code>DataFormatDescriptor</code> for the given <code>MimeType</code>.</p><p><em>Experimental:</em> This function is an experimental feature that is subject to change or removal from future versions.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-runtime-functions-location">location</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the location of a given value, or <code>null</code> if the location canât be traced back to a DataWeave file.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-runtime-functions-locationstring">locationString</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the location string of a given value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-runtime-functions-orelse">orElse</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the result of the <code>orElse</code> argument if the <code>previous</code> argument to <code>try</code> fails. Otherwise, the function returns the value of the <code>previous</code> argument.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-runtime-functions-orelsetry">orElseTry</a></p></td><td><p>Function to use with <code>try</code> to chain multiple <code>try</code> requests.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-runtime-functions-prop">prop</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the value of the property with the specified name or <code>null</code> if the property is not defined.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-runtime-functions-props">props</a></p></td><td><p>Returns all the properties configured for the DataWeave runtime, which executes the language.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-runtime-functions-run">run</a></p></td><td><p>Runs the input script under the provided context and executes the script in the current runtime.</p><p><em>Experimental:</em> This function is an experimental feature that is subject to change or removal from future versions.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-runtime-functions-runurl">runUrl</a></p></td><td><p>Runs the script at the specified URL.</p><p><em>Experimental:</em> This function is an experimental feature that is subject to change or removal from future versions.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-runtime-functions-try">try</a></p></td><td><p>Evaluates the delegate function and returns an object with <code>success: true</code> and <code>result</code> if the delegate function succeeds, or an object with <code>success: false</code> and <code>error</code> if the delegate function throws an exception.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-runtime-functions-version">version</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the DataWeave version that is currently running.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-runtime-functions-wait">wait</a></p></td><td><p>Stops the execution for the specified timeout period (in milliseconds).</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Types

- [Runtime Types](dw-runtime-types)
---

# appendIfMissing

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## appendIfMissing(text: String, suffix: String): String

Appends the `suffix` to the end of the `text` if the `text` does not already ends with the `suffix`.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The input string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>suffix</code></p></td><td><p>The text used as the suffix.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `appendIfMissing` behaves with different inputs and sizes.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import appendIfMissing from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
{
  "a": appendIfMissing(null, ""),
  "b": appendIfMissing("abc", ""),
  "c": appendIfMissing("", "xyz") ,
  "d": appendIfMissing("abc", "xyz") ,
  "e": appendIfMissing("abcxyz", "xyz")
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
  "a": null,
  "b": "abc",
  "c": "xyz",
  "d": "abcxyz",
  "e": "abcxyz"
}
```

## appendIfMissing(text: Null, suffix: String): Null

Helper function that enables `appendIfMissing` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0._
---

# camelize

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## camelize(text: String): String

Returns a string in camel case based on underscores in the string.

All underscores are deleted, including any underscores at the beginning of the string.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The string to convert to camel case.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example converts a string that contains underscores to camel case.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
{
  "a" : camelize("customer_first_name"),
  "b" : camelize("_name_starts_with_underscore")
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
   "a": "customerFirstName",
   "b": "nameStartsWithUnderscore"
 }
```

## camelize(text: Null): Null

Helper function that enables `camelize` to work with a `null` value.
---

# capitalize

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## capitalize(text: String): String

Capitalizes the first letter of each word in a string.

The function treats every non-alphabetic character as a separator and replaces underscores with
spaces. For example, `capitalize("a*s_bâs")` results in `"A*S BâS"`.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The string to capitalize.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example capitalizes a set of strings.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
{
  "a" : capitalize("customer"),
  "b" : capitalize("customer_first_name"),
  "c" : capitalize("customer NAME"),
  "d" : capitalize("customerName"),
  "e" : capitalize("a*s_bâs")
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
  "a": "Customer",
  "b": "Customer First Name",
  "c": "Customer Name",
  "d": "Customer Name",
  "e": "A*S BâS"
}
```

## capitalize(text: Null): Null

Helper function that enables `capitalize` to work with a `null` value.
---

# charCode

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## charCode(text: String): Number

Returns the Unicode for the first character in an input string.

For an empty string, the function fails and returns `Unexpected empty string`.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The input string.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example returns Unicode for the "M" in "Mule".

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
{
  "charCode" : charCode("Mule")
}
```

#### Output

```XML
{ "charCode" : 77 }
```

## charCode(text: Null): Null

Helper function that enables `charCode` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._
---

# charCodeAt

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## charCodeAt(content: String, position: Number): Number

Returns the Unicode for a character at the specified index.

This function fails if the index is invalid.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>content</code></p></td><td><p>The input string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>position</code></p></td><td><p>The index (a <code>Number</code> type) of a character in the string (as a string array). Note that the index of the first character is <code>0</code>.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example returns Unicode for the "u" at index `1` in "MuleSoft".

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
{
  "charCodeAt" : charCodeAt("MuleSoft", 1)
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "charCodeAt": 117 }
```

## charCodeAt(content: Null, position: Any): Null

Helper function that enables `charCodeAt` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._
---

# collapse

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## collapse(text: String): Array<String>

Collapses the string into substrings of equal characters.

Each substring contains a single character or identical characters that are adjacent to one another
in the input string. Empty spaces are treated as characters.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The string to collapse.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how the function collapses characters. Notice that the empty space (" ") is
treated as a character.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import collapse from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
collapse("a  b babb a")
```

#### Output

```Json
["a", "  ", "b", " ", "b", "a", "bb", " ", "a"]
```

## collapse(text: Null): Null

Helper function that enables `collapse` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._
---

# countCharactersBy

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## countCharactersBy(text: String, predicate: (character: String) -> Boolean): Number

Counts the number of times an expression that iterates through each character in a string returns
`true`.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The string to which the <code>predicate</code> applies.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>predicate</code></p></td><td><p>Expression to apply to each character in the <code>text</code> string. The expression must return a Boolean value.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example counts the digits in a string.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
"42 = 11 * 2 + 20" countCharactersBy isNumeric($)
```

#### Output

```Json
7
```

## countCharactersBy(text: Null, predicate: (character: Nothing) -> Any): Null

Helper function to make `countCharactersBy` work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._
---

# countMatches

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## countMatches(text: String, pattern: String): Number

Counts the number of matches in a string.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The string to search for matches.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>pattern</code></p></td><td><p>A substring to find in the text.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example counts matches in a string.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import countMatches from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
"hello worlo!" countMatches "lo"
```

#### Output

```Json
2
```

## countMatches(text: String, pattern: Regex): Number

Counts the number of times a regular expression matches text in a string.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The string to search for matches.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>pattern</code></p></td><td><p>The regex pattern to use in the search.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example counts the vowels in a string.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import countMatches from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
"hello, ciao!" countMatches /[aeiou]/
```

#### Output

```Json
5
```

## countMatches(text: Null, pattern: Any): Null

Helper function that enables `countMatches` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._
---

# dasherize

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## dasherize(text: String): String

Replaces spaces, underscores, and camel-casing in a string with dashes (hyphens).

If no spaces, underscores, and camel-casing are present, the output will match the input.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The input string.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example replaces the spaces, underscores, and camel-casing in the input. Notice that the input
"customer" is not modified in the output.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
{
  "a" : dasherize("customer"),
  "b" : dasherize("customer_first_name"),
  "c" : dasherize("customer NAME"),
  "d" : dasherize("customerName")
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
  "a": "customer",
  "b": "customer-first-name",
  "c": "customer-name",
  "d": "customer-name"
}
```

## dasherize(text: Null): Null

Helper function that enables `dasherize` to work with a `null` value.
---

# everyCharacter

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## everyCharacter(text: String, condition: (character: String) -> Boolean): Boolean

Checks whether a condition is valid for _every_ character in a string.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The string to check.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>condition</code></p></td><td><p>Expression that iterates through the characters in the string that it checks and returns a Boolean value.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example determines whether a string is composed of only digits and spaces.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
"12 34  56" everyCharacter $ == " " or isNumeric($)
```

#### Output

```Json
true
```

## everyCharacter(text: Null, condition: (character: Nothing) -> Any): true

Helper function that enables `everyCharacter` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._
---

# first

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## first(text: String, amount: Number): String

Returns characters from the beginning of a string to the specified number of characters in the
string, for example, the first two characters of a string.

If the number is equal to or greater than the number of characters in the string, the function
returns the entire string.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The string to process.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>amount</code></p></td><td><p>The number of characters to return. Negative numbers and <code>0</code> return an empty string. Decimals are rounded down to the nearest whole number.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example returns the first five characters from a string.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import first from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
"hello world!" first 5
```

#### Output

```Json
"hello"
```

## first(text: Null, amount: Any): Null

Helper function that enables `first` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._
---

# isAlpha

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## isAlpha(text: String): Boolean

Checks if the `text` contains only Unicode digits. This excludes digits, punctuation, and other
nonletter characters.

Note that the method does not allow for a leading sign, either positive or negative.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The input string.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `isAlpha` behaves with different inputs and sizes.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import isAlpha from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
{
  "a": isAlpha(null),
  "b": isAlpha(""),
  "c": isAlpha("  "),
  "d": isAlpha("abc"),
  "e": isAlpha("ab2c"),
  "f": isAlpha("ab-c")
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
  "a": false,
  "b": false,
  "c": false,
  "d": true,
  "e": false,
  "f": false
}
```

## isAlpha(text: Null): Boolean

Helper function that enables `isAlpha` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0._
---

# isNumeric

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## isNumeric(text: String): Boolean

Checks if the `text` contains only Unicode digits.

A decimal point is not a Unicode digit and returns false. Note that the method does not allow for a
leading sign, either positive or negative.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The input string.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `isNumeric` behaves with different inputs and sizes.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import isNumeric from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
{
  "a": isNumeric(null),
  "b": isNumeric(""),
  "c": isNumeric("  "),
  "d": isNumeric("123"),
  "e": isNumeric("à¥§à¥¨à¥©"),
  "f": isNumeric("12 3"),
  "g": isNumeric("ab2c"),
  "h": isNumeric("12-3"),
  "i": isNumeric("12.3"),
  "j": isNumeric("-123"),
  "k": isNumeric("+123")
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
  "a": false,
  "b": false,
  "c": false,
  "d": true,
  "e": true,
  "f": false,
  "g": false,
  "h": false,
  "i": false,
  "j": false,
  "k": false
}
```

## isNumeric(text: Null): Boolean

Helper function that enables `isNumeric` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0._
---

# leftPad

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## leftPad(text: String, size: Number, padText: String = " "): String

The specified `text` is _left_\-padded to the `size` using the `padText`. By default `padText` is
`" "`.

Returns left-padded `String` or original `String` if no padding is necessary.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The input string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>size</code></p></td><td><p>The size to pad to.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>padText</code></p></td><td><p>The text to pad with. It defaults to one space if not specified.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `leftPad` behaves with different inputs and sizes.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
{
   "a": leftPad(null, 3),
   "b": leftPad("", 3),
   "c": leftPad("bat", 5),
   "d": leftPad("bat", 3),
   "e": leftPad("bat", -1)
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
  "a": null,
  "b": "   ",
  "c": "  bat",
  "d": "bat",
  "e": "bat"
}
```

## leftPad(text: Null, size: Any, padText: Any = " "): Null

Helper function that enables `leftPad` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0._
---

# lines

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## lines(text: String): Array<String>

Returns an array of lines from a string.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The string to split into lines.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example divides a string into lines. An `\n` represents a line break.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import lines from dw::core::Strings
output application/json

lines("hello world\n\nhere   data-weave")
```

#### Output

```Json
["hello world", "", "here   data-weave"]
```

# mapString

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## mapString(@StreamCapable text: String, mapper: (character: String, index: Number) -> String): String

Applies an expression to every character of a string.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The string to map.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>mapper</code></p></td><td><p>Expression that applies to each character (<code>$</code>) or index (<code>$$</code>) of the <code>text</code> string and returns a string.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example redacts sensitive data from a string.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
{ balance: ("\$234" mapString if (isNumeric($)) "~" else $) }
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "balance": "$~~~"
}
```

## mapString(@StreamCapable text: Null, mapper: (character: Nothing, index: Nothing) -> Any): Null

Helper function that enables `mapString` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._
---

# ordinalize

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## ordinalize(num: Number): String

Returns a number as an ordinal, such as `1st` or `2nd`.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>num</code></p></td><td><p>An input number to return as an ordinal.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example returns a variety of input numbers as ordinals.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
{
  "a" : ordinalize(1),
  "b": ordinalize(2),
  "c": ordinalize(5),
  "d": ordinalize(103)
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
   "a": "1st",
   "b": "2nd",
   "c": "5th",
   "d": "103rd"
}
```

## ordinalize(num: Null): Null

Helper function that enables `ordinalize` to work with a `null` value.
---

# pluralize

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## pluralize(text: String): String

Pluralizes a singular string.

If the input is already plural (for example, "boxes"), the output will match the input.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The string to pluralize.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example pluralizes the input string "box" to return "boxes".

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
 { "pluralize" : pluralize("box") }
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "pluralize" : "boxes" }
```

## pluralize(text: Null): Null

Helper function that enables `pluralize` to work with a `null` value.
---

# prependIfMissing

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## prependIfMissing(text: String, prefix: String): String

Prepends the `prefix` to the beginning of the string if the `text` does not already start with that
prefix.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The input string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>prefix</code></p></td><td><p>The text to use as prefix.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `prependIfMissing` behaves with different inputs and sizes.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import prependIfMissing from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
{
  "a": prependIfMissing(null, ""),
  "b": prependIfMissing("abc", ""),
  "c": prependIfMissing("", "xyz"),
  "d": prependIfMissing("abc", "xyz"),
  "e": prependIfMissing("xyzabc", "xyz")
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
  "a": null,
  "b": "abc",
  "c": "xyz",
  "d": "xyzabc",
  "e": "xyzabc"
}
```

## prependIfMissing(text: Null, prefix: String): Null

Helper function that enables `prependIfMissing` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0._
---

# remove

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## remove(text: String, toRemove: String): String

Removes all occurrences of a specified pattern from a string.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The text to remove from.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>toRemove</code></p></td><td><p>The pattern to remove.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how the `remove` can be used to remove some unwanted properties.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import remove from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
"lazyness purity state higher-order stateful" remove "state"
```

#### Output

```Json
"lazyness purity  higher-order ful"
```

## remove(text: Null, toRemove: Any): Null

Helper function that enables `remove` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._
---

# repeat

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## repeat(text: String, times: Number): String

Repeats a `text` the number of specified `times`.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The input string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>times</code></p></td><td><p>Number of times to repeat char. Negative is treated as zero.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `repeat` behaves with different inputs and sizes.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
{
  "a": repeat("e", 0),
  "b": repeat("e", 3),
  "c": repeat("e", -2)
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
  "a": "",
  "b": "eee",
  "c": ""
}
```

## repeat(text: Null, times: Any): Null

Helper function that enables `repeat` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._
---

# replaceAll

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## replaceAll(text: String, target: String, replacement: String): String

Replaces all substrings that match a literal search string with a specified replacement string.

Replacement proceeds from the beginning of the string to the end. For example, the result of
replacing `"aa"` with `"b"` in the string\` `"aaa"` is `"ba"`, rather than `"ab"`.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The string to search.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>target</code></p></td><td><p>The string to find and replace in <code>text</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>replacement</code></p></td><td><p>The replacement string.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `replaceAll` behaves with different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
import * from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
{
    a: replaceAll("Mariano", "a" , "A"),
    b: replaceAll("AAAA", "AAA" , "B"),
    c: replaceAll(null, "aria" , "A"),
    d: replaceAll("Mariano", "j" , "Test"),
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
   "a": "MAriAno",
   "b": "BA",
   "c": null,
   "d": "Mariano"
 }
```

## replaceAll(text: Null, oldValue: String, newValue: String): Null

Helper function that enables `replaceAll` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._
---

# reverse

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## reverse(text: String): String

Reverses sequence of characters in a string.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The string to reverse.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `reverse` behaves with different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
 {
     a: reverse("Mariano"),
     b: reverse(null),
     c: reverse("")
 }
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "a": "onairaM",
  "b": null,
  "c": ""
}
```

## reverse(text: Null): Null

Helper function that enables `reverse` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._
---

# rightPad

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## rightPad(text: String, size: Number, padChar: String = " "): String

The specified `text` is _right_\-padded to the `size` using the `padText`. By default `padText` is
`" "`.

Returns right padded `String` or original `String` if no padding is necessary.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The input string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>size</code></p></td><td><p>The size to pad to.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>padText</code></p></td><td><p>The text to pad with. It defaults to one space if not specified.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `rightPad` behaves with different inputs and sizes.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
{
  "a": rightPad(null, 3),
  "b": rightPad("", 3),
  "c": rightPad("bat", 5),
  "d": rightPad("bat", 3),
  "e": rightPad("bat", -1)
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
  "a": null,
  "b": "   ",
  "c": "bat  ",
  "d": "bat",
  "e": "bat"
}
```

## rightPad(text: Null, size: Any, padText: Any = " "): Null

Helper function that enables `rightPad` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0._
---

# singularize

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## singularize(text: String): String

Converts a plural string to its singular form.

If the input is already singular (for example, "box"), the output will match the input.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The string to convert to singular form.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

This example converts the input string "boxes" to return "box".

#### Source

```DataWeave

output application/json
---
{ "singularize" : singularize("boxes") }
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "singularize" : "box" }
```

# someCharacter

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## someCharacter(text: String, condition: (character: String) -> Boolean): Boolean

Checks whether a condition is valid for at least one of the characters or blank spaces in a string.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The string to check.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>condition</code></p></td><td><p>Expression that iterates through the characters and spaces in the string and returns a Boolean value.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example determines whether a string has any uppercase characters.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
"someCharacter" someCharacter isUpperCase($)
```

#### Output

```Json
true
```

## someCharacter(text: Null, condition: (character: Nothing) -> Any): false

Helper function that enables `someCharacter` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._
---

# substring

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## substring(text: String, from: Number, until: Number): String

Returns a substring that spans from the character at the specified `from` index to the last
character before the `until` index.

The characters in the substring satisfy the condition `from <= indexOf(string) < until`.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The string, treated as an array of characters.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>from</code></p></td><td><p>The lowest index to include from the character array.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>until</code></p></td><td><p>The lowest index to exclude from the character array.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example returns the substring with characters at indices `1` through `4` in the input string.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
var text = "hello world!"
---
substring(text, 1, 5)
```

#### Output

```json
"ello"
```

## substring(text: Null, from: Any, until: Any): Null

Helper function that enables `substring` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._
---

# substringAfter

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## substringAfter(text: String, separator: String): String

Gets the substring after the first occurrence of a separator. The separator is not returned.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The input string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>separator</code></p></td><td><p>String to search for.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `substringAfter` behaves with different inputs and sizes.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
{
  "a": substringAfter(null, "'"),
  "b": substringAfter("", "-"),
  "c": substringAfter("abc", "b"),
  "d": substringAfter("abcba", "b"),
  "e": substringAfter("abc", "d"),
  "f": substringAfter("abc", "")
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{

  "a": null,
  "b": "",
  "c": "c",
  "d": "cba",
  "e": "",
  "f": "abc"
}
```

## substringAfter(text: Null, separator: String): Null

Helper function that enables `substringAfter` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0._
---

# substringAfterLast

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## substringAfterLast(text: String, separator: String): String

Gets the substring after the last occurrence of a separator. The separator is not returned.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The input string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>separator</code></p></td><td><p>String to search for.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `substringAfterLast` behaves with different inputs and sizes.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
{
  "a": substringAfterLast(null, "'"),
  "b": substringAfterLast("", "-"),
  "c": substringAfterLast("abc", "b"),
  "d": substringAfterLast("abcba", "b"),
  "e": substringAfterLast("abc", "d"),
  "f": substringAfterLast("abc", "")
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
 "a": null,
 "b": "",
 "c": "c",
 "d": "a",
 "e": "",
 "f": null
}
```

## substringAfterLast(text: Null, separator: String): Null

Helper function that enables `substringAfterLast` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0._
---

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The input string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>separator</code></p></td><td><p>String to search for.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>
