# substringBy

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## substringBy(text: String, predicate: (character: String, index: Number) -> Boolean): Array<String>

Splits a string at each character where the `predicate` expression returns `true`.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The string to split. The string is treated as an array of characters.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>predicate</code></p></td><td><p>Expression that tests each character and returns a Boolean value. The expression can iterate over each character and index of the string.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example splits a string where any of the specified characters (`"~"`, `"="`, or `"_"`) are
present.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import substringBy from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
"hello~world=here_data-weave" substringBy $ == "~" or $ == "=" or $ == "_"
```

#### Output

```Json
["hello", "world", "here", "data-weave"]
```

## substringBy(text: Null, predicate: (character: Nothing, index: Nothing) -> Any): Null

Helper function that enables `substringBy` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._
---

# substringEvery

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## substringEvery(text: String, amount: Number): Array<String>

Splits a string into an array of substrings equal to a specified length.

The last substring can be shorter than that length. If the length is greater than or equal to the
length of the string to split, the function returns the entire string.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The string to split.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>amount</code></p></td><td><p>The desired length of each substring.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `substringEvery` behaves when splitting an input string. The last returned
substring is shorter than the others.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import substringEvery from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
substringEvery("substringEvery", 3)
```

#### Output

```Json
["sub", "str", "ing", "Eve", "ry"]
```

## substringEvery(text: Null, amount: Any): Null

Helper function that enables `substringEvery` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._
---

# underscore

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## underscore(text: String): String

Replaces hyphens, spaces, and camel-casing in a string with underscores.

If no hyphens, spaces, and camel-casing are present, the output will match the input.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The input string.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example replaces the hyphens and spaces in the input. Notice that the input "customer" is not
modified in the output.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
{
   "a" : underscore("customer"),
   "b" : underscore("customer-first-name"),
   "c" : underscore("customer NAME"),
   "d" : underscore("customerName")
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
   "a": "customer",
   "b": "customer_first_name",
   "c": "customer_name",
   "d": "customer_name"
}
```

## underscore(text: Null): Null

Helper function that enables `underscore` to work with a `null` value.
---

# unwrap

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## unwrap(text: String, wrapper: String): String

Unwraps a given `text` from a `wrapper` text.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The input string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>wrapper</code></p></td><td><p>The text used to unwrap.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `unwrap` behaves with different inputs and sizes.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import unwrap from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
{
  "a": unwrap(null, ""),
  "b": unwrap(null, '\0'),
  "c": unwrap("'abc'", "'"),
  "d": unwrap("AABabcBAA", 'A'),
  "e": unwrap("A", '#'),
  "f": unwrap("#A", '#'),
  "g": unwrap("A#", '#')
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
   "a": null,
   "b": null,
   "c": "abc",
   "d": "ABabcBA",
   "e": "A",
   "f": "#A",
   "g": "A#"
 }
```

## unwrap(text: Null, wrapper: String): Null

Helper function that enables `unwrap` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0._
---

# withMaxSize

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## withMaxSize(text: String, maxLength: Number): String

Checks that the string length isnât greater than the specified `maxLength`. If the string is longer,
the function returns a substring that starts at the beginning of the string and is `maxLength`
characters long.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.3.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The input string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>maxLength</code></p></td><td><p>The maximum length of the string.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `withMaxSize` behaves with different `maxLength` values for the same string.
Note that if `withMaxSize` is 0, the function returns an unmodified string. If the input is `null`,
the output is always `null`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import withMaxSize from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
{
   a: "123" withMaxSize 10,
   b: "123" withMaxSize 3,
   c: "123" withMaxSize 2,
   d: "123" withMaxSize 0,
   e: null withMaxSize 23,
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
  "a": "123",
  "b": "123",
  "c": "12",
  "d": "123",
  "e": null
}
```

## withMaxSize(text: Null, maxLength: Number): Null

Helper function that enables `withMaxSize` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.3.0._
---

# words

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## words(text: String): Array<String>

Returns an array of words from a string.

Separators between words include blank spaces, new lines, and tabs.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The string to split into words.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example divides a string by the `words` it contains. An `\n` represents a line break, and `\t`
represents a tab.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import words from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
words("hello world\nhere\t\t\tdata-weave")
```

#### Output

```Json
["hello", "world", "here", "data-weave"]
```

## words(text: Null): Null

Helper function that enables `words` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._
---

# wrapIfMissing

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## wrapIfMissing(text: String, wrapper: String): String

Wraps `text` with `wrapper` if that `wrapper` is missing from the start or end of the given string.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The input string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>wrapper</code></p></td><td><p>The content used to wrap.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `wrapIfMissing` behaves with different inputs and sizes.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
 {
   "a": wrapIfMissing(null, "'"),
   "b": wrapIfMissing("", "'"),
   "c": wrapIfMissing("ab", "x"),
   "d": wrapIfMissing("'ab'", "'"),
   "e": wrapIfMissing("/", '/'),
   "f": wrapIfMissing("a/b/c", '/'),
   "g": wrapIfMissing("/a/b/c", '/'),
   "h": wrapIfMissing("a/b/c/", '/')
 }
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
   "a": null,
   "b": "'",
   "c": "xabx",
   "d": "'ab'",
   "e": "/",
   "f": "/a/b/c/",
   "g": "/a/b/c/",
   "h": "/a/b/c/"
 }
```

## wrapIfMissing(text: Null, wrapper: String): Null

Helper function that enables `wrapIfMissing` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0._
---

# wrapWith

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## wrapWith(text: String, wrapper: String): String

Wraps the specified `text` with the given `wrapper`.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The input string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>wrapper</code></p></td><td><p>The content used to wrap.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `wrapWith` behaves with different inputs and sizes.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Strings
output application/json
---
{
  "a": wrapWith(null, "'"),
  "b": wrapWith("", "'"),
  "c": wrapWith("ab", "x"),
  "d": wrapWith("'ab'", "'"),
  "e": wrapWith("ab", "'")
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
  "a": null,
  "b": "''",
  "c": "xabx",
  "d": "''ab''",
  "e": "'ab'"
}
```

## wrapWith(text: Null, wrapper: Any): Null

Helper function that enables `wrapWith` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.0._
---

# Strings (dw::core::Strings)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

This module contains helper functions for working with strings.

To use this module, you must import it to your DataWeave code, for example, by adding the line
`import * from dw::core::Strings` to the header of your DataWeave script.

## Functions

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-appendifmissing">appendIfMissing</a></p></td><td><p>Appends the <code>suffix</code> to the end of the <code>text</code> if the <code>text</code> does not already ends with the <code>suffix</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-camelize">camelize</a></p></td><td><p>Returns a string in camel case based on underscores in the string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-capitalize">capitalize</a></p></td><td><p>Capitalizes the first letter of each word in a string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-charcode">charCode</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the Unicode for the first character in an input string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-charcodeat">charCodeAt</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the Unicode for a character at the specified index.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-collapse">collapse</a></p></td><td><p>Collapses the string into substrings of equal characters.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-countcharactersby">countCharactersBy</a></p></td><td><p>Counts the number of times an expression that iterates through each character in a string returns <code>true</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-countmatches">countMatches</a></p></td><td><p>Counts the number of matches in a string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-dasherize">dasherize</a></p></td><td><p>Replaces spaces, underscores, and camel-casing in a string with dashes (hyphens).</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-everycharacter">everyCharacter</a></p></td><td><p>Checks whether a condition is valid for <em>every</em> character in a string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-first">first</a></p></td><td><p>Returns characters from the beginning of a string to the specified number of characters in the string, for example, the first two characters of a string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-fromcharcode">fromCharCode</a></p></td><td><p>Returns a character that matches the specified Unicode.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-hammingdistance">hammingDistance</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the Hamming distance between two strings.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-isalpha">isAlpha</a></p></td><td><p>Checks if the <code>text</code> contains only Unicode digits. A decimal point is not a Unicode digit and returns <code>false</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-isalphanumeric">isAlphanumeric</a></p></td><td><p>Checks if the <code>text</code> contains only Unicode letters or digits.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-islowercase">isLowerCase</a></p></td><td><p>Checks if the <code>text</code> contains only lowercase characters.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-isnumeric">isNumeric</a></p></td><td><p>Checks if the <code>text</code> contains only Unicode digits.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-isuppercase">isUpperCase</a></p></td><td><p>Checks if the <code>text</code> contains only uppercase characters.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-iswhitespace">isWhitespace</a></p></td><td><p>Checks if the <code>text</code> contains only whitespace.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-last">last</a></p></td><td><p>Returns characters from the end of string to a specified number of characters, for example, the last two characters of a string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-leftpad">leftPad</a></p></td><td><p>The specified <code>text</code> is <em>left</em>-padded to the <code>size</code> using the <code>padText</code>. By default <code>padText</code> is <code>" "</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-levenshteindistance">levenshteinDistance</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the Levenshtein distance (or edit distance) between two strings.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-lines">lines</a></p></td><td><p>Returns an array of lines from a string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-mapstring">mapString</a></p></td><td><p>Applies an expression to every character of a string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-ordinalize">ordinalize</a></p></td><td><p>Returns a number as an ordinal, such as <code>1st</code> or <code>2nd</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-pluralize">pluralize</a></p></td><td><p>Pluralizes a singular string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-prependifmissing">prependIfMissing</a></p></td><td><p>Prepends the <code>prefix</code> to the beginning of the string if the <code>text</code> does not already start with that prefix.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-remove">remove</a></p></td><td><p>Removes all occurrences of a specified pattern from a string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-repeat">repeat</a></p></td><td><p>Repeats a <code>text</code> the number of specified <code>times</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-replaceall">replaceAll</a></p></td><td><p>Replaces all substrings that match a literal search string with a specified replacement string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-reverse">reverse</a></p></td><td><p>Reverses sequence of characters in a string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-rightpad">rightPad</a></p></td><td><p>The specified <code>text</code> is <em>right</em>-padded to the <code>size</code> using the <code>padText</code>. By default <code>padText</code> is <code>" "</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-singularize">singularize</a></p></td><td><p>Converts a plural string to its singular form.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-somecharacter">someCharacter</a></p></td><td><p>Checks whether a condition is valid for at least one of the characters or blank spaces in a string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-substring">substring</a></p></td><td><p>Returns a substring that spans from the character at the specified <code>from</code> index to the last character before the <code>until</code> index.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-substringafter">substringAfter</a></p></td><td><p>Gets the substring after the first occurrence of a separator. The separator is not returned.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-substringafterlast">substringAfterLast</a></p></td><td><p>Gets the substring after the last occurrence of a separator. The separator is not returned.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-substringbefore">substringBefore</a></p></td><td><p>Gets the substring before the first occurrence of a separator. The separator is not returned.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-substringbeforelast">substringBeforeLast</a></p></td><td><p>Gets the substring before the last occurrence of a separator. The separator is not returned.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-substringby">substringBy</a></p></td><td><p>Splits a string at each character where the <code>predicate</code> expression returns <code>true</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-substringevery">substringEvery</a></p></td><td><p>Splits a string into an array of substrings equal to a specified length.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-underscore">underscore</a></p></td><td><p>Replaces hyphens, spaces, and camel-casing in a string with underscores.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-unwrap">unwrap</a></p></td><td><p>Unwraps a given <code>text</code> from a <code>wrapper</code> text.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-withmaxsize">withMaxSize</a></p></td><td><p>Checks that the string length is no larger than the specified <code>maxLength</code>. If the stringâs length is larger than the <code>maxLength</code>, the function cuts characters from left to right, until the string length meets the length limit.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-words">words</a></p></td><td><p>Returns an array of words from a string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-wrapifmissing">wrapIfMissing</a></p></td><td><p>Wraps <code>text</code> with <code>wrapper</code> if that <code>wrapper</code> is missing from the start or end of the given string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-strings-functions-wrapwith">wrapWith</a></p></td><td><p>Wraps the specified <code>text</code> with the given <code>wrapper</code>.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# envVar

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## envVar(variableName: String): String | Null

Returns an environment variable with the specified name or `null` if the environment variable is not
defined.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>variableName</code></p></td><td><p>String that provides the name of the environment variable.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example returns a Mac command console (`SHELL`) path and returns `null` on `FAKE_ENV_VAR` (an
undefined environment variable). `SHELL` is one of the standard Mac environment variables. Also
notice that the `import` command enables you to call the function without prepending the module name
to it.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::System
output application/json
---
{
    "envVars" : [
       "real" : envVar("SHELL"),
       "fake" : envVar("FAKE_ENV_VAR")
    ]
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
"envVars": [
  {
    "real": "/bin/bash"
  },
  {
    "fake": null
  }
]
```
---

# envVars

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## envVars(): Dictionary<String>

Returns all the environment variables defined in the host system as an array of strings.

### Example

This example returns a Mac command console (`SHELL`) path. `SHELL` is one of the standard Mac
environment variables. To return all the environment variables, you can use `dw::System::envVars()`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import dw::System
output application/json
---
{ "envVars" : dw::System::envVars().SHELL }
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "envVars": "/bin/bash" }
```
---

# System (dw::System)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

This module contains functions that allow you to interact with the underlying system.

To use this module, you must import it to your DataWeave code, for example, by adding the line
`import * from dw::System` to the header of your DataWeave script.

## Functions

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><a href="dw-system-functions-envvar">envVar</a></p></td><td><p>Returns an environment variable with the specified name or <code>null</code> if the environment variable is not defined.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-system-functions-envvars">envVars</a></p></td><td><p>Returns all the environment variables defined in the host system as an array of strings.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# dw::test::Asserts

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

This module contains the set of core matchers to use in your tests.

## Example

This example validates if a payload is of type `Object`.

### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import dw::tests::Asserts
---
payload must beObject()
```

## Functions

### anyOf

#### anyOf(matchers: Array<Matcher<Any>>): Matcher<Any>

Validates that the value satisfies at least one of the given matchers.

##### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>matchers</code></p></td><td><p>The list of matchers to be tested.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

##### Example

This example shows how to assert that a value must be of type `Object` or `String`.

###### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import dw::tests::Asserts
---
"A Text" must anyOf(beObject(), beString())
```

### beArray

#### beArray(): Matcher

Validates that a given value is of type `Array`.

##### Example

This example shows how to validate that a value is of type `Array`.

###### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import dw::tests::Asserts
---
[1, 4, 7] must beArray()
```

### beBlank

#### beBlank(): Matcher<String | Null>

Validates that the `String` value is blank.

##### Example

Validates that the string is empty or has whitespaces.

###### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import dw::tests::Asserts
---
"  " must beBlank()
```

### beBoolean

#### beBoolean(): Matcher

Validates that a given value is of type `Boolean`.

##### Example

This example shows how to validate that a value is of type `Boolean`.

###### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import dw::tests::Asserts
---
true must beBoolean()
```

### beEmpty

#### beEmpty(): Matcher<String | Object | Array | Null>

Validates that the value (of the type `String`, `Object`, or `Array`) is empty.

##### Example

Validates that the array is of size `0`.

###### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import dw::tests::Asserts
---
[] must beEmpty()
```

### beGreaterThan

#### beGreaterThan(expected: Comparable, inclusive: Boolean = false): Matcher<Comparable>

Validates that the asserted `Comparable` value is greater than the given one.

The value can be equal when using the _inclusive_ argument.

##### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>expected</code></p></td><td><p>The number to compare to.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>inclusive</code></p></td><td><p>Specifies if the comparison is inclusive or not (Optional). False by default.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

##### Example

Expects a `Number` to be greater than `2`.

###### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import dw::tests::Asserts
---
3 must beGreaterThan(2)
```

##### Example

Expects a number to be greater or equal than `2`.

###### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import dw::tests::Asserts
---
3 must beGreaterThan(2, true)
```

### beLowerThan

#### beLowerThan(expected: Comparable, inclusive: Boolean = false): Matcher<Comparable>

Validates that the asserted `Comparable` value is less than the given value.

The value can be equal when using the _inclusive_ argument.

##### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>expected</code></p></td><td><p>The number to compare to.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>inclusive</code></p></td><td><p>Specifies if the comparison is inclusive or not (Optional). False by default.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

##### Example

Expects a `Number` to be less than `2`.

###### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import dw::tests::Asserts
---
1 must beLowerThan(2)
```

##### Example

Expects a `Number` to be less or equal than `2`.

###### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import dw::tests::Asserts
---
1 must beLowerThan(2, true)
```

### beOneOf

Validates that the value is contained in the given array.

##### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>expected</code></p></td><td><p>The array of expected elements.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### contain

#### contain(expected: String): Matcher<String>

Validates that the asserted string contains the given string.

##### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>expected</code></p></td><td><p>The text expected to be contained within the input string.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

##### Example

Expects the value to contain the `String` value "ex".

###### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import dw::tests::Asserts
---
"A Text" must contain("ex")
```

#### contain(expected: Any): Matcher<Array<Any>>

Validates that the asserted array contains the given value.

##### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>expected</code></p></td><td><p>The value expected to be in the array.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

##### Example

Expects the `Array` value to contain the `Number` value 1.

###### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import dw::tests::Asserts
---
[1, "A Text", true] must contain(1)
```

### eachItem

#### eachItem(matcher: Matcher<Any>): Matcher<Array<Any>>

Validates that each item of the array satisfies the given matcher.

##### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>matcher</code></p></td><td><p>The matcher to apply to all the elements.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

##### Example

Expects all the elements in the array to be a number.

###### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import dw::tests::Asserts
---
[1,2,3] must eachItem(beNumber())
```

### endWith

#### endWith(expected: String): Matcher<String>

Validates that the asserted `String` value ends with the given string.

##### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>expected</code></p></td><td><p>Suffix of the 'String' value.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

Expects the `String` value to end with "xt".

```DataWeave
---

```

### equalTo

#### equalTo(expected: Any, equalToConfig: { unordered?: Boolean } = {}): Matcher<Any>

Validates that a value is equal to another one.

##### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>expected</code></p></td><td><p>The expected value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>equalToConfig</code></p></td><td><p>Configuration of how to compare them.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

##### Example

This example shows how to assert that a value must be `equal` to `3`.

###### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import dw::tests::Asserts
---
(1 + 2) must equalTo(3)
```

### equalToResource

#### equalToResource(resourceName: String, contentType: String = "application/dw", readerProperties: Object = {}): Matcher<Any>

Validates that the given value is equal to the content of a resource file.

The resource file must belong to the classpath.

##### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>resourceName</code></p></td><td><p>The resource name.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>contentType</code></p></td><td><p>The content type of the resource (Optional).</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>readerProperties</code></p></td><td><p>An object with the configuration properties (Optional).</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

##### Example

Expects a value to be equal to the content of the resource `user.json`.

###### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import dw::tests::Asserts
---
{ name: "Lionel", lastName: "Messi" } must equalToResource("user.json", "application/json")
```

### must

#### must<T>(value: T, matchExpressions: Array<(value: T) â Matcher<T> | MatcherResult | Boolean>): MatcherResult

This function enables you to assert a value with with a list of matchers or expressions.

##### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>value</code></p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>matchExpressions</code></p></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

##### Example

This example shows how to assert that a `payload` is of type `Object` and has a property `foo` that
is `null`.

###### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import dw::tests::Asserts
---
payload must [
    beObject(),
    $.foo is Null
]
```

#### must<T>(value: T, matcher: (value: T) â Matcher<T> | Boolean): MatcherResult

This function enables you to assert a value with a matcher of expressions.

##### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>value</code></p></td><td><p>The value to assert.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>matcher</code></p></td><td><p>The matcher to use.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

##### Example

This example shows how to assert that a `payload` is of type `Object`.

###### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import dw::tests::Asserts
---
payload must beObject()
```

## Variables

### MATCHED

Constant that represents a successful match.

## Types

### Matcher

Data type that represents a matcher to perform assertions.

#### Example

##### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import dw::tests::Asserts

fun beEqualToOne(): Matcher<Any> =
    (actual) -> do {
        {
            matches: actual == 1,
            description: { expected: "To be 1", actual: write(actual) as String }
        }
    }
```

Definition

```DataWeave
(value: T) -> MatcherResult
```

### MatcherResult

Data Type that represents the result of an assertion.

#### Example

##### Source

```DataWeave
{
  "matches": false,
  description : { expected : "Number type", actual: "A Text" }
}
```

Definition

```DataWeave
{ matches: Boolean, description: { expected: String, actual: String }, reasons?: Array<String> }
```
---

# currentMilliseconds

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## currentMilliseconds(): Number

Returns the current time in milliseconds.

### Example

This example shows the time in milliseconds when the function executed.

#### Source

```Dataweave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Timer
output application/json
---
{ "currentMilliseconds" : currentMilliseconds() }
```

#### Output

```XML
{ "currentMilliseconds": 1532923168900 }
```
---

# duration

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## duration<T>(valueToMeasure: () -> T): DurationMeasurement<T>

Executes the input function and returns an object with execution time in milliseconds and result of
that function.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>valueToMeasure</code></p></td><td><p>A function to pass to <code>duration</code>.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example passes a `wait` function (defined in the header), which returns the execution time and
result of that function in a `DurationMeasurement` object.

#### Source

```Dataweave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
fun myFunction() = dw::Runtime::wait("My result",100)
---
dw::util::Timer::duration(() -> myFunction())
```

#### Output

```XML
{
  "time": 101,
  "result": "My result"
}
```
---

# time

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## time<T>(valueToMeasure: () -> T): TimeMeasurement<T>

Executes the input function and returns a `TimeMeasurement` object that contains the start and end
time for the execution of that function, as well the result of the function.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>valueToMeasure</code></p></td><td><p>A function to pass to <code>time</code>.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example passes `wait` and `sum` functions (defined in the header), which return their results
in `TimeMeasurement` objects.

```Dataweave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
fun myFunction() = dw::Runtime::wait("My result",100)
fun myFunction2() = sum([1,2,3,4])
---
{ testing: [
    dw::util::Timer::time(() -> myFunction()),
    dw::util::Timer::time(() -> myFunction2())
  ]
}
```

#### Output

```XML
{
  "testing": [
    {
      "start": "2018-10-05T19:23:01.49Z",
      "result": "My result",
      "end": "2018-10-05T19:23:01.591Z"
    },
    {
      "start": "2018-10-05T19:23:01.591Z",
      "result": 10,
      "end": "2018-10-05T19:23:01.591Z"
    }
  ]
}
```
---

# toMilliseconds

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## toMilliseconds(date: DateTime): Number

Returns the representation of a specified date-time in milliseconds.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>date</code></p></td><td><p>A <code>DateTime</code> to evaluate.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows a date-time in milliseconds.

#### Source

```Dataweave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Timer
output application/json
---
{ "toMilliseconds" : toMilliseconds(|2018-07-23T22:03:04.829Z|) }
```

#### Output

```XML
{ "toMilliseconds": 1532383384829 }
```

# Timer Types (dw::util::Timer)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Type</th><th>Definition</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>DurationMeasurement</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type DurationMeasurement = { time: Number, result: T }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A return type that contains the execution time and result of a function call.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>TimeMeasurement</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type TimeMeasurement = { start: DateTime, result: T, end: DateTime }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A return type that contains a start time, end time, and result of a function call.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

# Timer (dw::util::Timer)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

This utility module contains functions for measuring time.

To use this module, you must import it to your DataWeave code, for example, by adding the line
`import * from dw::util::Timer` to the header of your DataWeave script.

## Functions

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><a href="dw-timer-functions-currentmilliseconds">currentMilliseconds</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the current time in milliseconds.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-timer-functions-duration">duration</a></p></td><td><p>Executes the input function and returns an object with execution time in milliseconds and result of that function.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-timer-functions-time">time</a></p></td><td><p>Executes the input function and returns a <code>TimeMeasurement</code> object that contains the start and end time for the execution of that function, as well the result of the function.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-timer-functions-tomilliseconds">toMilliseconds</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the representation of a specified date-time in milliseconds.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Types

- [Timer Types](dw-timer-types)

# asExpressionString

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## asExpressionString(path: Path): String

Transforms a `Path` value into a string representation of the path.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.2._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>path</code></p></td><td><p>The <code>Path</code> value to transform into a <code>String</code> value.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example transforms a `Path` value into a `String` representation of a selector for an attribute
of an object.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Tree
output application/json
---
asExpressionString([
        {kind: OBJECT_TYPE, selector: "user", namespace: null},
        {kind: ATTRIBUTE_TYPE, selector: "name", namespace: null}
    ])
```

#### Output

```Json
".user.@name"
```
---

# filterArrayLeafs

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## filterArrayLeafs(value: Any, criteria: (value: Any, path: Path) -> Boolean): Any

Applies a filtering expression to leaf or `Path` values of an array.

The leaf values in the array must be `SimpleType` or `Null` values. See
[Core Types](https://docs.mulesoft.com/dataweave/latest/dw-core-types) for descriptions of the
types.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>value</code></p></td><td><p>An input value of <code>Any</code> type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>criteria</code></p></td><td><p>Boolean expression to apply to <code>SimpleType</code> or <code>Null</code> leaf values of all arrays in the input <code>value</code>. If the result is <code>true</code>, the array retains the leaf value. If not, the function removes the leaf value from the output.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `filterArrayLeafs` behaves with different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Tree
var myArray = [1, {name: ["", true], test: 213}, "123", null]
output application/json
---
{
   a: myArray filterArrayLeafs ((value, path) ->
        !(value is Null or value is String)),
   b:  myArray filterArrayLeafs ((value, path) ->
        (value is Null or value == 1)),
   c: { a : [1,2] } filterArrayLeafs ((value, path) ->
        (value is Null or value == 1)),
   d: myArray filterArrayLeafs ((value, path) ->
        !isArrayType(path))
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "a": [
    1,
    {
      "name": [
        true
      ],
      "test": 213
    }
  ],
  "b": [
    1,
    {
      "name": [

      ],
      "test": 213
    },
    null
  ],
  "c": {
    "a": [
     1
    ]
  },
  "d": [
    {
      "name": [

      ],
      "test": 213
    }
  ]
}
```
---

# filterObjectLeafs

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## filterObjectLeafs(value: Any, criteria: (value: Any, path: Path) -> Boolean): Any

Applies a filtering expression to leaf or `Path` values of keys in an object.

The leaf values in the object must be `SimpleType` or `Null` values. See
[Core Types](https://docs.mulesoft.com/dataweave/latest/dw-core-types) for descriptions of the
types.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>value</code></p></td><td><p>An input value of <code>Any</code> type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>criteria</code></p></td><td><p>Boolean expression to apply to <code>SimpleType</code> or <code>Null</code> leaf values of all objects in the input <code>value</code>. If the result is <code>true</code>, the object retains the leaf value and its key. If not, the function removes the leaf value and key from the output.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `filterObjectLeafs` behaves with different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Tree
var myArray = [{name @(mail: "me@me.com", test:123 ): "", id:"test"},
               {name: "Me", id:null}]
output application/json
---
{
 a: {
     name: "Mariano",
     lastName: null,
     age: 123,
     friends: myArray
    }  filterObjectLeafs ((value, path) ->
         !(value is Null or value is String)),
 b: { c : null, d : "hello" } filterObjectLeafs ((value, path) ->
         (value is Null and isObjectType(path)))
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "a": {
    "age": 123,
    "friends": [
      {

      },
      {

      }
    ]
  },
  "b": {
    "c": null
  }
}
```
---

# filterTree

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## filterTree(value: Any, criteria: (value: Any, path: Path) -> Boolean): Any

Filters the value or path of nodes in an input based on a specified `criteria`.

The function iterates through the nodes in the input. The `criteria` can apply to the value or path
in the input. If the `criteria` evaluates to `true`, the node remains in the output. If `false`, the
function filters out the node.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>value</code></p></td><td><p>The value to filter.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>criteria</code></p></td><td><p>The expression that determines whether to filter the node.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `filterTree` behaves with different inputs. The output is `application/dw`
for demonstration purposes.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Tree
output application/dw
---
{
    a: {
          name : "",
          lastName @(foo: ""): "Achaval",
          friends @(id: 123): [{id: "", test: true}, {age: 123}, ""]
        } filterTree ((value, path) ->
            value match  {
                            case s is String -> !isEmpty(s)
                            else -> true
                          }
    ),
    b: null filterTree ((value, path) -> value is String),
    c: [
            {name: "Mariano", friends: []},
            {test: [1,2,3]},
            {dw: ""}
        ] filterTree ((value, path) ->
            value match  {
                            case a is Array ->  !isEmpty(a as Array)
                            else -> true
                        })
}
```

#### Output

```DataWeave
{
  a: {
    lastName: "Achaval",
    friends @(id: 123): [
      {
        test: true
      },
      {
        age: 123
      }
    ]
  },
  b: null,
  c: [
    {
      name: "Mariano"
    },
    {
      test: [
        1,
        2,
        3
      ]
    },
    {
      dw: ""
    }
  ]
}
```
---

# isArrayType

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## isArrayType(path: Path): Boolean

Returns `true` if the provided `Path` value is an `ARRAY_TYPE` expression.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>path</code></p></td><td><p>The <code>Path</code> value to validate.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `isArrayType` behaves with different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Tree
output application/json
---
{
  a: isArrayType([{kind: OBJECT_TYPE, selector: "user", namespace: null},
                  {kind: ATTRIBUTE_TYPE, selector: "name", namespace: null}]),
  b: isArrayType([{kind: OBJECT_TYPE, selector: "user", namespace: null},
                  {kind: ARRAY_TYPE, selector: 0, namespace: null}]),
  c: isArrayType([{kind: ARRAY_TYPE, selector: 0, namespace: null}])
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "a": false,
  "b": true,
  "c": true
}
```
---

# isAttributeType

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## isAttributeType(path: Path): Boolean

Returns `true` if the provided `Path` value is an `ATTRIBUTE_TYPE` expression.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>path</code></p></td><td><p>The <code>Path</code> value to validate.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

#### Source

```DataWeave

output application/json
---
{
  a: isAttributeType([{kind: OBJECT_TYPE, selector: "user", namespace: null},
                      {kind: ATTRIBUTE_TYPE, selector: "name", namespace: null}]),
  b: isAttributeType([{kind: OBJECT_TYPE, selector: "user", namespace: null},
                      {kind: ARRAY_TYPE, selector: "name", namespace: null}]),
  c: isAttributeType([{kind: ATTRIBUTE_TYPE, selector: "name", namespace: null}])
```

#### Output

```Json
  "a": true,
```

---

# mapLeafValues

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## mapLeafValues(value: Any, callback: (value: Any, path: Path) -> Any): Any

Maps the terminal (leaf) nodes in the tree.

Leafs nodes cannot have an object or an array as a value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.2._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>value</code></p></td><td><p>The value to map.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>callback</code></p></td><td><p>The mapper function.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example transforms all the string values to upper case.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Tree
output application/json
---
 {
     user: [{
         name: "mariano",
         lastName: "achaval"
     }],
     group: "data-weave"
 } mapLeafValues (value, path) -> upper(value)
```

#### Output

```Json
{
   "user": [
     {
       "name": "MARIANO",
       "lastName": "ACHAVAL"
     }
   ],
   "group": "DATA-WEAVE"
 }
```

### Example

This example returns a new value for an object, array, or attribute.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
import * from dw::util::Tree
---
{
    name: "Mariano",
    test: [1,2,3]
} mapLeafValues ((value, path) -> if(isObjectType(path))
                                        "***"
                                  else if(isArrayType(path))
                                        "In an array"
                                  else "Is an attribute")
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "name": "***",
  "test": [
    "In an array",
    "In an array",
    "In an array"
  ]
}
```
---

# nodeExists

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## nodeExists(value: Any, callback: (value: Any, path: Path) -> Boolean): Boolean

Returns `true` if any node in a given tree validates against the specified criteria.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.2._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>value</code></p></td><td><p>The value to search.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>callback</code></p></td><td><p>The criteria to apply to the input <code>value</code>.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example checks for each user by name and last name. Notice that you can also reference a
`value` with `$` and the `path` with `$$`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Tree
var myObject =  {
     user: [{
         name: "mariano",
         lastName: "achaval",
         friends: [
             {
                 name: "julian"
             },
             {
                 name: "tom"
             }
         ]
     },
     {
         name: "leandro",
         lastName: "shokida",
         friends: [
             {
                 name: "peter"
             },
             {
                 name: "robert"
             }
         ]

     }
     ]
 }
output application/json
---
{
    mariano : myObject nodeExists ((value, path) -> path[-1].selector == "name" and value == "mariano"),
    julian : myObject nodeExists ((value, path) -> path[-1].selector == "name" and value == "julian"),
    tom : myObject nodeExists ($$[-1].selector == "name" and $ == "tom"),
    leandro : myObject nodeExists ($$[-1].selector == "name" and $ ==  "leandro"),
    peter : myObject nodeExists ($$[-1].selector == "name" and $ == "peter"),
    wrongField: myObject nodeExists ($$[-1].selector == "wrongField"),
    teo: myObject nodeExists ($$[-1].selector == "name" and $ == "teo")
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "mariano": true,
  "julian": true,
  "tom": true,
  "leandro": true,
  "peter": true,
  "wrongField": false,
  "teo": false
}
```
---

# Tree Types (dw::util::Tree)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Type</th><th>Definition</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Path</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Path = Array&lt;PathElement&gt;</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Type that consists of an array of <code>PathElement</code> values that identify the location of a node in a tree. An example is <code>[{kind: OBJECT_TYPE, selector: "user", namespace: null}, {kind: ATTRIBUTE_TYPE, selector: "name", namespace: null}] as Path</code>.</p></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.2.</em></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>PathElement</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type PathElement = {| kind: "Object" | "Attribute" | "Array", selector: String | Number, namespace: Namespace | Null |}</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Type that represents a selection of a node in a path. An example is <code>{kind: ARRAY_TYPE, selector: "name", namespace: null} as PathElement</code>.</p></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.2.</em></p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# Tree Variables (dw::util::Tree)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Variable</th><th>Definition</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>ARRAY_TYPE</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>ARRAY_TYPE</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Variable used to identify a <code>PathElement</code> value as an array.</p></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.2.</em></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>ATTRIBUTE_TYPE</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>ATTRIBUTE_TYPE</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Variable used to identify a <code>PathElement</code> value as an attribute.</p></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.2.</em></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>OBJECT_TYPE</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>OBJECT_TYPE</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Variable used to identify a <code>PathElement</code> value as an object.</p></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.2.</em></p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# Tree (dw::util::Tree)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

This utility module provides functions for handling values as tree-data structures.

To use this module, you must import it to your DataWeave code, for example, by adding the line
`import * from dw::util::Tree` to the header of your DataWeave script.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.2._

## Functions

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><a href="dw-tree-functions-asexpressionstring">asExpressionString</a></p></td><td><p>Transforms a <code>Path</code> value into a string representation of the path.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-tree-functions-filterarrayleafs">filterArrayLeafs</a></p></td><td><p>Applies a filtering expression to leaf or <code>Path</code> values of an array.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-tree-functions-filterobjectleafs">filterObjectLeafs</a></p></td><td><p>Applies a filtering expression to leaf or <code>Path</code> values of keys in an object.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-tree-functions-filtertree">filterTree</a></p></td><td><p>Filters the value or path of nodes in an input based on a specified <code>criteria</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-tree-functions-isarraytype">isArrayType</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if the provided <code>Path</code> value is an <code>ARRAY_TYPE</code> expression.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-tree-functions-isattributetype">isAttributeType</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if the provided <code>Path</code> value is an <code>ATTRIBUTE_TYPE</code> expression.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-tree-functions-isobjecttype">isObjectType</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if the provided <code>Path</code> value is an <code>OBJECT_TYPE</code> expression.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-tree-functions-mapleafvalues">mapLeafValues</a></p></td><td><p>Maps the terminal (leaf) nodes in the tree.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-tree-functions-nodeexists">nodeExists</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if any node in a given tree validates against the specified criteria.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Variables

- [Tree Variables](dw-tree-variables)

## Types

- [Tree Types](dw-tree-types)
---

# arrayItem

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## arrayItem(t: Type): Type

Returns the type of the given array. This function fails if the input is not an Array type.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.3.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>t</code></p></td><td><p>The type to check.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `arrayItem` behaves with different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Types
type ArrayOfString = Array<String>
type ArrayOfNumber = Array<Number>
type ArrayOfAny = Array<Any>
type ArrayOfAnyDefault = Array
output application/json
---
{
   a: arrayItem(ArrayOfString),
   b: arrayItem(ArrayOfNumber),
   c: arrayItem(ArrayOfAny),
   d: arrayItem(ArrayOfAnyDefault)
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "a": "String",
  "b": "Number",
  "c": "Any",
  "d": "Any"
}
```
---

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

```Json
  "a": "String"

```

# functionParamTypes

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## functionParamTypes(t: Type): Array<FunctionParam>

Returns the list of parameters from the given function type. This function fails if the provided
type is not a Function type.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.3.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>t</code></p></td><td><p>The function type.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `functionParamTypes` behaves with different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
import * from dw::core::Types
type AFunction = (String, Number) -> Number
type AFunction2 = () -> Number
---
{
    a: functionParamTypes(AFunction),
    b: functionParamTypes(AFunction2)
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
   "a": [
     {
       "paramType": "String",
       "optional": false
     },
     {
       "paramType": "Number",
       "optional": false
     }
   ],
   "b": [

   ]
 }
```
---

# intersectionItems

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## intersectionItems(t: Type): Array<Type>

Returns an array of all the types that define a given Intersection type. This function fails if the
input is not an Intersection type.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.3.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>t</code></p></td><td><p>The type to check.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `intersectionItems` behaves with different inputs. Note that the `AType`
variable defines an Intersection type `{name: String} & {age: Number}` by using an `&` between the
two objects.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Types
type AType = {name: String} & {age: Number}
output application/json
---
{
   a: intersectionItems(AType)
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "a": ["Object","Object"]
}
```
---

# isNamespaceType

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## isNamespaceType(t: Type): Boolean

Returns `true` if the input is the Namespace type.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.3.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>t</code></p></td><td><p>The type to check.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `isNamespaceType` behaves with different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Types
type ANamespace = Namespace
output application/json
---
{
   a: isNamespaceType(ANamespace),
   b: isNamespaceType(Namespace),
   c: isNamespaceType(String),
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "a": true,
  "b": true,
  "c": false
}
```
---

# isReferenceType

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## isReferenceType(t: Type): Boolean

Returns `true` if the input type is a Reference type.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.3.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>t</code></p></td><td><p>The type to check.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `isReferenceType` behaves with different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
import * from dw::core::Types
 type AArray = Array<String> {n: 1}
 type AArray2 = Array<AArray>
 ---
 {
     a: isReferenceType( AArray),
     b: isReferenceType(arrayItem(AArray2)),
     c: isReferenceType(String)
 }
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "a": false,
  "b": true,
  "c": false
}
```

# nameOf

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## nameOf(t: Type): String

Returns the name of the input type.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.3.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>t</code></p></td><td><p>The type to query</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json

---
 {
     a: nameOf(AArray),
     b: nameOf(AArray2),
     c: nameOf(String)
 }
```

#### Output

```Json
   "a": "AArray",
   "b": "AArray2",

 }
```

# objectFields

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## objectFields(t: Type): Array<Field>

Returns the array of fields from the given Object type. This function fails if the type is not an
Object type.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.3.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>t</code></p></td><td><p>The function type.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `objectFields` behaves with different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
import * from dw::core::Types
ns ns0 http://acme.com
type ADictionary = {_ : String}
type ASchema = {ns0#name @(ns0#foo: String): {}}
type AUser = {name @(foo?: String,l: Number)?: String, lastName*: Number}
---
{
    a: objectFields(ADictionary),
    b: objectFields(ASchema),
    c: objectFields(Object),
    d: objectFields(AUser)
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "a": [
    {
      "key": {
        "name": {
          "localName": "_",
          "namespace": null
        },
        "attributes": [

        ]
      },
      "required": true,
      "repeated": false,
      "value": "String"
    }
  ],
  "b": [
    {
      "key": {
        "name": {
          "localName": "name",
          "namespace": "http://acme.com"
        },
        "attributes": [
          {
            "name": {
              "localName": "foo",
              "namespace": "http://acme.com"
            },
            "value": "String",
            "required": true
          }
        ]
      },
      "required": true,
      "repeated": false,
      "value": "Object"
    }
  ],
  "c": [

  ],
  "d": [
    {
      "key": {
        "name": {
          "localName": "name",
          "namespace": null
        },
        "attributes": [
          {
            "name": {
              "localName": "foo",
              "namespace": null
            },
            "value": "String",
            "required": false
          },
          {
            "name": {
              "localName": "l",
              "namespace": null
            },
            "value": "Number",
            "required": true
          }
        ]
      },
      "required": false,
      "repeated": false,
      "value": "String"
    },
    {
      "key": {
        "name": {
          "localName": "lastName",
          "namespace": null
        },
        "attributes": [

        ]
      },
      "required": true,
      "repeated": true,
      "value": "Number"
    }
  ]
}
```
---

# unionItems

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## unionItems(t: Type): Array<Type>

Returns an array of all the types that define a given Union type. This function fails if the input
is not a Union type.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.3.0._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>t</code></p></td><td><p>The type to check.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows how `unionItems` behaves with different inputs.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::Types
type AType = String | Number
output application/json
---
{
   a: unionItems(AType)
}
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "a": ["String","Number"]
}
```
---

# Types Types (dw::core::Types)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Type</th><th>Definition</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Attribute</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Attribute = { name: QName, required: Boolean, value: Type }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Represents an Attribute definition that is part of an Object field Key.</p></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.3.0.</em></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Field</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type Field = { key: { name: QName, attributes: Array&lt;Attribute&gt; }, required: Boolean, repeated: Boolean, value: Type }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Represents a Field description that is part of an Object.</p></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.3.0.</em></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>FunctionParam</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type FunctionParam = { paramType: Type, optional: Boolean }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Represents a Function parameter that is part of a Function type.</p></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.3.0.</em></p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>QName</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type QName = { localName: String, namespace: Namespace | Null }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Represents a Qualified Name definition with a <code>localName</code> (a string) and a <code>namespace</code>. If the QName does not have a Namespace, its value is <code>null</code>.</p></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.3.0.</em></p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# Types (dw::core::Types)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

This module enables you to perform type introspection.

To use this module, you must import it to your DataWeave code, for example, by adding the line
`import * from dw::core::Types` to the header of your DataWeave script.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.3.0._

## Functions

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><a href="dw-types-functions-arrayitem">arrayItem</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the type of the given array. This function fails if the input is not an Array type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-types-functions-basetypeof">baseTypeOf</a></p></td><td><p>Returns an the base type of the given type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-types-functions-functionparamtypes">functionParamTypes</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the list of parameters from the given function type. This function fails if the provided type is not a Function type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-types-functions-functionreturntype">functionReturnType</a></p></td><td><p>Returns the type of a functionâs return type. This function fails if the input type is not a Function type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-types-functions-intersectionitems">intersectionItems</a></p></td><td><p>Returns an array of all the types that define a given Intersection type. This function fails if the input is not an Intersection type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-types-functions-isanytype">isAnyType</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if the input is the Any type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-types-functions-isarraytype">isArrayType</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if the input type is the Array type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-types-functions-isbinarytype">isBinaryType</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if the input is the Binary type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-types-functions-isbooleantype">isBooleanType</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if the input is the Boolean type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-types-functions-isdatetimetype">isDateTimeType</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if the input is the DateTime type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-types-functions-isdatetype">isDateType</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if the input is the Date type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-types-functions-isfunctiontype">isFunctionType</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if the input is the Function type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-types-functions-isintersectiontype">isIntersectionType</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if the input type is the Intersection type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-types-functions-iskeytype">isKeyType</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if the input is the Key type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-types-functions-isliteraltype">isLiteralType</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if the input is the Literal type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-types-functions-islocaldatetimetype">isLocalDateTimeType</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if the input is the LocalDateTime type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-types-functions-islocaltimetype">isLocalTimeType</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if the input is the LocalTime type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-types-functions-isnamespacetype">isNamespaceType</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if the input is the Namespace type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-types-functions-isnothingtype">isNothingType</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if the input is the Nothing type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-types-functions-isnulltype">isNullType</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if the input is the Null type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-types-functions-isnumbertype">isNumberType</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if the input is the Number type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-types-functions-isuritype">isUriType</a></p></td><td><p>Returns <code>true</code> if the input is the Uri type.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a the array of fields from the given Object type. This function fails if the input is not a Union type.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>
