# compose

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## compose(parts: Array<String>, interpolation: Array<String>): String

Uses a custom string interpolator to replace URL components with a `encodeURIComponent` result. You
can call this function using the standard call, or a simplified version.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>baseStringArray</code></p></td><td><p>A string array that contains the URL parts to interpolate using the strings in the <code>interpolationStringArray</code> parameter.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>interpolationStringArray</code></p></td><td><p>A string array that contains the strings used to interpolate the <code>baseStringArray</code>.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

The following example uses the compose function to form an encoded URL, the first parameter is an
array of two strings that are part of the URL and the second parameter is the `urlPath` variable
that is used to interpolate the strings in the first parameter. Notice that the spaces in the input
are encoded in the output URL as `%20`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
var urlPath = "content folder"
import * from dw::core::URL
---
 { "encodedURL" : compose(["http://examplewebsite.com/", "/page.html"], ["$(urlPath)"]) }
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "encodedURL" : "http://examplewebsite.com/content%20folder/page.html" }
```

### Example

You can also call this function using the simplified syntax, which uses backticks (`` ` ``) to
enclose the string that includes the variable to encode. This example shows how to use the
simplified syntax to obtain the same result as in the previous example.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
var urlPath = "content folder"
import * from dw::core::URL
---
{ "encodedURL" : compose `http://examplewebsite.com/$(urlPath)/page.html`}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{ "encodedURL" : "http://examplewebsite.com/content%20folder/page.html" }
```
---

# decodeURI

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## decodeURI(text: String): String

Decodes the escape sequences (such as `%20`) in a URI.

The function replaces each escape sequence in the encoded URI with the character that it represents,
but does not decode escape sequences that could not have been introduced by `encodeURI`. The
character `#` is not decoded from escape sequences.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The URI to decode.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example decodes a URI that contains the URL percent encoding `%20`, which is used for spaces.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::URL
output application/json
---
{
  "decodeURI" : decodeURI('http://asd/%20text%20to%20decode%20/text')
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
  "decodeURI": "http://asd/ text to decode /text"
}
```
---

# decodeURIComponent

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## decodeURIComponent(text: String): String

Decodes a Uniform Resource Identifier (URI) component previously created by `encodeURIComponent` or
a similar routine.

For an example, see `encodeURIComponent`.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>URI component string.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example decodes a variety of URI components.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::URL
output application/json
---
{
  "decodeURIComponent": {
    "decodeURIComponent" : decodeURIComponent("%20PATH/%20TO%20/DECODE%20"),
    "decodeURIComponent" : decodeURIComponent("%3B%2C%2F%3F%3A%40%26%3D"),
    "decodeURIComponent" : decodeURIComponent("%2D%5F%2E%21%7E%2A%27%28%29%24"),
  }
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
   decodeURIComponent: {
     decodeURIComponent: " PATH/ TO /DECODE ",
     decodeURIComponent: ";,/?:@&=",
    decodeURIComponent: "-_.!~*'()\$"
   }
}
```
---

# encodeURI

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## encodeURI(text: String): String

Encodes a URI with UTF-8 escape sequences.

Applies up to four escape sequences for characters composed of two "surrogate" characters. The
function assumes that the URI is a complete URI, so it does not encode reserved characters that have
special meaning.

The function _does not encode these characters_ with UTF-8 escape sequences:

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Type (not escaped)</th><th>Examples</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Reserved characters</p></td><td><p>; , / ? : @ &amp; = $</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Unescaped characters</p></td><td><p>alphabetic, decimal digits, - _ . ! ~ * ' ( )</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Number sign</p></td><td><p>#</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>The URI to encode.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example shows encodes spaces in one URL and lists some characters that do not get encoded in
the `not_encoded` string.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::URL
output application/json
---
{
    "encodeURI" : encodeURI("http://asd/ text to decode /%/\"\\/text"),
    "not_encoded": encodeURI("http://:;,/?:@&=\$_-_.!~*'()")
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
    "encodeURI": "http://asd/%20text%20to%20decode%20/%25/%22%5C/text",
    "not_encoded": "http://:;,/?:@&=$_-_.!~*'()"
}
```
---

# encodeURIComponent

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## encodeURIComponent(text: String): String

Escapes certain characters in a URI component using UTF-8 encoding.

There can be only four escape sequences for characters composed of two "surrogate" \* characters.
`encodeURIComponent` escapes all characters _except the following_: alphabetic, decimal digits,
`- _ . ! ~ * ' ( )`. Note that `encodeURIComponent` differs from `encodeURI` in that it encodes
reserved characters and the Number sign `#` of `encodeURI`:

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Type</th><th>Includes</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Reserved characters</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Unescaped characters</p></td><td><p>alphabetic, decimal digits, - _ . ! ~ * ' ( )</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Number sign</p></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>text</code></p></td><td><p>URI component string.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example encodes a variety of URI components.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::URL
output application/json
---
{
  "comparing_encode_functions_output" : {
  	"encodeURIComponent" : encodeURI(" PATH/ TO /ENCODE "),
  	"encodeURI" : encodeURI(" PATH/ TO /ENCODE "),
  	"encodeURIComponent_to_hex" : encodeURIComponent(";,/?:@&="),
  	"encodeURI_not_to_hex" : encodeURI(";,/?:@&="),
  	"encodeURIComponent_not_encoded" : encodeURIComponent("-_.!~*'()"),
  	"encodeURI_not_encoded" : encodeURI("-_.!~*'()")
  }
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
  "comparing_encode_functions_output": {
    "encodeURIComponent": "%20PATH/%20TO%20/ENCODE%20",
    "encodeURI": "%20PATH/%20TO%20/ENCODE%20",
    "encodeURIComponent_to_hex": "%3B%2C%2F%3F%3A%40%26%3D",
    "encodeURI_not_to_hex": ";,/?:@&=",
    "encodeURIComponent_not_encoded": "-_.!~*'()",
    "encodeURI_not_encoded": "-_.!~*'()"
  }
}
```
---

# parseURI

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## parseURI(uri: String): URI

Parses a URL and returns a `URI` object.

The `isValid: Boolean` property in the output `URI` object indicates whether the parsing process
succeeded. Every field in this object is optional, and a field will appear in the output only if it
was present in the URL input.

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>uri</code></p></td><td><p>The URI input.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example parses a URL.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::core::URL
output application/json
---
{
  'composition': parseURI('https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uniform_Resource_Identifier#footer')
}
```

#### Output

```JSON
{
  "composition": {
    "isValid": true,
    "raw": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uniform_Resource_Identifier#footer",
    "host": "en.wikipedia.org",
    "authority": "en.wikipedia.org",
    "fragment": "footer",
    "path": "/wiki/Uniform_Resource_Identifier",
    "scheme": "https",
    "isAbsolute": true,
    "isOpaque": false
  }
}
```
---

# URL Types (dw::core::URL)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Type</th><th>Definition</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>URI</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type URI = { isValid: Boolean, host?: String, authority?: String, fragment?: String, path?: String, port?: Number, query?: String, scheme?: String, user?: String, isAbsolute?: Boolean, isOpaque?: Boolean }</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Describes the URI type. For descriptions of the fields, see <a href="https://docs.mulesoft.com/dataweave/latest/dataweave-types#dw_type_url">URL Types (dw::core::URL)</a>.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# URL (dw::core::URL)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

This module contains helper functions for working with URIs.

To use this module, you must import it to your DataWeave code, for example, by adding the line
`import * from dw::core::URL` to the header of your DataWeave script.

## Functions

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><a href="dw-url-functions-compose">compose</a></p></td><td><p>Uses a custom string interpolator to replace URL components with a <code>encodeURIComponent</code> result. You can call this function using the standard call, or a simplified version.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-url-functions-decodeuri">decodeURI</a></p></td><td><p>Decodes the escape sequences (such as <code>%20</code>) in a URI.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-url-functions-decodeuricomponent">decodeURIComponent</a></p></td><td><p>Decodes a Uniform Resource Identifier (URI) component previously created by <code>encodeURIComponent</code> or a similar routine.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-url-functions-encodeuri">encodeURI</a></p></td><td><p>Encodes a URI with UTF-8 escape sequences.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-url-functions-encodeuricomponent">encodeURIComponent</a></p></td><td><p>Escapes certain characters in a URI component using UTF-8 encoding.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-url-functions-parseuri">parseURI</a></p></td><td><p>Parses a URL and returns a <code>URI</code> object.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Types

- [URL Types](dw-url-types)

# attr

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## attr(namespace: Namespace | Null = null, name: String): PathElement

This function creates a `PathElement` to use for selecting an XML attribute and populates the typeâs
`selector` field with the given string.

Some versions of the `update` and `mask` functions accept a `PathElement` as an argument.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.2._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>namespace</code></p></td><td><p>The namespace of the attribute to select. If not specified, a null value is set.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>name</code></p></td><td><p>The string that names the attribute to select.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example creates an attribute selector for a specified namespace (`ns0`) and sets the selectorâs
value to `"myAttr"`. In the output, also note that the value of the `"kind"` key is `"Attribute"`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
import * from dw::util::Values
ns ns0 http://acme.com/fo
---
attr(ns0 , "myAttr")
```

#### Output

```Json
{
   "kind": "Attribute",
   "namespace": "http://acme.com/foo",
   "selector": "myAttr"
 }
```
---

# field

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## field(namespace: Namespace | Null = null, name: String): PathElement

This function creates a `PathElement` data type to use for selecting an _object field_ and populates
the typeâs `selector` field with the given string.

Some versions of the `update` and `mask` functions accept a `PathElement` as an argument.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.2._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>namespace</code></p></td><td><p>The namespace of the field to select. If not specified, a null value is set.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>name</code></p></td><td><p>A string that names the attribute to select.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example creates an object field selector for a specified namespace (`ns0`) and sets the
selectorâs value to `"myFieldName"`. In the output, also note that the value of the `"kind"` key is
`"Object"`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
import * from dw::util::Values
ns ns0 http://acme.com/foo
---
field(ns0 , "myFieldName")
```

#### Output

```Json
{
   "kind": "Object",
   "namespace": "http://acme.com/foo",
   "selector": "myFieldName"
 }
```
---

# index

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## index(index: Number): PathElement

This function creates a `PathElement` data type to use for selecting an _array element_ and
populates the typeâs `selector` field with the specified index.

Some versions of the `update` and `mask` functions accept a `PathElement` as an argument.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.2._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>index</code></p></td><td><p>The index.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example creates an selector for a specified index. It sets the selectorâs value to `0`. In the
output, also note that the value of the `"kind"` key is `"Array"`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
output application/json
import * from dw::util::Values
ns ns0 http://acme.com/foo
---
index(0)
```

#### Output

```Json
{
   "kind": "Array",
   "namespace": null,
   "selector": 0
 }
```
---

# update

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## update(objectValue: Object, fieldName: String): UpdaterValueProvider<Object>

This `update` function updates a field in an object with the specified string value.

The function returns a new object with the specified field and value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.2._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>objectValue</code></p></td><td><p>The object to update.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>fieldName</code></p></td><td><p>A string that provides the name of the field.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example updates the `name` field in the object `{name: "Mariano"}` with the specified value.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Values
output application/json
---
{name: "Mariano"} update "name" with "Data Weave"
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "name": "Data Weave"
}
```

## update(objectValue: Object, fieldName: PathElement): UpdaterValueProvider<Object>

This `update` function updates an object field with the specified `PathElement` value.

The function returns a new object with the specified field and value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.2._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>objectValue</code></p></td><td><p>The object to update.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>fieldName</code></p></td><td><p>A <code>PathElement</code> that specifies the field name.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example updates the value of a `name` field in the object `{name: "Mariano"}`. It uses
`field("name")` to return the `PathElement` that it passes to `update`. It uses `with "Data Weave"`
to specify the value (`Data Weave`) of `name`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Values
output application/json
---
{name: "Mariano"} update field("name") with "Data Weave"
```

#### Output

```Json
{
  "name": "Data Weave"
}
```

## update(arrayValue: Array, indexToUpdate: Number): UpdaterValueProvider<Array>

Updates an array index with the specified value.

This `update` function returns a new array that changes the value of the specified index.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.2._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>objectValue</code></p></td><td><p>The array to update.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>indexToUpdate</code></p></td><td><p>The index of the array to update. The index must be a number.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example replaces the value `2` (the index is `1`) with `5` in the the input array `[1,2,3]`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Values
output application/json
---
[1,2,3] update 1 with 5
```

#### Output

```Json
[
   1,
   5,
   3
 ]
```

## update(arrayValue: Array, indexToUpdate: String): UpdaterValueProvider<Array>

This `update` function updates all objects within the specified array with the given string value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.2._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>objectValue</code></p></td><td><p>The array of objects to update.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>indexToUpdate</code></p></td><td><p>A string providing the name of the field to update.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example updates value of the `role` fields in the array of objects.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Values
output application/json
---
[{role: "a", name: "spiderman"}, {role: "b", name: "batman"}] update "role" with "Super Hero"
```

#### Output

```Json
[{
   "role": "Super Hero",
   "name": "spiderman"
 },
 {
   "role": "Super Hero",
   "name": "batman"
}]
```

## update(arrayValue: Array, indexToUpdate: PathElement): UpdaterValueProvider<Array>

This `update` function updates the specified index of an array with the given `PathElement` value.

The function returns a new array that contains given value at the specified index.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.2._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>objectValue</code></p></td><td><p>The array to update.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>indexToUpdate</code></p></td><td><p>The index of the array to update. The index must be specified as a <code>PathElement</code>.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example updates the value of an element in the input array. Notice that it uses `index(1)` to
return the index as a `PathElement`, which it passes to `update`. It replaces the value `2` at that
index with `5`.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Values
output application/json
---
[1,2,3] update index(1) with 5
```

#### Output

```Json
[
   1,
   5,
   3
 ]
```

## update(value: Array | Object | Null, path: Array<String | Number | PathElement>): UpdaterValueProvider<Array | Object | Null>

Updates the value at the specified path with the given value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.2._

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>objectValue</code></p></td><td><p>The value to update. Accepts an array, object, or null value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>path</code></p></td><td><p>The path to update. The path must be an array of strings, numbers, or `PathElement`s.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Example

This example updates the name of the user.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Values
output application/json
---
{user: {name: "Mariano"}} update ["user", field("name")] with "Data Weave"
```

#### Output

```Json
{
   "user": {
     "name": "Data Weave"
   }
 }
```

### Example

This example updates one of the recurring fields.

#### Source

```DataWeave
%dw 2.0
import * from dw::util::Values
output application/xml
---
payload update ["users", "user", "language"] with (if ($ == "English") "Gibberish" else $)
```

#### Input

```XML
<users>
  <user>
    <name>Phoebe</name>
    <language>French</language>
  </user>
  <user>
    <name>Joey</name>
    <language>English</language>
  </user>
</users>
```

#### Output

```XML
<users>
  <user>
    <name>Phoebe</name>
    <language>French</language>
  </user>
  <user>
    <name>Joey</name>
    <language>Gibberish</language>
  </user>
</users>
```

## update(value: Null, toUpdate: Number | String | PathElement): UpdaterValueProvider<Null>

Helper function that enables `update` to work with a `null` value.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.2._
---

# Values Types (dw::util::Values)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Type</th><th>Definition</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>UpdaterValueProvider</p></td><td><div><div><p><code>type UpdaterValueProvider = (newValueProvider: (oldValue: Any, index: Number) -&gt; Any) -&gt; ReturnType</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Type that represents the output type of the update function.</p></div><div><p><em>Introduced in DataWeave version 2.4.0.</em></p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# Values (dw::util::Values)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

This utility module simplifies changes to values.

To use this module, you must import it to your DataWeave code, for example, by adding the line
`import * from dw::util::Values` to the header of your DataWeave script.

_Introduced in DataWeave version 2.2.2._

## Functions

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><a href="dw-values-functions-attr">attr</a></p></td><td><p>This function creates a <code>PathElement</code> to use for selecting an XML attribute and populates the typeâs <code>selector</code> field with the given string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-values-functions-field">field</a></p></td><td><p>This function creates a <code>PathElement</code> data type to use for selecting an <em>object field</em> and populates the typeâs <code>selector</code> field with the given string.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-values-functions-index">index</a></p></td><td><p>This function creates a <code>PathElement</code> data type to use for selecting an <em>array element</em> and populates the typeâs <code>selector</code> field with the specified index.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-values-functions-mask">mask</a></p></td><td><p>This <code>mask</code> function replaces all <em>simple</em> elements that match the specified criteria.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><a href="dw-values-functions-update">update</a></p></td><td><p>This <code>update</code> function updates a field in an object with the specified string value.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Types

- [Values Types](dw-values-types)
---

# DataWeave Overview

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

This MuleSoft programming language simplifies data transformation tasks and serves as Mule runtimeâs
expression language for configuring components and connectors. Build powerful integration solutions
with intuitive scripting capabilities.

DataWeave enables you to build a simple solution for a common use case for integration developers:
read and parse data from one format, transform the data, and write it out as a different format. For
example, a DataWeave script can receive a CSV file as input and transform it into an array of
complex JSON objects, or receive an XML input and write the data out to a flat file format.
DataWeave enables developers to focus on the transformation logic instead of thinking about the
specifics of reading, parsing, and writing specific data formats in a performant way.

- To learn more about DataWeave, visit [the language guide](dataweave-language-guide).
- To look up reference information about DataWeave operators and functions, visit
  [the reference guide](dw-functions).
- To see sample scripts to solve common transformation scenarios, visit
  [DataWeave examples](dataweave-cookbook).
- To try DataWeave scripts interactively, visit
  [DataWeave Playground](https://dataweave.mulesoft.com/learn/).
- To get started with DataWeave for Mule runtime engine (Mule) version 4.4 and later, visit
  [the quickstart](dataweave-quickstart).

## Compatibility

This table specifies which version of DataWeave is bundled with each Mule runtime engine release:
